topics.title: Domino Incident Investigation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Domino Incident Investigation

# 🎯 DOMINO INCIDENT INVESTIGATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Domino Incident:** A cascading or chain-reaction accident in which a **primary event** (the initiating accident) at one installation triggers one or more **secondary events** at neighbouring units, installations, or process vessels, thereby amplifying the overall damage footprint, casualty count, and environmental release far beyond the original incident envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Domino Incident Investigation** is the systematic, multi-tier forensic procedure used to reconstruct the **causal chain**, identify the **escalation vectors** (heat radiation, blast overpressure, projectile fragments, toxic dispersion), quantify the **propagation probability**, and assign **root-cause and contributory-cause responsibility** across multiple installations within a Seveso/COMAH-type industrial complex.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a row of dominoes standing on a table. Knock the first one down (the **primary event** — say, a gas leak ignition) and you trigger a chain reaction. The second domino falls (a **secondary event** — a neighbouring LPG sphere rupturing from thermal radiation), the third falls (a chlorine cylinder releasing toxic gas), and so on. Each fall is *not independent* — it is **causally linked** to the previous one.

In a refinery or petrochemical cluster, the **installations are the dominoes**: storage tanks, reactors, pipe racks, compressor houses. The **escalation vectors are the forces** that topple the next domino — a flame impinging on a tank wall, a shock wave crossing the inter-unit gap, or a toxic cloud drifting across the plant boundary. The investigator's job is to walk backwards along that chain, photograph every fallen domino in order, and determine *why each one fell when it did*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Keywords:** Primary Event, Secondary Event, Escalation Vector, Escalation Probability, Threshold of Domino Effect, Credible Domino Scenario, Footprint Amplification Factor, Multi-Installation Causal Chain.

### 📌 Key Metrics & Standard Thresholds

The internationally accepted escalation thresholds (from the **EU SEVESO-III Directive / CoMAH 2015** and the **CCPS Guidelines**) are:

- **Heat Radiation Threshold:** $12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ on a target vessel for thermal escalation (pool fire / jet fire exposure).
- **Blast Overpressure Threshold:** **20 kPa** (≈ 3 psi) for damage to secondary installations (structural collapse, vessel displacement).
- **Missile/Projectile Threshold:** Any credible fragment with kinetic energy $> 1.5 \times 10^4 \text{ J}$ is treated as a domino vector.
- **Toxic Concentration Threshold:** **IDLH** (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) value of the released chemical at the target unit boundary.

### 🏗️ Why Domino Investigation is a Distinct Discipline

A conventional incident investigation (e.g., TapRooT, Why-Tree, 5-Whys) ends at the boundary of the *failed unit*. A **domino investigation continues across the fenceline** into neighbouring units. This demands:

1. **Multi-installation data acquisition** (DCS logs, CCTV, witness accounts from each affected plant).
2. **Cross-unit timeline synchronization** to the second (often using GPS-timestamped SCADA data).
3. **Quantitative escalation modelling** to prove that the secondary event was *credible*, not coincidental.
4. **Legal-regulatory accountability mapping** because multiple operators (and often multiple jurisdictions) share liability.

> [!TIP]
> The **1998 Esso Longford Gas Explosion (Australia)** and the **2005 Texas City Refinery (BP) blast** are textbook KTU-level case studies where domino-style secondary fires and toxic releases drastically widened the casualty envelope beyond the primary vessel failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Domino Investigation Framework (CCPS-Aligned)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) treats domino investigation as a **five-phase structured protocol**. Each phase has a definite input, process, and deliverable.

### Phase 1 — **Incident Detection & Preliminary Scoping**
- **Goal:** Establish the geographic and operational perimeter. Identify the primary unit, the suspected secondary units, and the credible escalation vectors.
- **Inputs:** Plant emergency logs, fire-water pump start records, gas-detection alarms, mutual-aid callouts.
- **Output:** A preliminary **Domino Perimeter Map** with a circle of influence of radius typically **1.5× the largest credible event footprint**.

### Phase 2 — **Evidence Preservation & Multi-Unit Data Acquisition**
- **Goal:** Lock down all physical, electronic, and testimonial evidence *across every involved installation* before it is disturbed.
- **Key actions:**
  * Tag and bag fragments (metallurgical fingerprinting).
  * Image-disk all DCS/PLC/SIS controllers (forensic bit-stream capture).
  * Secure CCTV NVRs within a 1 km radius.
  * Conduct structured witness interviews (PEACE model or cognitive interview).
  * Collect meteorological data (wind speed, direction, stability class) from the nearest met-station.

### Phase 3 — **Causal Chain Reconstruction (Timeline Forensics)**
- **Goal:** Build a *synchronized, second-by-second timeline* of events from primary through tertiary cascades.
- **Tool:** **Event Sequence Diagram (ESD)** — a multi-track Gantt-style chart with one track per affected installation.
- **Critical technique:** Use **GPS-timestamped SCADA logs** as the master clock; align CCTV, witness statements, and physical damage to this clock.

### Phase 4 — **Escalation Vector Quantification**
- **Goal:** For each credible secondary event, calculate the magnitude of the escalation vector and compare it to the SEVESO threshold.
- **Sub-tasks:**
  * Compute heat flux received by target vessel using point-source or solid-flame radiation models.
  * Compute blast overpressure using TNT-equivalence or TNO Multi-Energy methods.
  * Compute toxic dose received at the downwind installation using Gaussian/Pasquill-Gifford dispersion.

### Phase 5 — **Root Cause, Contributory Cause & Regulatory Reporting**
- **Goal:** Identify the management-system failures (Bow-Tie), assign responsibilities, and produce a report compliant with **KTU safety audit norms** and SEVESO reporting duties.
- **Deliverable:** Final **Multi-Installation Investigation Report** with Bow-Tie diagrams for both the primary and every secondary event.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Phase

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Phase 1 matters:** Without a clearly bounded perimeter, the investigator will drown in data. The 1.5× footprint rule is a pragmatic cut-off — beyond that, escalation probability drops below ~10⁻⁶/year.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Phase 3 matters:** The legal and insurance definition of a "domino event" requires *demonstrated causal linkage within a credible time-window* (usually < 60 minutes). Without a synchronized timeline, this linkage is non-provable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Phase 4 matters:** A "domino" claim cannot rest on spatial proximity alone. It must be **quantitatively proven** that the escalation vector *exceeded* the failure threshold of the target installation. This is where Probit models enter.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Equation / Value** | **Units** | **KTU Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_e$ | Probability of escalation | $P_e = 1 - \exp(-\lambda \cdot t)$ | dimensionless | Decay of escalation probability over time |
| $\lambda$ | Escalation frequency | tabulated per installation | yr⁻¹ | SEVESO Land-Use Planning |
| $Y$ | Probit variable | $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ | dimensionless | Damage probability calculation |
| $V$ | Escalation vector intensity | heat flux, overpressure, or dose | kW/m², kPa, ppm·min | Input to Probit |
| $P_r$ | Probability of response (damage) | $P_r = 50\% \text{ at } Y = 5$ | % | Convert Probit to % |
| $q$ | Heat flux at target | $q = \chi_r \cdot Q \cdot F_{12} / (4\pi r^2)$ | kW/m² | Pool/Jet fire escalation |
| $\chi_r$ | Radiative fraction | 0.15 – 0.40 (fuel dependent) | dimensionless | Flame radiation |
| $F_{12}$ | View factor | geometric, $0 \le F_{12} \le 1$ | dimensionless | Geometry of tank-to-tank |
| $r$ | Inter-unit distance | measured from plot plan | m | Site layout driver |
| $p_s$ | Side-on overpressure | from TNT/Multi-Energy | kPa | Blast escalation |
| $W_{TNT}$ | TNT equivalent mass | $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$ | kg | Explosion scaling |
| $\eta$ | Explosion efficiency | 0.01 – 0.10 (gas cloud) | dimensionless | TNO Multi-Energy |
| $I_d$ | Toxic dose | $I_d = \int C^n \, dt$ | ppmⁿ·min | Toxic escalation |
| $C_{IDLH}$ | IDLH threshold | chemical-specific | ppm | Domino threshold |
| $D_{th}$ | Damage threshold for domino | **12.5 kW/m²** or **20 kPa** or **IDLH** | mixed | SEVESO cut-off |
| $F_{amp}$ | Footprint amplification factor | $F_{amp} = A_{total} / A_{primary}$ | dimensionless | Severity metric |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Domino Investigation

1. **Land-Use Planning (LUP):** Domino distances dictate minimum separation between hazardous installations.
2. **Insurance Underwriting:** Re-insurers (Munich Re, Swiss Re) use domino probability to set premiums.
3. **Emergency Planning:** Mutual-aid agreements and offsite emergency planning zones (OEPZ) are sized using domino envelopes.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** SEVESO-III Art. 12 and Indian Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989) Schedule 9 mandate domino risk assessment for "specified installations".
5. **Corporate Liability:** Post-incident, the investigation report becomes the legal record for criminal/civil proceedings (e.g., Buncefield 2005 prosecution).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Escalation Probability Decay Model

**Problem Statement:** A neighbouring atmospheric storage tank containing gasoline is exposed to a pool fire in an adjacent dyke for a duration $t$ minutes. Given an initial escalation frequency $\lambda$ (per minute), derive the probability that escalation has *not yet occurred* by time $t$, and hence the probability that escalation *has* occurred.

### Step 1 — Define the Hazard Rate
We assume the escalation follows a **homogeneous Poisson process** (memoryless). The instantaneous probability of escalation in an infinitesimally small interval $dt$ is:

$$
dP = \lambda \, dt
$$

### Step 2 — Survival Probability Differential
Let $S(t)$ be the probability the secondary installation has *not* yet escalated by time $t$. The probability of *surviving* the next instant is:

$$
S(t+dt) = S(t) \cdot (1 - \lambda \, dt)
$$

### Step 3 — Form the Differential Equation
Subtract $S(t)$ from both sides and divide by $dt$:

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = -\lambda \, S(t)
$$

### Step 4 — Solve the First-Order ODE
This is a standard separable ODE. Integrating with the initial condition $S(0) = 1$:

$$
\int_{1}^{S(t)} \frac{dS}{S} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln S(t) = -\lambda t
$$

$$
S(t) = \exp(-\lambda t)
$$

### Step 5 — Escalation Probability
By the complement rule:

$$
P_e(t) = 1 - S(t) = 1 - \exp(-\lambda t)
$$

> **Interpretation:** As $t \to \infty$, $P_e \to 1$ (certainty of escalation given infinite exposure). As $\lambda t$ increases, the curve saturates. For typical hydrocarbon tank exposure, $\lambda \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ per minute, so within 30 minutes, $P_e \approx 4.5\%$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Flux at Target Installation (Point-Source Model)

**Problem Statement:** A circular pool fire of diameter $D$ burns gasoline in an adjacent dyke. Derive the heat flux received at the shell of a vertical storage tank located at distance $r$ from the pool centre.

### Step 1 — Heat Release Rate
The mass burning rate of gasoline is $\dot{m}'' \approx 0.055 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$ and heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 43.7 \text{ MJ/kg}$. For a pool of diameter $D$:

$$
Q = \dot{m}'' \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

### Step 2 — Radiative Power
Only a fraction $\chi_r$ (radiative fraction) of the total HRR is emitted as thermal radiation. For gasoline pool fires, $\chi_r \approx 0.2$:

$$
Q_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot Q
$$

### Step 3 — Geometric Spreading (Point-Source Assumption)
At large distances ($r \gg D$), the radiated power spreads over a sphere of surface area $4\pi r^2$. The unfocused radiative flux is:

$$
q_{unfoc} = \frac{Q_{rad}}{4\pi r^2}
$$

### Step 4 — View-Factor Correction
The receiving tank only intercepts a fraction $F_{12}$ (the **view factor**) of the emitted radiation. For two parallel coaxial cylinders, $F_{12}$ is computed from geometry (typically 0.05 – 0.30 for typical refinery layouts).

### Step 5 — Atmospheric Transmissivity
The humidity and CO₂ content of air absorb a fraction of the radiation. Using a simple transmissivity $\tau_a$:

$$
\tau_a = \log_{10}(r) \cdot k_c \quad \text{(Beer's law approximation)}
$$

where $k_c \approx 0.05$ – $0.1$ per decade of distance for typical conditions.

### Step 6 — Final Heat Flux Equation

$$
q = \frac{\chi_r \cdot Q \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau_a}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Substituting the gasoline numbers** ($D = 10$ m, $r = 30$ m, $F_{12} = 0.15$, $\tau_a = 0.75$):

$$
Q = 0.055 \times \frac{\pi (10)^2}{4} \times 43.7 \times 10^6 = 1.89 \times 10^8 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{rad} = 0.20 \times 1.89 \times 10^8 = 3.78 \times 10^7 \text{ W}
$$

$$
q = \frac{3.78 \times 10^7 \times 0.15 \times 0.75}{4\pi (30)^2} = 1.50 \times 10^3 \text{ W/m}^2 = 1.5 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

> **Result:** The target tank receives only 1.5 kW/m², which is **below the 12.5 kW/m² SEVESO domino threshold**. Hence, thermal escalation is *not credible* at 30 m distance for this configuration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probit Analysis for Escalation Damage Probability

**Problem Statement:** A BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion) of a propane vessel at 25 m distance impinges on a target tank. Estimate the probability of catastrophic damage.

### Step 1 — Compute Blast Overpressure
Using TNT-equivalence with $\eta = 0.05$ and propane mass $M = 10{,}000$ kg, $\Delta H_c = 46 \text{ MJ/kg}$:

$$
W_{TNT} = \frac{0.05 \times 10{,}000 \times 46 \times 10^6}{4.68 \times 10^6} = 4914.5 \text{ kg TNT}
$$

### Step 2 — Scaled Distance
$$
Z = \frac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{25}{(4914.5)^{1/3}} = \frac{25}{17.06} = 1.466 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

### Step 3 — Side-On Overpressure (TNT Correlation, Baker)
$$
\frac{p_s}{p_0} = \frac{808 \left[ 1 + \left( \frac{Z}{4.5} \right)^2 \right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{Z}{0.048} \right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{Z}{0.32} \right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{Z}{1.35} \right)^2}}
$$

Plugging in $Z = 1.466$:

$$
Z/4.5 = 0.326, \quad Z/0.048 = 30.54, \quad Z/0.32 = 4.58, \quad Z/1.35 = 1.086
$$

$$
\frac{p_s}{p_0} = \frac{808 \left[1 + 0.106\right]}{\sqrt{1 + 932.7} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 20.99} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 1.180}} = \frac{893.7}{30.59 \times 4.71 \times 1.476} = 4.20
$$

$$
p_s = 4.20 \times 101.325 = 425.6 \text{ kPa}
$$

### Step 4 — Probit Equation for Structural Damage
For "catastrophic damage to a vessel" (CCPS):

$$
Y = -18.96 + 3.27 \ln(p_s) = -18.96 + 3.27 \ln(425.6)
$$

$$
\ln(425.6) = 6.054
$$

$$
Y = -18.96 + 3.27 \times 6.054 = -18.96 + 19.80 = 0.84
$$

### Step 5 — Probability of Response
Using the standard Probit-to-Probability conversion:

$$
P_r = 50\% \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 50\% \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{0.84 - 5}{1.414}\right)\right]
$$

$$
\frac{0.84 - 5}{1.414} = -2.94
$$

$$
P_r = 50\% \times [1 + \text{erf}(-2.94)] = 50\% \times [1 - 0.9975] = 0.125\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Only a 0.125% probability of catastrophic damage — under the threshold of "credible" (typically 1% or 10% depending on jurisdiction). The 25 m separation is *just* sufficient.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Domino Threshold Reference

| **Vector Type** | **Damage Level** | **Probit Equation** | **Domino Threshold** | **Engineering Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat radiation | Catastrophic tank failure | $Y = 12.54 - 1.847 \ln(t) \cdot \ln(q)$ | $q = 12.5$ kW/m² for $t > 10$ min | SEVESO standard |
| Blast overpressure | Heavy damage to process building | $Y = -23.8 + 2.92 \ln(p_s)$ | $p_s = 20$ kPa | Structural collapse onset |
| Missile impact | Penetration of vessel wall | $Y = -10.6 + 1.65 \ln(E_k)$ | $E_k = 1.5 \times 10^4$ J | Wall penetration |
| Toxic dose | Lethality (LC₅₀) | $Y = a + b \ln(I_d)$ | $I_d = $ IDLH · $t$ | Evacuation trigger |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Domino Investigation Master Flowchart

The following Mermaid block renders the full five-phase domino investigation protocol with sub-decision points.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Incident Detected] --> phaseA1[Phase 1: Preliminary Scoping]
    phaseA1 --> idA1{Is Multi-Unit Involvement Suspected?}
    idA1 -- No --> convA1[Standard Single-Unit Investigation]
    idA1 -- Yes --> mapA1[Draw 1.5x Footprint Perimeter Map]
    mapA1 --> phaseA2[Phase 2: Evidence Preservation]
    phaseA2 --> evidA2[Lock DCS, CCTV, Fragments, Witness]
    evidA2 --> phaseA3[Phase 3: Timeline Reconstruction]
    phaseA3 --> syncA3[GPS-Synced Multi-Track Event Sequence Diagram]
    syncA3 --> idA3{Causal Linkage Within 60 min?}
    idA3 -- No --> coincA3[Coincidental — Not Domino]
    idA3 -- Yes --> phaseA4[Phase 4: Escalation Vector Quantification]
    phaseA4 --> vecA4[Compute Heat, Blast, Missile, Toxic]
    vecA4 --> compA4{Vector Exceeds Threshold?}
    compA4 -- No --> ruledA4[Domino Ruled Out]
    compA4 -- Yes --> phaseA5[Phase 5: RCA and Reporting]
    phaseA5 --> bowA5[Bow-Tie Analysis per Unit]
    bowA5 --> regA5[Regulatory Report - SEVESO / MSIHC]
    regA5 --> endA[Closed]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Domino Propagation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRIMARY[Primary Event Block]
        pUnit[Primary Unit e.g. Pump house]
        pCause[Cause e.g. Seal failure]
        pEvent[Primary Event e.g. Gas cloud explosion]
        pUnit --> pCause --> pEvent
    end

    subgraph VECTOR[Escalation Vector Block]
        vHeat[Heat Radiation]
        vBlast[Blast Overpressure]
        vMiss[Missile Fragment]
        vTox[Toxic Plume]
    end

    subgraph SECONDARY[Secondary Event Block]
        sUnit1[Tank 1]
        sUnit2[Reactor 2]
        sUnit3[Pipe Rack]
    end

    subgraph TERTIARY[Tertiary Event Block]
        tUnitA[Compressor House]
        tUnitB[Control Room]
    end

    pEvent --> vHeat
    pEvent --> vBlast
    pEvent --> vMiss
    pEvent --> vTox

    vHeat --> sUnit1
    vBlast --> sUnit2
    vMiss --> sUnit3
    vTox --> sUnit1

    sUnit1 --> tUnitA
    sUnit2 --> tUnitB
    sUnit3 --> tUnitA
```

## 4.3 Bow-Tie Diagram of a Generic Domino Event

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LHS[Left Side - Threats]
        t1[Primary Loss of Containment]
        t2[Heat Radiation on Target Tank]
        t3[Blast on Target Reactor]
    end

    subgraph CTR[Centre - Top Event]
        topE[TOP EVENT: Domino Cascade Initiated]
    end

    subgraph RHS[Right Side - Consequences]
        c1[Secondary Tank BLEVE]
        c2[Toxic Release]
        c3[Multi-Casualty Offsite Impact]
    end

    t1 --> topE
    t2 --> topE
    t3 --> topE

    topE --> c1
    topE --> c2
    topE --> c3

    subgraph P1[Preventive Controls]
        pGas[Gas Detection]
        pIso[Block Valve Isolation]
    end

    subgraph M1[Mitigative Controls]
        mDyke[Firewall / Dyke]
        mWat[Deluge Water Spray]
        mDistan[Inter-Unit Separation]
    end

    t1 -.blocked by.-> pGas
    t1 -.blocked by.-> pIso
    topE -.mitigated by.-> mDyke
    topE -.mitigated by.-> mWat
    topE -.mitigated by.-> mDistan
```

## 4.4 Investigation Reporting Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rep1[Site Investigation Team - Level 1]
    rep2[Corporate Safety Audit - Level 2]
    rep3[Independent Regulatory Body - Level 3]
    rep4[National Disaster Authority - Level 4]

    rep1 --> rep2
    rep2 --> rep3
    rep3 --> rep4

    rep1 --> out1[Site Level Report]
    rep2 --> out2[Corporate Lessons Learnt]
    rep3 --> out3[Public Inquiry Report]
    rep4 --> out4[National Safety Directive]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams are designed to render natively in GitHub, VS Code preview, and most modern Mermaid renderers. Node IDs are intentionally alphanumeric to avoid Mermaid parser conflicts.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a "domino incident" with a suitable example from the process industry. State the internationally accepted escalation thresholds for the four principal escalation vectors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A **domino incident** is a cascading accident in which a primary event at one installation triggers secondary events at neighbouring units via escalation vectors, amplifying the casualty and damage envelope (1 mark). *Example:* A pump-house gas-cloud explosion (primary) in a refinery triggers a BLEVE of a neighbouring LPG sphere (secondary) via thermal radiation, which in turn causes a toxic release from a chlorine cylinder (tertiary) (1 mark). The SEVESO thresholds are: heat radiation $\ge 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$, blast overpressure $\ge 20 \text{ kPa}$, missile kinetic energy $\ge 1.5 \times 10^4 \text{ J}$, and toxic dose $\ge$ IDLH (1 mark).

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]
**List the five phases of a domino incident investigation as per the CCPS framework and briefly explain the objective of Phase 3.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The five phases are (i) Preliminary Scoping, (ii) Evidence Preservation, (iii) Causal Chain Reconstruction, (iv) Escalation Vector Quantification, and (v) Root Cause and Reporting (2 marks). **Phase 3 (Causal Chain Reconstruction)** aims to build a *GPS-synchronised, second-by-second timeline* across all involved installations using SCADA logs as the master clock, in order to prove temporal causal linkage within the 60-minute "credible domino window" (1 mark).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **five-phase domino investigation protocol**. For each phase, state the primary deliverable and the key technical input required.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vertical gasoline storage tank of diameter 8 m is located 25 m from a circular pool fire of diameter 12 m in an adjacent dyke. Using the point-source heat-flux model, compute the heat flux received by the target tank shell and state whether thermal domino escalation is credible. Assume: $\dot{m}'' = 0.055 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$, $\Delta H_c = 43.7 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $\chi_r = 0.20$, $F_{12} = 0.20$, $\tau_a = 0.78$, $p_0 = 101.3 \text{ kPa}$.

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Five-Phase Protocol
| Phase | Primary Deliverable | Key Technical Input |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Preliminary Scoping | Domino Perimeter Map (1.5× footprint) | Plant plot plan, emergency logs |
| 2. Evidence Preservation | Forensic evidence locker (per unit) | DCS, CCTV, fragments, witness |
| 3. Timeline Reconstruction | Multi-Track Event Sequence Diagram | GPS-timestamped SCADA |
| 4. Vector Quantification | Probit probability for each secondary unit | Heat-flux, overpressure, dose |
| 5. RCA and Reporting | Multi-Unit Bow-Tie and final report | Audit trail, regulatory mandates |

*(1 mark per row + 1 mark for overall coherence & sequence of explanation; total 5+1.5+0.5 = 7)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Heat-Flux Calculation

**Step 1 (1 mark):** HRR of the pool fire
$$
Q = \dot{m}'' \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \Delta H_c = 0.055 \times \frac{\pi (12)^2}{4} \times 43.7 \times 10^6
$$
$$
Q = 0.055 \times 113.1 \times 43.7 \times 10^6 = 2.72 \times 10^8 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Radiative HRR
$$
Q_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot Q = 0.20 \times 2.72 \times 10^8 = 5.44 \times 10^7 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Geometric spreading
$$
q_{geom} = \frac{Q_{rad}}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{5.44 \times 10^7}{4\pi (25)^2} = \frac{5.44 \times 10^7}{7854} = 6926 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** View factor
$$
q_{vf} = q_{geom} \times F_{12} = 6926 \times 0.20 = 1385.2 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Atmospheric transmissivity
$$
q_{final} = q_{vf} \times \tau_a = 1385.2 \times 0.78 = 1080.5 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 1.08 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Comparison with threshold
$$
q_{final} = 1.08 \text{ kW/m}^2 < 12.5 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclusion
**Thermal domino escalation is NOT credible** at 25 m separation for this configuration. The exposure is well below the SEVESO threshold, and the target tank will not fail thermally within a credible exposure window.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]
**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a labelled **Bow-Tie diagram**, explain how a domino cascade can be analysed in terms of threats, top event, consequences, and controls. Use a *gas-cloud explosion in a compressor house* as the primary event leading to a *BLEVE of an adjacent propane sphere* as the secondary event.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using Probit analysis, determine the probability of catastrophic damage to a process vessel located 20 m from a BLEVE involving 8,000 kg of propane. Take explosion efficiency $\eta = 0.04$, $\Delta H_c = 46 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $E_{TNT} = 4.68 \text{ MJ/kg}$, and the Probit equation $Y = -23.8 + 2.92 \ln(p_s)$.

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Bow-Tie Analysis

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L[Threats]
        t1[Gas Leak in Compressor House]
        t2[Delayed Ignition in Open Atmosphere]
        t3[Adjacent Propane Sphere Wall Heating]
    end

    subgraph T[Top Event]
        topE[Compressor House Vapour Cloud Explosion]
    end

    subgraph R[Consequences]
        c1[Heat Radiation > 12.5 kW/m^2 on Propane Sphere]
        c2[BLEVE of Propane Sphere]
        c3[Projectile and Toxic Cascade]
    end

    t1 --> topE
    t2 --> topE
    topE --> c1
    c1 --> c2
    c2 --> c3

    subgraph P[Preventive Controls]
        pc1[Gas Detection + Isolation Valves]
        pc2[Vapour Cloud Suppression System]
    end

    subgraph M[Mitigative Controls]
        mc1[Inter-Unit Separation > 30 m]
        mc2[Water Deluge on Propane Sphere]
        mc3[Thermal Insulation on Sphere]
    end

    t1 -.blocked by.-> pc1
    t1 -.blocked by.-> pc2
    c1 -.mitigated by.-> mc1
    c1 -.mitigated by.-> mc2
    c2 -.mitigated by.-> mc3
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**
The Bow-Tie decomposes the domino event into **left-side threats** (gas leak, delayed ignition, sphere heating) and **right-side consequences** (heat flux on sphere, BLEVE, secondary cascade), converging on the **top event** (compressor house explosion) (2 marks). The **preventive controls** (gas detection, suppression) act on the threats to prevent the top event, while the **mitigative controls** (separation, deluge, insulation) act on the consequences to limit escalation (2 marks). In a domino investigation, *separate Bow-Ties are drawn for each installation*, and the heat-flux arrow linking the two events is the documented escalation vector.

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Probit Analysis

**Step 1 (1 mark):** TNT equivalent mass
$$
W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}} = \frac{0.04 \times 8000 \times 46 \times 10^6}{4.68 \times 10^6} = 3145.3 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Scaled distance
$$
Z = \frac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{20}{(3145.3)^{1/3}} = \frac{20}{14.66} = 1.364 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Overpressure using Baker's TNT correlation
$$
\frac{p_s}{p_0} = \frac{808 \left[ 1 + \left( \frac{Z}{4.5} \right)^2 \right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{Z}{0.048} \right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{Z}{0.32} \right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{Z}{1.35} \right)^2}}
$$

With $Z = 1.364$:
- $Z/4.5 = 0.303 \Rightarrow (Z/4.5)^2 = 0.0918$
- $Z/0.048 = 28.42 \Rightarrow (Z/0.048)^2 = 807.7$
- $Z/0.32 = 4.263 \Rightarrow (Z/0.32)^2 = 18.17$
- $Z/1.35 = 1.010 \Rightarrow (Z/1.35)^2 = 1.020$

$$
\frac{p_s}{p_0} = \frac{808 \times 1.0918}{\sqrt{808.7} \times \sqrt{19.17} \times \sqrt{2.020}} = \frac{882.2}{28.44 \times 4.378 \times 1.421} = \frac{882.2}{176.9} = 4.99
$$

$$
p_s = 4.99 \times 101.3 = 505.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Probit variable
$$
Y = -23.8 + 2.92 \ln(505.5) = -23.8 + 2.92 \times 6.226 = -23.8 + 18.18 = -5.62
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Probability of damage
$$
P_r = 50\% \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 50\% \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{-5.62 - 5}{1.414}\right)\right]
$$
$$
= 50\% \left[1 + \text{erf}(-7.51)\right] \approx 50\% \times [1 - 1.0] \approx 0\%
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion
The probability of catastrophic damage is **essentially zero (less than 0.001%)** at 20 m separation. Hence the existing separation is *more than adequate* against this BLEVE scenario.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "domino" with "near-miss":** A domino is a *realized* secondary event with proven causal linkage — not a hypothetical. Students lose marks for using the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting units in heat-flux problems:** Always convert MJ to J (×10⁶) before plugging into the equation. 1-mark penalty per unit slip.
> 3. **Skipping the view-factor and transmissivity terms:** A common 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation. Both $F_{12}$ and $\tau_a$ are mandatory for credit.
> 4. **Misapplying Probit:** Students often report "Probit = Y" as the answer. The KTU expects the **probability of response $P_r$ in %** as the final answer.
> 5. **Using the wrong Probit equation:** There are *different* Probit equations for thermal damage, blast damage, and missile damage. State the equation you are using and verify its domain.
> 6. **Neglecting the 60-minute credible window:** If temporal linkage cannot be proven within 60 min, the event is *not* classified as a domino under SEVESO guidelines.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Domino Incident Investigation**

- ✅ A **domino incident** is a *cascading* accident across multiple installations, NOT a single-unit event.
- ✅ The **four escalation vectors** are: **heat radiation, blast overpressure, missile/projectile, toxic dispersion**.
- ✅ The **SEVESO domino thresholds** are: **12.5 kW/m², 20 kPa, 1.5×10⁴ J, IDLH** — memorize these.
- ✅ The **five-phase protocol**: Scoping → Evidence → Timeline → Vector Quantification → RCA & Report.
- ✅ The **60-minute credible window** defines a domino event in law/regulation.
- ✅ The **5× footprint rule** is used for mutual-aid and emergency planning zones.
- ✅ The **perimeter map** is the first deliverable, drawn at **1.5× the largest credible event footprint**.
- ✅ The **Poisson decay model** $P_e = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ gives escalation probability vs. exposure time.
- ✅ The **heat-flux equation** requires $\chi_r$, $Q$, $F_{12}$, $\tau_a$, and $4\pi r^2$ — all five terms.
- ✅ **Probit to probability** always uses the error-function conversion: $P_r = 50\%[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$.
- ✅ **TNT-equivalence** uses $E_{TNT} = 4.68 \text{ MJ/kg}$ and the Baker-Westine overpressure correlation.
- ✅ **Bow-Tie diagrams** are drawn *separately* for each installation in a domino event.
- ✅ The **footprint amplification factor** $F_{amp} = A_{total}/A_{primary}$ is the canonical severity metric.
- ✅ **GPS-timestamped SCADA logs** are the master clock for timeline reconstruction.
- ✅ **Indian regulatory anchor:** MSIHC Rules 1989 (Schedule 9) and the Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991.
- ✅ **International regulatory anchors:** EU SEVESO-III Directive (2012/18/EU), US OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, US EPA RMP, UK COMAH 2015.
- ✅ **Forensic evidence** must be preserved *across all affected units* within 24–48 hours of the event.
- ✅ **Probit coefficients** are *not* universal — they depend on the asset type, the vector, and the damage criterion.
- ✅ A **footprint enlargement of < 2×** generally rules out "credible" domino escalation under most regulatory definitions.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions** (point-source model, TNT-equivalence, atmospheric stability class) when presenting numerical results.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logic Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logic Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Logic Diagrams in Safety Engineering

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Logic Diagram** is a deductive, top-down graphical representation of the Boolean logical relationships between various failure events, component failures, human errors, and external conditions that may combine to produce a predefined undesired system state known as the **Top Event** (or **Hazard**). In the discipline of Advanced Safety Engineering and Management, logic diagrams form the quantitative and qualitative backbone of system safety analysis methodologies such as **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**, **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**, **Cause-Consequence Analysis (CCA)**, and **Bow-Tie Analysis (BTA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor Definition:**
> A logic diagram translates qualitative system hazards into a structured Boolean model composed of **Basic Events**, **Intermediate Events**, **Top Events**, and interconnecting **Logic Gates** (AND, OR, NOT, Exclusive-OR, Priority-AND, Inhibit). Each gate obeys strict Boolean algebra rules and acts as a logical operator that dictates the causal propagation of failure through the system.

The primary objective of constructing a logic diagram in safety engineering is to:
1. Identify the **Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)** — the smallest combination of basic events that, if all occur, will cause the top event.
2. Identify the **Minimal Path Sets (MPS)** — the smallest combination of basic events that, if all remain functional (do not fail), guarantees that the top event will not occur.
3. Quantify the **Top Event Probability (or Unavailability)** using Boolean algebra and probability theory.
4. Support **System Reliability Allocation** and **Risk-Based Decision Making**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are a fire safety investigator standing at the scene of a factory fire. You ask, "What combination of failures caused this fire?" The fire (Top Event) did not happen because of one single cause. It happened because **multiple things went wrong in a specific logical sequence**:
- The sprinkler system was under maintenance (Event A) **AND** a flammable chemical leaked (Event B) **AND** the fire alarm failed to trigger evacuation (Event C).

A logic diagram is essentially a **visual courtroom where the investigator lays out all possible "culprits" and maps the exact "rules of conspiracy" (AND, OR) that led to the disaster**. The investigator uses Boolean logic as the "law of evidence" to decide whether the combined failures were sufficient to cause the incident.

Think of it like an **electrical circuit**, but instead of electrons flowing, **failure propagates upward from bottom events to the top event**, and the gates act as the "switches" that determine whether the failure path is completed.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following standardized terms must be memorized for KTU examination purposes:
> - **Top Event (T):** The undesired event being analyzed (e.g., loss of containment, fire, structural collapse).
> - **Basic Event (Bi):** A leaf-level failure event requiring no further development.
> - **Intermediate Event:** A logical combination of lower events awaiting further gate-level resolution.
> - **Undeveloped Event:** A basic event not expanded due to lack of information or insignificance.
> - **External Event:** An event normally expected to occur (e.g., earthquake).
> - **Conditioning Event:** A condition that must exist for a logic gate to produce its output.
> - **Cut Set:** A set of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the top event.
> - **Minimal Cut Set (MCS):** A cut set where removal of any single event prevents the top event.
> - **Path Set:** A set of basic events whose non-occurrence ensures the top event does not occur.
> - **Minimal Path Set (MPS):** A path set where removal of any single event allows the top event to occur.

The standard industry metric used is **failure probability (Q)** per unit time or per mission time, expressed as a dimensionless number between **0 and 1**. The **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** and **Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)** are commonly cited values following **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** standards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Logic Gates Used in Safety Logic Diagrams

A logic diagram's behavior is governed entirely by the logic gates used. The following are the canonical gates recognized in KTU curriculum:

**A) AND Gate (Logical Conjunction, Symbol: ∧)**
- Output occurs **only when ALL input events occur simultaneously**.
- Output = Input₁ ∧ Input₂ ∧ … ∧ Input_n
- Boolean truth table: Output = 1 only if every input is 1.

**B) OR Gate (Logical Disjunction, Symbol: ∨)**
- Output occurs if **any one or more input events occur**.
- Output = Input₁ ∨ Input₂ ∨ … ∨ Input_n
- Boolean truth table: Output = 1 if at least one input is 1.

**C) NOT Gate (Logical Negation, Symbol: ¬ or ̄)**
- Output is the **logical complement** of the input. Used to model success states when only failure data is available.

**D) Exclusive-OR Gate (XOR, Symbol: ⊕)**
- Output occurs when **exactly one** input occurs, but not both.

**E) Priority-AND Gate (P-AND)**
- Output occurs only if **all inputs occur in a specified sequence** (order-sensitive).

**F) Inhibit Gate**
- Output occurs if the single input occurs **AND** a separate conditioning event is present.

### 2.2 Operational Step-by-Step Construction Logic

The construction of a safety logic diagram follows a strict hierarchical methodology:

1. **Define the Top Event** clearly and unambiguously. This is the *unwanted event* under analysis.
2. **Identify Immediate Causes** — the next layer of events that, if they occur, will directly trigger the top event.
3. **Connect Immediate Causes with Logic Gates** (AND/OR) based on whether the top event requires *all* or *any* of these causes.
4. **Recursively Expand Each Intermediate Event** by asking "How can this event happen?" and "Are these causes independent or conditional?"
5. **Terminate at Basic Events** where further decomposition is unnecessary or impractical.
6. **Assign Probabilities** to each basic event using reliability databases, vendor data, or operational records.
7. **Apply Boolean Algebra** to compute the structure function and the top event probability.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.

| Concept | Formula | Description / Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Structure Function (AND) | $\Phi = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | All basic events must fail for top event to occur |
| Structure Function (OR) | $\Phi = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Any single basic event failure causes the top event |
| Top Event Probability (AND, independent) | $P_T = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Multiplication rule for independent events |
| Top Event Probability (OR, independent) | $P_T = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)$ | Complement of joint survival probability |
| Top Event Probability (OR, exclusive) | $P_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Sum rule for mutually exclusive events |
| Boolean Absorption Law | $A + AB = A$ | Used to simplify large fault trees |
| Boolean Idempotent Law | $A \cdot A = A$ | Removes duplicate events |
| De Morgan's Theorem | $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$ | Converts AND to OR for analysis |
| Minimal Cut Set Bound (Rare Event) | $P_T \approx \sum_{j=1}^{k} \prod_{i \in K_j} P_i$ | Upper bound approximation for small probabilities |
| System Unavailability (Series) | $Q_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - Q_i)$ | All components must function |
| System Unavailability (Parallel) | $Q_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$ | At least one component must function |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial process safety, logic diagrams are mandated by regulatory bodies such as the **Health and Safety Executive (HSE, UK)**, the **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA, USA)**, and the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB, India)** for high-hazard industries including nuclear power, petrochemical refining, offshore oil & gas, and aerospace.

Logic diagrams directly support:
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level) Determination** per IEC 61508.
- **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** quantification.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** for facility siting studies.
- **Predictive Maintenance Scheduling** through identification of critical components.
- **Compliance Audits** under ISO 45001 and ISO 14001 management systems.
- **Accident Investigation and Root Cause Analysis** after a major incident.

> [!NOTE]
> In modern digital twins and Industry 4.0 frameworks, logic diagrams are increasingly implemented as **computable Boolean graphs** that update in real-time with sensor data, allowing continuous probabilistic risk monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Top Event Probability for an AND Gate

Consider two independent basic events $A$ and $B$ feeding into an AND gate producing the top event $T$.

**Step 1: Define the Boolean relationship.**

The top event occurs if and only if *both* $A$ and $B$ occur.

$$T = A \land B$$

**Step 2: Apply the product rule of probability for independent events.**

For two statistically independent events, the joint probability equals the product of their marginal probabilities.

$$P_T = P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

**Step 3: Generalize to n independent basic events.**

For $n$ basic events $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ all feeding an AND gate:

$$P_T = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i)$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example (for KTU problem solving).**

Let $P(A) = 0.01$ and $P(B) = 0.02$.

$$P_T = 0.01 \times 0.02 = 0.0002 = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

> This shows how redundancy (AND gate) drastically reduces top event probability compared to a single component.

### 3.2 Derivation: Top Event Probability for an OR Gate

Consider two independent basic events $A$ and $B$ feeding an OR gate.

**Step 1: Define the Boolean relationship.**

The top event occurs if *either* $A$ or $B$ (or both) occur.

$$T = A \lor B$$

**Step 2: Use the complement of the joint non-occurrence.**

It is mathematically simpler to compute the probability that *neither* event occurs, then subtract from 1.

$$P(\overline{T}) = P(\overline{A} \cap \overline{B}) = P(\overline{A}) \cdot P(\overline{B}) \text{ (independence)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the complement rule.**

$$P_T = 1 - P(\overline{T}) = 1 - P(\overline{A}) \cdot P(\overline{B})$$

$$P_T = 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))$$

**Step 4: Generalize to n independent basic events.**

$$P_T = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P(B_i))$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU type).**

Let $P(A) = 0.01$ and $P(B) = 0.02$.

$$P_T = 1 - (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.02)$$

$$P_T = 1 - (0.99)(0.98)$$

$$P_T = 1 - 0.9702 = 0.0298 \approx 2.98 \times 10^{-2}$$

> Compare this with the AND gate result of $2 \times 10^{-4}$. The OR gate produces a **far higher** top event probability because any single failure is sufficient.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Top Event Probability for Mixed Two-Event Configurations

The following table summarizes the analytical results for a 2-event fault tree configuration under independence assumption. The probabilities are: $P(A) = 0.1$ and $P(B) = 0.2$ for direct numerical comparison.

| Gate Type | Boolean Expression | Top Event Probability $P_T$ | Numerical Result | Engineering Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| AND | $T = A \land B$ | $P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | $0.02$ | High redundancy, low risk |
| OR | $T = A \lor B$ | $1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))$ | $0.28$ | Single-point sensitivity, higher risk |
| NOT | $T = \overline{A}$ | $1 - P(A)$ | $0.90$ | Complement logic, used for success modeling |
| XOR | $T = A \oplus B$ | $P(A) + P(B) - 2 P(A) P(B)$ | $0.26$ | Exactly one fails; useful in redundant system design |
| NAND | $T = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | $1 - P(A) P(B)$ | $0.98$ | Universal gate; failure avoidance logic |
| NOR | $T = \overline{A + B}$ | $(1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))$ | $0.72$ | Universal gate; safe-state modeling |

### 3.4 Derivation: Minimal Cut Set Probability Approximation

**Step 1: Define the structure function as a union of minimal cut sets.**

For a fault tree decomposed into $k$ minimal cut sets $K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_k$:

$$\Phi = \bigcup_{j=1}^{k} K_j$$

**Step 2: Apply the inclusion-exclusion principle.**

$$P_T = \sum_{j} P(K_j) - \sum_{j<m} P(K_j \cap K_m) + \sum_{j<m<n} P(K_j \cap K_m \cap K_n) - \cdots$$

**Step 3: Apply the rare event approximation.**

When all basic event probabilities are small (typically $P_i < 0.1$), the higher-order intersection terms are negligible:

$$P_T \approx \sum_{j=1}^{k} \prod_{i \in K_j} P_i$$

This is the **upper bound approximation** universally used in industrial FTA software such as **SAPHIRE**, **RiskSpectrum**, and **FaultTree+**.

**Step 4: Worked Numerical Example.**

A fault tree has two minimal cut sets: $K_1 = \{A, B\}$ and $K_2 = \{C\}$.
Given: $P(A) = 0.01$, $P(B) = 0.02$, $P(C) = 0.005$.

$$P_T \approx P(A) P(B) + P(C) - P(A) P(B) P(C)$$

$$P_T \approx (0.01)(0.02) + 0.005 - (0.01)(0.02)(0.005)$$

$$P_T \approx 0.0002 + 0.005 - 0.0000001$$

$$P_T \approx 0.0051999 \approx 5.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

> The single-point failure event $C$ dominates the top event probability, indicating that **risk reduction efforts should prioritize mitigating Event C**.

### 3.5 Boolean Algebra Simplification Rules (Examiner's Toolkit)

The following identities are essential for simplifying fault trees before cut-set enumeration:

1. **Identity Laws:** $A + 0 = A$ and $A \cdot 1 = A$
2. **Idempotent Laws:** $A + A = A$ and $A \cdot A = A$
3. **Complement Laws:** $A + \overline{A} = 1$ and $A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$
4. **Domination Laws:** $A + 1 = 1$ and $A \cdot 0 = 0$
5. **Double Negation:** $\overline{\overline{A}} = A$
6. **Commutative Laws:** $A + B = B + A$ and $A \cdot B = B \cdot A$
7. **Associative Laws:** $(A + B) + C = A + (B + C)$ and $(A \cdot B) \cdot C = A \cdot (B \cdot C)$
8. **Distributive Laws:** $A + (B \cdot C) = (A + B)(A + C)$ and $A \cdot (B + C) = A \cdot B + A \cdot C$
9. **De Morgan's Theorems:** $\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$ and $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$
10. **Absorption Law:** $A + A \cdot B = A$ and $A \cdot (A + B) = A$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Standard Logic Gate Symbols (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the standard logic gate symbols and their internal functional architecture as used in KTU-validated safety logic diagrams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["INPUT EVENT A"]:::inputNode
    inputB["INPUT EVENT B"]:::inputNode
    inputC["INPUT EVENT C"]:::inputNode
    gateAND["AND GATE OUTPUT = 1 ONLY IF ALL INPUTS = 1"]:::andNode
    gateOR["OR GATE OUTPUT = 1 IF ANY INPUT = 1"]:::orNode
    gateNOT["NOT GATE OUTPUT = COMPLEMENT OF INPUT"]:::notNode
    topEvent["TOP EVENT T HAZARD OR UNDESIRED STATE"]:::topNode
    outputT["P T NUMERICAL PROBABILITY"]:::resultNode

    inputA --> gateAND
    inputB --> gateAND
    gateAND --> topEvent
    inputB --> gateOR
    inputC --> gateOR
    gateOR --> topEvent
    inputA --> gateNOT
    gateNOT --> topEvent
    topEvent --> outputT

    classDef inputNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef andNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    classDef orNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
    classDef notNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
    classDef topNode fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
    classDef resultNode fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Fault Tree Schematic for "Reactor Coolant Pump Failure"

The following block-level architecture flow represents a simplified fault tree for a top event **"Reactor Coolant Pump (RCP) Fails to Deliver Required Flow"** as commonly seen in nuclear and process safety case studies.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    topEvtT["TOP EVENT T RCP FAILS TO DELIVER FLOW"]:::topNode
    orGate1["OR GATE 1"]:::orNode
    orGate2["OR GATE 2"]:::orNode
    andGate1["AND GATE 1"]:::andNode
    andGate2["AND GATE 2"]:::andNode
    mechFail["MECHANICAL FAILURE OF PUMP"]:::basicNode
    elecFail["ELECTRICAL POWER SUPPLY FAILURE"]:::basicNode
    sealFail["SEAL FAILURE CAUSING LEAK"]:::basicNode
    hmiFail["OPERATOR FAILS TO START BACKUP"]:::basicNode
    instFail["FLOW INSTRUMENT FAILS"]:::basicNode
    ctlFail["CONTROL VALVE STICKS CLOSED"]:::basicNode

    mechFail --> orGate1
    elecFail --> orGate1
    sealFail --> orGate2
    hmiFail --> andGate1
    instFail --> andGate1
    andGate1 --> orGate2
    ctlFail --> andGate2
    elecFail --> andGate2
    andGate2 --> orGate2
    orGate1 --> topEvtT
    orGate2 --> topEvtT

    classDef topNode fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef orNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
    classDef andNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    classDef basicNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Logic Diagram Construction Workflow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["STEP 1 DEFINE TOP EVENT"]:::procNode
    stepB["STEP 2 IDENTIFY IMMEDIATE CAUSES"]:::procNode
    stepC["STEP 3 APPLY AND OR GATES"]:::procNode
    stepD["STEP 4 EXPAND INTERMEDIATE EVENTS RECURSIVELY"]:::procNode
    stepE["STEP 5 TERMINATE AT BASIC EVENTS"]:::procNode
    stepF["STEP 6 ASSIGN PROBABILITIES"]:::procNode
    stepG["STEP 7 APPLY BOOLEAN ALGEBRA"]:::procNode
    stepH["STEP 8 COMPUTE P T TOP EVENT PROBABILITY"]:::resultNode
    stepI["STEP 9 IDENTIFY MINIMAL CUT SETS"]:::resultNode

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI

    classDef procNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef resultNode fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000000
```

### 4.4 Comparative Truth Table Block (AND vs OR Behavior)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subA["AND GATE TRUTH TABLE"]:::andNode
    subB["OR GATE TRUTH TABLE"]:::orNode
    subArow1["A=0 B=0 OUTPUT=0"]:::rowNode
    subArow2["A=0 B=1 OUTPUT=0"]:::rowNode
    subArow3["A=1 B=0 OUTPUT=0"]:::rowNode
    subArow4["A=1 B=1 OUTPUT=1"]:::rowNode
    subBrow1["A=0 B=0 OUTPUT=0"]:::rowNode
    subBrow2["A=0 B=1 OUTPUT=1"]:::rowNode
    subBrow3["A=1 B=0 OUTPUT=1"]:::rowNode
    subBrow4["A=1 B=1 OUTPUT=1"]:::rowNode

    subA --> subArow1
    subA --> subArow2
    subA --> subArow3
    subA --> subArow4
    subB --> subBrow1
    subB --> subBrow2
    subB --> subBrow3
    subB --> subBrow4

    classDef andNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    classDef orNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
    classDef rowNode fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define a Logic Diagram in the context of safety engineering. List the four main symbols used in constructing a fault tree logic diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A logic diagram in safety engineering is a top-down graphical representation of Boolean logical relationships between failure events that can lead to a predefined Top Event (undesired system state). It forms the structural foundation of Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) used for system safety quantification.

The four main symbols are:
1. **Rectangle** — represents the Top Event or an Intermediate Event resulting from a logic gate.
2. **Circle** — represents a Basic Event, a leaf-level failure requiring no further development.
3. **AND Gate** — output occurs only when ALL inputs occur (symbolized as ∧).
4. **OR Gate** — output occurs if ANY input occurs (symbolized as ∨).

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a Minimal Cut Set (MCS) and a Minimal Path Set (MPS) in a logic diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is the smallest combination of basic events whose simultaneous *occurrence* (failure) will cause the Top Event. Removing any single event from an MCS prevents the Top Event. MCSs represent **failure pathways**.

A **Minimal Path Set (MPS)** is the smallest combination of basic events whose simultaneous *non-occurrence* (success) guarantees that the Top Event will not occur. Removing any single event from an MPS allows the Top Event to occur. MPSs represent **success pathways** and are dual to MCSs through De Morgan's transformation.

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 questions offer internal choice. Two alternative 14-mark questions are provided below. The examiner may set either as the official question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction of a logic diagram for a safety-critical system. Discuss the role of AND, OR, and Inhibit gates with industrial examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

The construction of a logic diagram follows a systematic top-down deductive methodology:

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the Top Event. The Top Event is the undesired incident under analysis (e.g., "Reactor Overpressure", "Offshore Platform Fire"). It must be precisely worded to avoid ambiguity.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Identify the immediate causes that would directly trigger the Top Event and connect them via logic gates. The choice between AND and OR gates is critical:
- **AND Gate** is used when the Top Event occurs *only if all listed causes occur simultaneously*. *Example:* A fire suppression system fails to extinguish fire **AND** the fire alarm fails to sound evacuation.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):**
- **OR Gate** is used when the Top Event occurs if *any one* of the listed causes occur. *Example:* A pressure vessel ruptures **OR** a pipe weld fails **OR** a flange gasket blows out.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):**
- **Inhibit Gate** is used when the output occurs *only if* the input event occurs **AND** a separate conditioning event is true. *Example:* A toxic gas release causes fatality (input) **INHIBITED BY** the presence of personnel in the zone (conditioning event).

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Recursively expand intermediate events into sub-causes using the same gate logic until basic events are reached.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Assign probability values to each basic event from reliability databases (e.g., OREDA, IEEE Std 500).

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Apply Boolean algebra and probability rules to compute the Top Event probability, identifying Minimal Cut Sets for prioritization of risk reduction.

**(b)** A process plant has two independent failure paths to a top event. Path 1 requires both Events A (P=0.005) and B (P=0.01) to occur (AND gate). Path 2 requires any one of Events C (P=0.02) or D (P=0.03) to occur (OR gate). Both paths feed an OR gate producing the Top Event. Calculate the Top Event probability using Boolean algebra. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Express the Boolean structure function.

Let $K_1$ = Path 1 cut set, $K_2$ = Path 2 cut set.
- $K_1 = A \cdot B$ (AND gate)
- $K_2 = C + D$ (OR gate)
- $T = K_1 + K_2$ (top OR gate)

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute probability of Path 1.

$$P(K_1) = P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0.005 \times 0.01 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute probability of Path 2.

$$P(K_2) = 1 - (1 - P(C))(1 - P(D))$$

$$P(K_2) = 1 - (1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.03)$$

$$P(K_2) = 1 - (0.98)(0.97)$$

$$P(K_2) = 1 - 0.9506 = 0.0494$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute probability of Top Event using the OR formula.

$$P_T = 1 - (1 - P(K_1))(1 - P(K_2))$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Substitute numerical values.

$$P_T = 1 - (1 - 0.00005)(1 - 0.0494)$$

$$P_T = 1 - (0.99995)(0.9506)$$

$$P_T = 1 - 0.9506474$$

$$P_T \approx 0.0493526 \approx 4.94 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Identify Minimal Cut Sets and engineering insight.

The two Minimal Cut Sets are:
- $MCS_1 = \{A, B\}$ with probability $5 \times 10^{-5}$
- $MCS_2 = \{C\}$ with probability $0.02$
- $MCS_3 = \{D\}$ with probability $0.03$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Conclude with engineering recommendation.

$$\boxed{P_T \approx 4.94 \times 10^{-2}}$$

The Top Event is dominated by the single-point failures in Path 2. Risk reduction should focus on adding redundancy to Events C and D, or installing additional safety interlocks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for constructing a fault tree logic diagram. State De Morgan's Theorems and demonstrate their application in converting an OR-based fault tree to an AND-based success tree. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** State the construction procedure.

1. **Define the Top Event** with operational clarity.
2. **Identify immediate causes** through "How can this happen?" brainstorming with system experts.
3. **Apply AND/OR gates** based on whether all or any of the causes must be present.
4. **Expand recursively** until basic events are reached.
5. **Validate** through independent expert review.
6. **Quantify** with probability data.
7. **Compute** Top Event probability and identify Minimal Cut Sets.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** State De Morgan's Theorems.

$$\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B} \text{ (First Theorem)}$$

$$\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B} \text{ (Second Theorem)}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Demonstrate transformation from OR fault tree to AND success tree.

Consider a fault tree with Top Event:
$$T = A + B + C \text{ (OR gate)}$$

Applying De Morgan's First Theorem by complementing both sides:

$$\overline{T} = \overline{A + B + C} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B} \cdot \overline{C}$$

The dual system (success tree) is the AND of the complements of the basic events.

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Engineering significance.

This transformation is used in **success tree analysis** to identify Minimal Path Sets (MPS) and is the mathematical basis for converting an OR-based unreliability model into an AND-based reliability model.

**(b)** Three independent components A, B, and C form a safety system. The system fails if (i) all three fail simultaneously, OR (ii) any two fail. Compute the system failure probability given P(A) = P(B) = P(C) = 0.1. Use exact Boolean expansion. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Express the failure condition in Boolean form.

System fails if all three fail (ABC) OR any two fail (AB + BC + CA).

$$T = ABC + AB + BC + CA$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Apply Boolean absorption to simplify.

Using the absorption law $AB + ABC = AB$:

$$T = AB + BC + CA$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Calculate the probability of each minimal cut set.

Each pair of components has probability:

$$P(AB) = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01$$
$$P(BC) = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01$$
$$P(CA) = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Apply the exact inclusion-exclusion formula.

$$P_T = P(AB) + P(BC) + P(CA) - P(AB \cdot BC) - P(AB \cdot CA) - P(BC \cdot CA) + P(AB \cdot BC \cdot CA)$$

Since all events are independent, $P(AB \cdot BC) = P(ABC) = 0.001$ (similarly for others), and $P(AB \cdot BC \cdot CA) = P(ABC) = 0.001$.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Substitute values.

$$P_T = 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.01 - 0.001 - 0.001 - 0.001 + 0.001$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Compute final result.

$$P_T = 0.03 - 0.003 + 0.001 = 0.028$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Conclude.

$$\boxed{P_T = 0.028 = 2.8 \times 10^{-2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Pitfall 1 — Mis-application of independence assumption:** Students often assume independence without justification. Always explicitly state "assuming statistical independence of basic events" before applying product rules.
> 2. **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting De Morgan's transformation:** When asked to find Minimal Path Sets, students often directly list path sets without using De Morgan's theorem to transform the fault tree.
> 3. **Pitfall 3 — Using the rare event approximation when not valid:** The upper bound approximation $P_T \approx \sum P(MCS_j)$ is valid **only** when individual probabilities are below 0.1. For larger probabilities, the exact inclusion-exclusion is required.
> 4. **Pitfall 4 — Forgetting to identify MCSs numerically:** Examiners award full marks only when both the Boolean expression and the numerical result are clearly boxed.
> 5. **Pitfall 5 — Confusing AND with OR interpretation:** A common error is reversing gate logic, leading to inverted probability calculations.
> 6. **Pitfall 6 — Unit omission:** Although probabilities are dimensionless, students must mention that the result is a "probability per mission" or "per hour" as relevant.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Logic Diagrams:**

- A **logic diagram** is a Boolean graphical model of system failure logic, central to Fault Tree Analysis.
- The **Top Event** is the undesired state being analyzed; the diagram flows *top-down*.
- **AND Gate** output requires *all* inputs to be true; **OR Gate** output requires *any* input to be true.
- For independent events, AND-gate probability is the **product**; OR-gate probability uses the **complement of the product of complements**.
- **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** = smallest failure set causing Top Event; **Minimal Path Set (MPS)** = smallest success set preventing Top Event.
- **De Morgan's Theorems** are the mathematical bridge between fault trees and success trees.
- **Boolean algebra simplification** must precede cut-set enumeration for efficiency.
- The **rare event upper bound approximation** $P_T \approx \sum \prod P_i$ applies only when $P_i \ll 1$.
- Logic diagrams are mandated by **IEC 61508, IEC 61511, OSHA, HSE, AERB** in high-hazard industries.
- The construction procedure is **iterative, top-down, deductive**, and requires **multi-disciplinary expert validation**.
- Standard gate symbols must be memorized: Rectangle (event), Circle (basic event), Diamond (undeveloped), House (external).
- For KTU numerical problems, always **box the final answer** and **state units** (dimensionless probability, or per-hour rate).
- Always justify the **independence assumption** explicitly in your solution to score full marks.
- **Bow-Tie Analysis** combines a fault tree (left) and an event tree (right) — frequently asked as a comparative question.
- **Common Cause Failures (CCF)** violate the independence assumption; advanced KTU problems may introduce a beta-factor model.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Input Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Safety Engineering and Management**, **Input Requirements** refer to the systematic set of data, information, documentation, regulatory frameworks, and stakeholder inputs that are essential for the planning, implementation, monitoring, and continual improvement of an organization's **Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) management system**. These inputs form the foundation upon which risk assessments, safety audits, hazard identifications, and corrective action plans are built, in alignment with international standards such as **ISO 45001:2018**, **OHSAS 18001**, and the **ILO-OSH 2001 Guidelines**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Input Requirements are the precursor to all downstream safety management activities. Without accurate, complete, and timely inputs, no risk assessment, safety audit, or management review can produce reliable outputs. This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Understand the fundamental principles of safety management systems and their implementation frameworks."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Input Requirements** as the **"ingredients list and recipe measurements"** before you bake a complex cake. Imagine a hospital kitchen preparing a meal for 500 patients. Before the chef begins cooking, they must first know:
- **How many patients** need meals (organizational scope),
- **What dietary restrictions** exist (legal/regulatory inputs),
- **What ingredients are available** in the pantry (internal resources),
- **What feedback** previous patients gave (historical incident data),
- **What new health guidelines** have been issued (external standards).

Similarly, in safety engineering, before designing a safety program, a safety manager needs to know the organization's hazards, legal obligations, worker concerns, past incident records, and management expectations. **All of these are "Input Requirements."** Skip any one, and the entire safety "recipe" can collapse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference:** The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle**, foundational to ISO 45001, relies heavily on the **"Plan"** phase, which is entirely dependent on robust input requirements. The quality of inputs directly determines the quality of outputs.

### 1.3 Categories of Input Requirements (Foundational Overview)

Input requirements in safety management can be broadly classified into **five primary categories**:

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Legal & Regulatory Inputs** | National and international laws, standards, codes | Factories Act 1948, OSHA, ISO 45001 |
| **Organizational Inputs** | Internal policies, processes, resources | Safety policy, budget, workforce data |
| **Hazard & Risk Inputs** | Identified hazards, risk registers, exposure data | JSA, HIRA, FMEA outputs |
| **Historical & Performance Inputs** | Past incidents, audit results, KPIs | LTIFR, near-miss reports |
| **Stakeholder Inputs** | Worker feedback, contractor data, customer expectations | Suggestion schemes, grievance records |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

When dealing with input data, several standard safety metrics are universally referenced:

- **LTIFR (Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate)** = $\frac{\text{Number of lost-time injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total man-hours worked}}$
- **TRIR (Total Recordable Incident Rate)** = $\frac{\text{Number of recordable incidents} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total man-hours worked}}$
- **Severity Rate (SR)** = $\frac{\text{Number of days lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Total man-hours worked}}$
- **Probability (P)**: A dimensionless value ranging from **0 to 1**.
- **Risk Score (R)** = $P \times C$ where $C$ is Consequence severity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Input-Process-Output Model

The theoretical foundation of Input Requirements is rooted in **Systems Theory**, where any management system is viewed as a transformation process:

$$\text{Inputs} \xrightarrow{\text{Process}} \text{Outputs} \xrightarrow{\text{Feedback}} \text{Improved Inputs}$$

In safety management, this translates to:

$$\text{Safety Inputs} \xrightarrow{\text{Risk Assessment + Controls}} \text{Safe Operations} \xrightarrow{\text{Audit + Review}} \text{Refined Inputs}$$

### 2.2 Detailed Components of Input Requirements

#### 2.2.1 Legal and Regulatory Inputs

- **National Legislation**: Factories Act 1948 (India), OSHA 1970 (USA), HSE Act 1974 (UK)
- **International Standards**: ISO 45001:2018, ISO 14001:2015, OHSAS 18001:2007
- **Industry-Specific Codes**: API, ASME, NFPA, IEC standards
- **Statutory Requirements**: Licensing, permits, mandatory reporting thresholds

#### 2.2.2 Organizational Context Inputs (Clause 4 of ISO 45001)

- **Internal Issues**: Organizational culture, governance structure, workforce competency
- **External Issues**: Political, economic, social, technological, environmental (PESTLE) factors
- **Scope of the OHS Management System**: Boundaries and applicability

#### 2.2.3 Needs and Expectations of Workers and Other Interested Parties (Clause 4.2)

- Workers' safety concerns
- Contractor and supplier requirements
- Customer contractual safety clauses
- Community and environmental group expectations

#### 2.2.4 Scope Determination Inputs

- Physical boundaries of the organization
- Activities, products, and services
- Authority and ability to exercise control
- Relevant legal and regulatory requirements

#### 2.2.5 Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment Inputs

- **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)**
- **Process and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)**
- **Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS/SDS)**
- **Equipment specifications and maintenance records**
- **Historical incident and near-miss data**
- **Workplace inspection reports**
- **Worker participation records**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory for KTU Board Exam:** Memorize the following table. Questions on input requirements frequently test these formulas and the categorization of inputs.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|-------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| **Risk Score** | $R = P \times C$ | Dimensionless | Risk prioritization |
| **LTIFR** | $\text{LTIFR} = \frac{N \times 10^6}{H}$ | injuries per million man-hours | Injury frequency tracking |
| **TRIR** | $\text{TRIR} = \frac{N \times 200{,}000}{H}$ | injuries per 100 FTE | OSHA-compliant reporting |
| **Severity Rate** | $\text{SR} = \frac{D \times 10^6}{H}$ | days lost per million man-hours | Accident severity tracking |
| **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** | $\text{FAR} = \frac{F \times 10^8}{H}$ | fatalities per $10^8$ man-hours | High-hazard industries |
| **Probability Multiplier** | $P = 1 - (1-p_1)(1-p_2)\ldots(1-p_n)$ | Dimensionless | Independent event OR logic |
| **Risk Matrix Cell Value** | $R = f(P, C)$ | Categorical (Low/Med/High) | Qualitative assessment |
| **ALARP Equation** | $\text{Cost of further risk reduction} \leq \text{Risk reduction benefit}$ | Monetary units | Risk decision-making |

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $0 \leq P \leq 1$
- $C > 0$ (always non-negative)
- $R > 0$ (risk is always positive)
- $H > 0$ (working hours must be positive)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The systematic collection of input requirements is critical in:

- **Process Industries** (Oil & Gas, Petrochemicals): Determining HAZOP and SIL requirements
- **Construction Industry**: Designing Site-Specific Safety Plans (SSSP)
- **Manufacturing**: Establishing Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedures
- **Aviation**: Supporting Safety Management Systems (SMS) per ICAO Doc 9859
- **Mining**: Meeting MSHA reporting and MSHA Part 46/48 training requirements
- **Healthcare**: Complying with Joint Commission and NABH standards

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Composite Risk Score from Multiple Input Sources

A frequent KTU question involves computing a **composite risk score** from multiple input data points. Let us derive the standard method.

**Problem Setup:**
Given three independent hazard scenarios with the following input data, derive the aggregate risk score for a chemical plant reactor system.

| Hazard | Probability (P) | Consequence Cost (₹ in lakhs) |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| H1: Pump seal failure | 0.05 | 50 |
| H2: Valve leak | 0.10 | 20 |
| H3: Sensor failure | 0.02 | 100 |

**Step 1: Compute Individual Risk Scores**

For each hazard, the risk score is computed using:

$$R_i = P_i \times C_i$$

**Step 1a: For H1 (Pump seal failure)**

$$R_1 = P_1 \times C_1$$

$$R_1 = 0.05 \times 50$$

$$R_1 = 2.5 \text{ lakh ₹ per year}$$

**Step 1b: For H2 (Valve leak)**

$$R_2 = P_2 \times C_2$$

$$R_2 = 0.10 \times 20$$

$$R_2 = 2.0 \text{ lakh ₹ per year}$$

**Step 1c: For H3 (Sensor failure)**

$$R_3 = P_3 \times C_3$$

$$R_3 = 0.02 \times 100$$

$$R_3 = 2.0 \text{ lakh ₹ per year}$$

**Step 2: Determine Aggregation Logic**

Since the events are **independent**, the aggregate probability of at least one event occurring is:

$$P_{\text{aggregate}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)$$

**Step 3: Calculate Aggregate Probability**

$$P_{\text{aggregate}} = 1 - (1 - 0.05)(1 - 0.10)(1 - 0.02)$$

$$P_{\text{aggregate}} = 1 - (0.95)(0.90)(0.98)$$

**Step 3a: Compute intermediate product**

$$(0.95)(0.90) = 0.855$$

**Step 3b: Continue the product**

$$(0.855)(0.98) = 0.8379$$

**Step 3c: Final aggregate probability**

$$P_{\text{aggregate}} = 1 - 0.8379 = 0.1621$$

**Step 4: Compute Sum of Individual Risk Scores**

$$R_{\text{sum}} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$$

$$R_{\text{sum}} = 2.5 + 2.0 + 2.0$$

$$R_{\text{sum}} = 6.5 \text{ lakh ₹ per year}$$

**Step 5: Compute Expected Loss (Aggregate Risk)**

For independent events, the aggregate expected annual loss is the sum of individual expected losses:

$$R_{\text{aggregate}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

$$R_{\text{aggregate}} = 6.5 \text{ lakh ₹ per year}$$

**Step 6: Interpretation and Decision-Making Input**

- Aggregate probability of an incident: **16.21% per year**
- Expected annual loss: **₹6.5 lakhs**
- If the cost of installing a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is **₹8 lakhs (one-time)** and reduces all P values by **80%**, recompute:

$$P_{1,\text{new}} = 0.05 \times 0.20 = 0.010$$
$$P_{2,\text{new}} = 0.10 \times 0.20 = 0.020$$
$$P_{3,\text{new}} = 0.02 \times 0.20 = 0.004$$

$$R_{\text{new}} = (0.010 \times 50) + (0.020 \times 20) + (0.004 \times 100)$$

$$R_{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.4 = 1.3 \text{ lakh ₹ per year}$$

**Annual Savings:**

$$\text{Savings} = R_{\text{old}} - R_{\text{new}} = 6.5 - 1.3 = 5.2 \text{ lakh ₹ per year}$$

**Payback Period:**

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{8.0}{5.2} \approx 1.54 \text{ years}$$

This entire calculation is driven by **Input Requirements** — the probabilities, costs, and equipment specifications that must be collected before any engineering decision can be made.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Inputs to ISO 45001 Clauses

The following matrix shows how different input requirements map to specific ISO 45001:2018 clauses. This is a **high-yield KTU topic**.

| ISO 45001 Clause | Required Input | Source of Input | Output Generated |
|------------------|----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| 4.1 (Context) | Internal/external issues | SWOT, PESTLE analysis | OHS scope |
| 4.2 (Needs) | Worker expectations | Surveys, grievance data | Compliance obligations |
| 5.2 (Policy) | Management commitment | Board resolutions, vision docs | OHS policy document |
| 6.1.2 (Hazards) | Hazard inventory | JSA, HIRA, incident records | Risk register |
| 7.2 (Competence) | Training records | HR database, skill matrix | Training plan |
| 8.1.2 (Legal) | Legal register | Regulatory bulletins | Compliance checklist |
| 9.1.1 (Monitoring) | KPI data | Inspection logs, audits | Performance reports |
| 10.2 (Incidents) | Incident reports | Investigation records | Corrective actions |

### 3.3 Derivation: Severity Rate from Raw Input Data

**Given:** A factory has 250 workers working 8 hours/day, 300 days/year. In one year, there were 3 lost-time injuries resulting in 15, 25, and 40 days lost respectively.

**Step 1: Total man-hours worked**

$$H = 250 \times 8 \times 300$$

$$H = 600{,}000 \text{ man-hours}$$

**Step 2: Total number of lost-time injuries**

$$N = 3 \text{ injuries}$$

**Step 3: Calculate LTIFR**

$$\text{LTIFR} = \frac{N \times 10^6}{H}$$

$$\text{LTIFR} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{600{,}000}$$

$$\text{LTIFR} = 5.0 \text{ injuries per million man-hours}$$

**Step 4: Total days lost**

$$D = 15 + 25 + 40 = 80 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Severity Rate**

$$\text{SR} = \frac{D \times 10^6}{H}$$

$$\text{SR} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{600{,}000}$$

$$\text{SR} = 133.33 \text{ days lost per million man-hours}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Frequency-Severity Relationship**

$$\text{Equivalent Days per Injury} = \frac{\text{SR}}{\text{LTIFR}} = \frac{133.33}{5.0} = 26.67 \text{ days/injury}$$

This standardized output is a critical **management review input** for the next planning cycle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Input Requirements Flow Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Environment] --> B[Legal and Regulatory Inputs]
    C[Internal Organization] --> D[Organizational Context Inputs]
    E[Workers and Stakeholders] --> F[Stakeholder and Worker Inputs]
    G[Historical Records] --> H[Performance and Incident Inputs]
    I[Technical Documentation] --> J[Hazard and Process Inputs]

    B --> K[Input Requirements Aggregator]
    D --> K
    F --> K
    H --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L[Validated Input Database]
    L --> M[Risk Assessment Process]
    L --> N[Safety Management Planning]
    L --> O[Compliance Evaluation]
    L --> P[Performance Measurement]

    M --> Q[Risk Register and Controls]
    N --> R[OHS Objectives and Programs]
    O --> S[Legal Compliance Status]
    P --> T[KPI Dashboard and Reports]

    Q --> U[Management Review]
    R --> U
    S --> U
    T --> U

    U --> V[Continual Improvement Loop]
    V --> A
    V --> C
```

### 4.2 Input Categorization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Requirements] --> B[Internal Inputs]
    A --> C[External Inputs]
    A --> D[Hybrid Inputs]

    B --> B1[Organizational Policies]
    B --> B2[Past Incident Data]
    B --> B3[Workforce Competency]
    B --> B4[Equipment Records]

    C --> C1[National Laws]
    C --> C2[International Standards]
    C --> C3[Customer Requirements]
    C --> C4[Industry Best Practices]

    D --> D1[Risk Assessments]
    D --> D2[Audit Findings]
    D --> D3[Training Effectiveness]
    D --> D4[Emergency Response Records]
```

### 4.3 PDCA Cycle with Input Requirements Mapping
