These represent the system's *strongest defense lines*. ### 2.3.1 Example: Identifying an MCS Consider the Boolean expression: $$T = A \cdot B + C \cdot D + A \cdot C$$ Expanding using distributive law: $$T = A B + A C + C D$$ The **Minimal Cut Sets** are: $\{A, B\}$, $\{A, C\}$, and $\{C, D\}$. (Notice $\{A,B,C\}$ is *not* minimal because removing $A$ still gives the cut set $\{B, C\}$ which also triggers T.) --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **THE FOLLOWING TABLE IS THE EXAMINER'S BIBLE FOR LOGIC DIAGRAM QUESTIONS — MEMORIZE IT.** | # | Concept | Formula | Application / Use Case | |:-:|:--------|:--------|:-----------------------| | 1 | AND Probability (independent) | $P_{\text{AND}} = P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdots P_n$ | Series redundancy; simultaneous failure | | 2 | OR Probability (independent) | $P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$ | Multiple alternative failure paths | | 3 | Unavailability (constant failure rate) | $Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx \lambda t$ | Probability component is failed at time $t$ | | 4 | Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of *no* failure up to time $t$ | | 5 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ | Expected operational time between failures | | 6 | Top Event Probability (Boolean expansion) | $P_T = P(\text{MCS}_1) + P(\text{MCS}_2) - \text{overlaps}$ | Quantitative risk assessment | | 7 | Minimal Cut Set (1st order) | Single component failure causes T | Most critical — needs immediate redesign | | 8 | Minimal Cut Set (2nd order) | Two simultaneous component failures cause T | High priority for redundancy review | | 9 | De Morgan (AND → OR) | $\overline{A \cdot B} = \bar{A} + \bar{B}$ | Success tree ↔ Fault tree conversion | | 10 | k-out-of-n Voting | $P_T = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} Q^j (1-Q)^{n-j}$ | Reactor trip systems, sensor voting | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications | Industry | Application of Logic Diagrams | |:---------|:------------------------------| | **Nuclear Power** | Reactor Safety Study (WASH-1400) used fault trees to model core melt frequency. | | **Chemical Process** | Dow Fire & Explosion Index uses logic trees for process hazard analysis. | | **Aviation** | Air crash investigators reconstruct logic diagrams to identify causal chains. | | **Automotive** | ISO 26262 mandates fault trees for functional safety of ADAS systems. | | **Offshore Oil & Gas** | Used for Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) of blowout preventers. | --- # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Top Event Probability from a Simple AND/OR Logic Diagram > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** > A safety-critical system has a top event T. The fault tree is: > T = (A AND B) OR (C AND D) OR (E) > Given: $P(A) = 0.01$, $P(B) = 0.02$, $P(C) = 0.005$, $P(D) = 0.008$, $P(E) = 0.001$. > All events are independent and rare. Find $P(T)$. ### Step 1: Write the Boolean Expression $$T = (A \cdot B) + (C \cdot D) + E$$ ### Step 2: Identify the Minimal Cut Sets Using Boolean expansion, the three minimal cut sets are: - $\text{MCS}_1 = \{A, B\}$ - $\text{MCS}_2 = \{C, D\}$ - $\text{MCS}_3 = \{E\}$ ### Step 3: Calculate Individual MCS Probabilities $$\begin{aligned} P(\text{MCS}_1) &= P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0.01 \times 0.02 = 0.0002 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \\ P(\text{MCS}_2) &= P(C) \cdot P(D) = 0.005 \times 0.008 = 0.00004 = 4 \times 10^{-5} \\ P(\text{MCS}_3) &= P(E) = 0.001 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4: Apply the OR Combination Formula (Exact) $$P(T) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{3} \left[1 - P(\text{MCS}_i)\right]$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(T) &= 1 - \big[1 - 2\times10^{-4}\big] \cdot \big[1 - 4\times10^{-5}\big] \cdot \big[1 - 1\times10^{-3}\big] \\ &= 1 - (0.9998)(0.99996)(0.999) \\ &= 1 - 0.99876 \\ &= 0.00124 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5: Verification using the Rare-Event Approximation Since each $P(\text{MCS}_i) \ll 1$, we can use the linear sum approximation: $$P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{3} P(\text{MCS}_i) = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 4 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-3} = 1.24 \times 10^{-3}$$ This matches the exact result to within **0.01% error** — validating the rare-event approximation. ### Step 6: Engineering Interpretation $$\boxed{P(T) \approx 1.24 \times 10^{-3} = 0.124\% \text{ per demand}}$$ > **Conclusion:** The single-event MCS ($\{E\}$) **dominates** the top-event probability by an order of magnitude. Safety redesign should prioritize reducing $P(E)$ — for example, by adding redundancy or improving inspection frequency. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: k-out-of-n Voting System Probability (2-out-of-3) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** > A reactor trip system uses 3 redundant sensors. The system trips (Top Event) if **at least 2 of 3** sensors fail. Each sensor has an unavailability $Q = 0.01$. Find the probability of spurious trip. ### Step 1: Define the Top Event $$T = \text{At least 2 out of 3 sensors fail}$$ ### Step 2: Identify Possible Failure Combinations There are $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ ways to have exactly 2 failures, and $\binom{3}{3} = 1$ way to have all 3 failures. $$P(T) = P(\text{exactly 2 fail}) + P(\text{exactly 3 fail})$$ ### Step 3: Calculate "Exactly 2 Fail" Probability $$\begin{aligned} P(\text{exactly 2 fail}) &= \binom{3}{2} \cdot Q^2 \cdot (1-Q)^{1} \\ &= 3 \times (0.01)^2 \times (0.99)^1 \\ &= 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.99 \\ &= 2.97 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4: Calculate "Exactly 3 Fail" Probability $$\begin{aligned} P(\text{exactly 3 fail}) &= \binom{3}{3} \cdot Q^3 \cdot (1-Q)^{0} \\ &= 1 \times (0.01)^3 \times 1 \\ &= 1 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5: Sum the Probabilities $$P(T) = 2.97 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 2.98 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3 \times 10^{-4}$$ ### Step 6: Engineering Insight A 2-out-of-3 voting system reduces the trip probability from $Q = 0.01$ (single sensor) to $P(T) \approx 3 \times 10^{-4}$ — a **33-fold improvement** at the cost of 2 extra sensors. This is the quantitative justification for the **1002, 2003, 1002D** voting architectures in IEC 61508/61511. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Boolean Algebraic Simplification of a Complex Fault Tree > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** > Given a fault tree with structure: $T = AB + A\bar{B}C + AC + BC$. Simplify and identify minimal cut sets. ### Step 1: Write the Original Expression $$T = A \cdot B + A \cdot \bar{B} \cdot C + A \cdot C + B \cdot C$$ ### Step 2: Group and Factor $$\begin{aligned} T &= AB + AC(\bar{B} + 1) + BC \\ &= AB + AC(1) + BC \quad \text{(since } \bar{B} + 1 = 1\text{)} \\ &= AB + AC + BC \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3: Check for Absorption Note that $AC$ is absorbed by $A(B+C)$ term — but $AB + AC + BC$ is the canonical **symmetric form** and cannot be reduced further. ### Step 4: Verify the Minimal Cut Sets The Minimal Cut Sets are: $\{A, B\}$, $\{A, C\}$, $\{B, C\}$ — three second-order cut sets. ### Step 5: Probability Calculation (Using Rare-Event Approximation) $$\begin{aligned} P(T) &\approx P(A)P(B) + P(A)P(C) + P(B)P(C) - \text{overlaps} \\ &\approx P(A)P(B) + P(A)P(C) + P(B)P(C) \quad \text{(negligible overlaps)} \end{aligned}$$ If $P(A) = P(B) = P(C) = 0.1$: $$P(T) \approx 3 \times (0.1)(0.1) = 3 \times 0.01 = 0.03 = 3\%$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Method | Best For | Limitation | Computational Cost | |:-------|:---------|:-----------|:-------------------| | **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Deductive analysis (cause → effect) | Static; difficult for dynamic systems | Medium | | **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** | Inductive analysis (event → outcomes) | Requires pre-defined initiating event | Low | | **Boolean Truth Table** | Small systems (≤ 6 events) | Exponential growth (2ⁿ rows) | High for large n | | **Minimal Cut Set Method** | Identifying weak links | Loses temporal information | Medium | | **Monte Carlo Simulation** | Complex dynamic systems | Requires probabilistic input data | High (computational) | | **Bayesian Networks** | Systems with conditional dependencies | Needs prior probability data | High | --- # 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Standard Logic Gate Symbol Set (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **standard logic gate notation** used in formal safety logic diagrams (per IEC 61025 — Fault Tree Analysis standard). ```mermaid flowchart TD A["INPUT EVENT A
Basic Event 1"]:::basic B["INPUT EVENT B
Basic Event 2"]:::basic C["INPUT EVENT C
Basic Event 3"]:::basic D["INPUT EVENT D
Basic Event 4"]:::basic AND1["AND GATE
Output Y1 = A AND B"]:::andgate OR1["OR GATE
Output Y2 = C OR D"]:::orgate FINAL["TOP EVENT T
Y1 OR Y2"]:::topevent A --> AND1 B --> AND1 C --> OR1 D --> OR1 AND1 --> FINAL OR1 --> FINAL classDef basic fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef andgate fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef orgate fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef topevent fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Blue circles = Basic Events** (lowest-level failures, no further decomposition). - **Green gate = AND** (requires both A and B to fail). - **Orange gate = OR** (requires either C or D to fail). - **Red diamond = Top Event** (the undesired outcome being analyzed). --- ## 4.2 Complete Fault Tree for a Pressure Vessel Rupture Scenario ```mermaid flowchart TD TOP["TOP EVENT T
PRESSURE VESSEL RUPTURE"]:::topevent OVER["G1: OVERPRESSURE FAILURE"]:::andgate CORR["G2: WALL THINNING"]:::orgate MECH["G3: MECHANICAL DAMAGE"]:::orgate OP1["High Inlet Flow"]:::basic OP2["PSV Fails to Open"]:::basic OP3["Operator Fails to Respond"]:::basic C1["Internal Corrosion"]:::basic C2["External Corrosion"]:::basic C3["Erosion"]:::basic M1["Impact Load"]:::basic M2["Vibration Fatigue"]:::basic M3["Thermal Stress"]:::basic TOP --> OVER TOP --> CORR TOP --> MECH OVER --> OP1 OVER --> OP2 OVER --> OP3 CORR --> C1 CORR --> C2 CORR --> C3 MECH --> M1 MECH --> M2 MECH --> M3 classDef basic fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef andgate fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef orgate fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef topevent fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 ``` **Engineering Reading:** - **Top Event (Red):** Pressure vessel rupture. - **OR Gate (Orange G2, G3):** Vessel can rupture from wall thinning OR mechanical damage OR overpressure. - **AND Gate (Green G1):** Overpressure failure requires THREE independent failures to align: high flow **AND** pressure safety valve (PSV) stuck **AND** operator fails to respond. --- ## 4.3 Transformation: Fault Tree ↔ Success Tree (De Morgan's Duality) The block-level functional architecture below shows how a **Fault Tree (failure logic)** and a **Success Tree (system success logic)** are related through De Morgan's theorem. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FAULT_TREE["FAULT TREE - FAILURE LOGIC"] FT_TOP["Top Event T - SYSTEM FAILS"]:::topevent FT_AND["AND Gate
All Inputs Must Fail"]:::andgate FT_A["Event A fails"]:::basic FT_B["Event B fails"]:::basic FT_C["Event C fails"]:::basic FT_TOP --> FT_AND FT_A --> FT_AND FT_B --> FT_AND FT_C --> FT_AND end subgraph SUCCESS_TREE["SUCCESS TREE - SYSTEM WORKS"] ST_TOP["Top Event S - SYSTEM SUCCESS"]:::success ST_OR["OR Gate
Any Path Succeeds"]:::orgate ST_AN["Event A works NOT A"]:::basic ST_BN["Event B works NOT B"]:::basic ST_CN["Event C works NOT C"]:::basic ST_TOP --> ST_OR ST_AN --> ST_OR ST_BN --> ST_OR ST_CN --> ST_OR end TRANSFORM["DE MORGANS THEOREM
S = NOT T
NOT A AND B = NOT A OR NOT B"]:::transform FAULT_TREE -.->|DUAL TRANSFORM| TRANSFORM TRANSFORM -.->|EQUIVALENT| SUCCESS_TREE classDef basic fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef andgate fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef orgate fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef topevent fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 classDef success fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 classDef transform fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` **Key Insight:** The success tree is the **Boolean complement** of the fault tree. An AND gate in the fault tree becomes an **OR gate** in the success tree, and vice versa — this is De Morgan's theorem in action. --- # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A consists of direct short-answer questions testing definitions, symbols, and basic principles. Answers should be **concise (3–5 lines) with diagrams where applicable**. --- ### ✏️ Question A1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define a Logic Diagram in the context of safety engineering. List any three logic gates used in Fault Tree Analysis with their symbolic representation.** **Model Answer:** > A **Logic Diagram** is a graphical deductive representation that uses standardized Boolean logic symbols to depict the combinations of basic events (failures, errors, conditions) that can lead to a defined undesired **Top Event** in a system. It forms the foundation of Fault Tree Analysis (FTA). > > Three standard logic gates used: > > 1. **AND Gate** — Output occurs only if **ALL** input events occur. Symbol: ⊓ (flat-bottomed shield). > 2. **OR Gate** — Output occurs if **ANY** input event occurs. Symbol: ⊔ (shield with curved bottom). > 3. **NOT / Inhibit Gate** — Output occurs if the input occurs **AND** an enabling condition is satisfied. Symbol: Diamond with input bar. > > (1 mark for definition + 1 mark for symbol explanation + 1 mark for engineering relevance) --- ### ✏️ Question A2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Distinguish between a Minimal Cut Set (MCS) and a Minimal Path Set (MPS) in a fault tree. Why are MCSs critical to safety engineering?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Minimal Cut Set (MCS) | Minimal Path Set (MPS) | |:-------|:----------------------|:------------------------| | Definition | Smallest set of basic events whose **occurrence** causes Top Event | Smallest set of basic events whose **non-occurrence** prevents Top Event | | Boolean perspective | Conjunctive failure | Disjunctive success | | Engineering use | Identifies **weakest links** of system | Identifies **strongest defenses** | | Example (3-pump system) | Any 2 pumps fail → system fails | All 3 pumps working → system succeeds | > **Why MCSs are critical:** They identify the **fewest components** whose failure can cause a system accident. Safety investments (redundancy, inspection, better maintenance) are prioritized to **break** these cut sets. A 1st-order MCS (single component failure) is the most dangerous and demands immediate redesign per **ALARP** principles. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Full derivation, diagrams, and engineering interpretation are expected. --- ### 📝 Question B1 — OPTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Construct the fault tree for a "Fire in a Chemical Storage Warehouse" given the following causes: - Event A: Flammable vapor release - Event B: Presence of an ignition source - Event C: Failure of fire suppression system - Event D: Failure of ventilation system - Event E: Human error (improper handling) State the Boolean expression and identify all minimal cut sets. **(b) [7 Marks]** If $P(A) = 0.05$, $P(B) = 0.10$, $P(C) = 0.02$, $P(D) = 0.03$, $P(E) = 0.08$, calculate the probability of the fire event assuming rare-event approximation. --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR B1(a) **Step 1: Build the Fault Tree Structure (Graphical)** ```mermaid flowchart TD TOP["TOP EVENT T
FIRE IN WAREHOUSE"]:::topevent G1["G1: IGNITION EVENT
AND GATE"]:::andgate G2["G2: SUPPRESSION FAILURE
OR GATE"]:::orgate A["A: Flammable Vapor"]:::basic B["B: Ignition Source"]:::basic E["E: Human Error"]:::basic C["C: Sprinkler Fails"]:::basic D["D: Ventilation Fails"]:::basic TOP --> G1 TOP --> G2 A --> G1 B --> G1 E --> G2 C --> G2 D --> G2 classDef basic fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef andgate fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef orgate fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef topevent fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 ``` **Step 2: Write the Boolean Expression** $$T = (A \cdot B) + C + D + E$$ **Step 3: Identify Minimal Cut Sets** - $\text{MCS}_1 = \{A, B\}$ (2nd-order — both A and B must occur) - $\text{MCS}_2 = \{C\}$ (1st-order — sprinkler failure alone) - $\text{MCS}_3 = \{D\}$ (1st-order — ventilation failure alone) - $\text{MCS}_4 = \{E\}$ (1st-order — human error alone) > **Critical Safety Note:** The presence of three **1st-order cut sets** ($\{C\}, \{D\}, \{E\}$) means the system is **highly vulnerable** — a single failure of the sprinkler, ventilation, or human action can cause the top event. This violates good safety design practice (defense-in-depth). --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR B1(b) **Step 1: Compute Probability of Each MCS** $$\begin{aligned} P(\text{MCS}_1) &= P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0.05 \times 0.10 = 0.005 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \\ P(\text{MCS}_2) &= P(C) = 0.02 = 2 \times 10^{-2} \\ P(\text{MCS}_3) &= P(D) = 0.03 = 3 \times 10^{-2} \\ P(\text{MCS}_4) &= P(E) = 0.08 = 8 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Apply Rare-Event Approximation (Linear Sum)** $$P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{4} P(\text{MCS}_i)$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(T) &\approx 0.005 + 0.02 + 0.03 + 0.08 \\ P(T) &\approx 0.135 = 13.5\% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Interpretation** The Top Event probability of **13.5%** is **alarmingly high** for an industrial facility. The dominant contribution comes from human error (8%) and ventilation failure (3%). The recommended actions are: - **Automate the ventilation system** (reduce $P(D)$ by 90% → drops $P(T)$ to 10.7%). - **Improve operator training and use barcode-checked handling** (reduce $P(E)$ by 50% → drops $P(T)$ to 9.5%). - **Add redundant sprinklers** to convert 1st-order cut set $\{C\}$ into 2nd-order, dramatically reducing contribution. --- ### 📝 Question B2 — OPTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **k-out-of-n voting gate** in fault tree analysis. A chemical reactor protection system has **3 temperature sensors**, and the system trips if **at least 2 of 3** sensors indicate high temperature. Each sensor has an unavailability $Q = 0.005$. Derive the formula and calculate the probability of a successful trip (Top Event). **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the application of **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** in safety engineering, with one industry example for each. --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR B2(a) **Step 1: Definition of k-out-of-n Voting Gate** A **k-out-of-n voting gate** represents a system where the Top Event occurs if and only if **at least k out of n** identical components (sensors, valves, pumps) experience the triggering event. It is the mathematical representation of redundant safety systems used in **IEC 61508 SIL-rated** instrumented systems. Common configurations: **1oo2** (1 out of 2), **2oo3** (2 out of 3), **1oo2D** (1 out of 2 with diagnostics). **Step 2: Derive the Probability Formula** For **2-out-of-3 voting**, the Top Event (trip) occurs if: - Exactly 2 sensors trigger, OR - Exactly 3 sensors trigger. $$\begin{aligned} P(T) &= P(\text{exactly 2 of 3}) + P(\text{exactly 3 of 3}) \\ &= \binom{3}{2} \cdot Q^2 \cdot (1-Q)^{1} + \binom{3}{3} \cdot Q^3 \cdot (1-Q)^{0} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Substitute the Values ($Q = 0.005$)** $$\begin{aligned} P(\text{exactly 2 of 3}) &= 3 \times (0.005)^2 \times (0.995) \\ &= 3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.995 \\ &= 7.4625 \times 10^{-5} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(\text{exactly 3 of 3}) &= 1 \times (0.005)^3 \times 1 \\ &= 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Sum the Probabilities** $$P(T) = 7.4625 \times 10^{-5} + 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \approx 7.475 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 5: Engineering Insight** The 2oo3 voting reduces trip probability from $Q = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ (single sensor) to $P(T) \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ — a **66-fold reliability improvement**. This is why nuclear, petrochemical, and aerospace industries mandate voting architectures for critical safety interlocks. --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR B2(b) | Aspect | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) | Event Tree Analysis (ETA) | |:-------|:---------------------------|:---------------------------| | **Logic direction** | Deductive (cause → effect) | Inductive (event → outcomes) | | **Starting point** | Top Event (undesired outcome) | Initiating Event (loss of containment, etc.) | | **Symbols** | AND, OR, NOT, Voting gates | Binary branches (Success / Failure) | | **Output** | Minimal Cut Sets + Top Event probability | Outcome event tree (sequences) | | **Time dependence** | Static (time-independent) | Can incorporate time delays and operator responses | | **Best suited for** | Single-failure accident scenarios | Multiple-outcome scenarios post-initiation | | **Industry Example 1** | **Nuclear:** WASH-1400 Reactor Safety Study (1975) | **Nuclear:** WASH-1400 also used ETA for release categories | | **Industry Example 2** | **Chemical:** Dow Chemical used FTA to analyze runaway reaction probability | **Aviation:** Used to model post-engine-failure landing outcomes | > **Key Synthesis:** FTA answers "**How** can the accident happen?" while ETA answers "**What** happens after the initiating event?". Modern Probabilistic Safety Assessments (PSA) use **both in tandem** — ETA to scope the scenarios, then FTA to quantify each branch. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **MOST COMMON MARK-LOSS ZONES — READ CAREFULLY!** > > 1. **Forgetting the AND/OR probability rules in product form:** Students often write $P(A+B) = P(A) + P(B) + P(A)P(B)$ for OR gates — this is **WRONG**. The correct form is $P(A+B) = 1 - (1-P(A))(1-P(B))$. > > 2. **Confusing cut sets with path sets:** A *cut set* triggers failure; a *path set* maintains success. Mixing these up costs **2-3 marks** instantly. > > 3. **Skipping the diagram:** KTU examiners award **at least 1–2 marks** for a well-labeled fault tree diagram. Always draw the tree first, then derive. > > 4. **Using rare-event approximation without stating the assumption:** Always write "**Since all $P_i \ll 1$, we use the rare-event approximation**" before applying the linear sum. > > 5. **Forgetting minimality in MCS:** $\{A, B, C\}$ is **not** minimal if removing any one still gives a valid cut set. Check all subsets! > > 6. **Wrong Boolean expansion:** Apply distributive law **carefully**: $A + BC \neq AB + AC$ unless $A$ appears as a common factor on **both** terms. > > 7. **No units / context in final answer:** End with an **engineering interpretation** ("This probability is unacceptable for a public-facing facility" or "ALARP requires reducing this by 10×") — this typically earns the last **1 mark** for application. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **SAVE THIS SECTION FOR LAST-MINUTE REVISION — IT IS YOUR EXAM SURVIVAL SHEET.** ### 🔑 Core Definitions - [ ] Logic Diagram = Graphical Boolean representation of causal pathways to a Top Event. - [ ] Top Event = The undesired outcome (accident, failure) being analyzed. - [ ] Basic Event = Lowest-level failure with no further decomposition. - [ ] Minimal Cut Set (MCS) = Smallest event combination that **triggers** Top Event. - [ ] Minimal Path Set (MPS) = Smallest event combination whose **non-occurrence** prevents Top Event. ### 🔑 Logic Gates Mastery - [ ] **AND Gate** → $P_{\text{output}} = \prod P_i$ (product rule for independent events). - [ ] **OR Gate** → $P_{\text{output}} = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$ (complement rule). - [ ] **NOT Gate** → Used with conditional input (inhibit gate). - [ ] **k-out-of-n Gate** → Binomial sum from $k$ to $n$. ### 🔑 Boolean Algebra Must-Know Laws - [ ] De Morgan's: $\overline{A \cdot B} = \bar{A} + \bar{B}$ - [ ] Absorption: $A + AB = A$ - [ ] Distributive: $A + BC = (A+B)(A+C)$ - [ ] Complement: $A + \bar{A} = 1$, $A \cdot \bar{A} = 0$ ### 🔑 Quantitative Formulas - [ ] $P_T \approx \sum P(\text{MCS}_i)$ for rare events. - [ ] $Q(t) \approx \lambda t$ for small $t$. - [ ] $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ - [ ] $P(T)_{2oo3} = 3Q^2(1-Q) + Q^3$ ### 🔑 Engineering Wisdom - [ ] 1st-order MCS = **MOST CRITICAL** — redesign immediately. - [ ] 2nd-order MCS = Add redundancy to break the cut set. - [ ] AND gates → system is *protected* (product of failures). - [ ] OR gates → system is *vulnerable* (any failure triggers top event). - [ ] Redundancy + voting = **defense in depth** (IEC 61508 philosophy). ### 🔑 Key Standards - [ ] **IEC 61025** → Fault Tree Analysis standard. - [ ] **IEC 61508** → Functional Safety of E/E/PE systems. - [ ] **ISO 12100** → Safety of machinery — risk assessment. - [ ] **OSHA 29 CFR 1910** → Process Safety Management. ### 🔑 Always Remember - [ ] **Draw the diagram FIRST** (1-2 marks reserved). - [ ] **State the Boolean expression** before computing. - [ ] **Show all derivation steps** — no skipping! - [ ] **End with engineering interpretation** for the final application mark. ---

topics.title: Input Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Input Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Safety Engineering and Management**, **Input Requirements** refer to the systematic collection of qualitative and quantitative data, parameters, specifications, and boundary conditions that must be gathered, validated, and organized **before** initiating any safety analysis, risk assessment, hazard identification, or safety management system (SMS) design activity.

These inputs form the **foundational information layer** upon which all subsequent safety decisions, design choices, and risk control measures are predicated. The integrity of the final safety output (risk level, safety recommendation, design safeguard) is directly proportional to the **completeness, accuracy, and relevance** of the input requirements gathered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** As per the OESFT831 Module 1 framework, Input Requirements encompass all preliminary data needed to define the system under analysis (SUA), establish operational envelopes, identify credible hazards, and align with applicable regulatory and corporate safety standards.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Input Requirements** as the **ingredients list and recipe measurements** before baking a cake:

| Baking Analogy | Safety Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Recipe (cake type, servings) | System description & scope definition |
| Ingredients (flour, sugar, eggs) | Process data, material properties, equipment specs |
| Oven temperature & time | Operating conditions, environmental parameters |
| Dietary restrictions (allergens) | Regulatory constraints & corporate safety standards |
| Past baking failures | Historical incident/accident data |
| Baker's experience | Expert judgment & human factors data |
| Kitchen safety rules | Emergency response & procedural safeguards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Just as a baker cannot produce a quality cake with missing or spoiled ingredients, a safety engineer cannot produce a credible risk assessment without complete, verified Input Requirements.**

## Classification of Input Requirements

Input Requirements in safety engineering are broadly classified into **seven (7) primary categories**:

1. **System/Process Definition Data** — What is the system, its boundaries, and functions?
2. **Physical & Chemical Property Data** — Material hazards, energy sources, toxicity data.
3. **Operating & Environmental Parameters** — Temperature, pressure, flow rates, ambient conditions.
4. **Failure & Reliability Data** — Component failure rates, Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).
5. **Human Factors & Ergonomic Data** — Human error probabilities, shift patterns, training levels.
6. **Regulatory & Standards-Based Data** — Applicable codes (OSHA, NFPA, IS, IEC, API).
7. **Historical & Empirical Data** — Past incident reports, near-miss records, lessons learned.

> [!TIP]
> Each input category contributes a unique "dimension" to the safety picture. Omitting any one category creates blind spots that may lead to catastrophic underestimation of risk.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Input Requirements Hierarchy (IRH)

The **Input Requirements Hierarchy (IRH)** is a structured framework used in safety engineering to organize inputs in a logical, cascading order — from broad system context down to specific numerical parameters.

### Hierarchical Structure (Top-Down Flow)

- **Level 1 — Strategic Inputs**
  - Corporate safety policy
  - Regulatory jurisdiction
  - Project scope & objectives
  - Stakeholder requirements
- **Level 2 — System Definition Inputs**
  - Process Flow Diagrams (PFD)
  - Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&ID)
  - Block Flow Diagrams (BFD)
  - System boundaries & interfaces
  - Equipment lists & specifications
- **Level 3 — Operational Inputs**
  - Normal operating parameters (T, P, flow, level)
  - Startup/shutdown sequences
  - Maintenance procedures
  - Emergency operating procedures
- **Level 4 — Hazard & Failure Inputs**
  - Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS/SDS)
  - Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) data
  - Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) event libraries
  - Component failure rate databases (e.g., OREDA, IEEE Std 500)
- **Level 5 — Human & Organizational Inputs**
  - Human error probabilities (HEP)
  - Training and competency records
  - Shift scheduling & workload data
  - Safety culture assessments
- **Level 6 — Environmental & External Inputs**
  - Ambient weather/climate data
  - Seismic, flood, or geotechnical data
  - Adjacent facility interactions
  - Public/societal risk acceptance criteria
- **Level 7 — Verification & Validation Inputs**
  - Inspection & testing records
  - Audit findings
  - Near-miss reporting logs
  - Post-incident investigation reports

## The "GAR" Quality Model for Inputs

Every input requirement must satisfy the **GAR Triad** for it to be considered reliable:

- **G — Genuine**: The data originates from a verified, primary source (manufacturer datasheet, regulatory database, direct measurement).
- **A — Accurate**: The data is within acceptable error bounds (typically $\pm 5\%$ for engineering calculations).
- **R — Relevant**: The data directly applies to the system under analysis (contextually appropriate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If any input fails the **GAR** test, it must be flagged, supplemented with conservative assumptions, or replaced. Never use unverified data in final safety decisions.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basic Event Probability (FMEA) | $P(A) = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot t}{1 + \lambda \cdot t}$ | Probability of failure within time $t$ where $\lambda$ is failure rate | dimensionless |
| Failure Rate (Exponential Distribution) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Constant failure rate assuming exponential distribution | failures/hour |
| Human Error Probability (HEP) | $HEP = \dfrac{\text{Number of errors}}{\text{Number of opportunities}}$ | Ratio-based human reliability metric | dimensionless |
| Combined Failure Rate (Series) | $\lambda_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Total failure rate for $n$ components in series | failures/hour |
| Combined Failure Rate (Parallel, identical) | $\lambda_{parallel} = \dfrac{2\lambda^2}{\mu} \cdot t$ | For 2 identical parallel components, with repair rate $\mu$ | failures/hour |
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System availability over duty cycle | dimensionless (0–1) |
| Risk Score (Basic) | $R = P \times C$ | Risk = Probability $\times$ Consequence | risk units |
| Consequence Severity (Multi-factor) | $C = f(\text{health}, \text{env}, \text{property}, \text{reputation})$ | Composite consequence function | composite index |
| Risk Acceptance Threshold | $R_{actual} \leq R_{acceptable}$ | As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP) gate | inequality |
| Data Confidence Factor | $DCF = \dfrac{N_{verified}}{N_{total}}$ | Ratio of verified data points to total inputs | dimensionless (0–1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical bar substitution:** For absolute value expressions in calculations, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ notation rather than the pipe symbol to avoid markdown table conflicts.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

Input Requirements drive every major safety engineering deliverable:

- **Process Safety:** Feed into HAZOP, LOPA, QRA for chemical/petrochemical plants.
- **Occupational Safety:** Inform Job Safety Analysis (JSA) and Permit-to-Work systems.
- **Functional Safety:** Provide basis for Safety Instrumented System (SIS) design per IEC 61511.
- **Machine Safety:** Support risk assessment per ISO 12100 and EN ISO 13849.
- **Construction Safety:** Enable Site-Specific Safety Plans and method statements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Failure Probability from Failure Rate

**Given:** A component with constant failure rate $\lambda$ (failures per hour) operating for a continuous time interval $t$ (hours).

**Assumption:** The exponential reliability model is applicable, which is standard for electronic and many mechanical components with random (memoryless) failure behavior.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

We begin with the reliability function $R(t)$, defined as the probability that the component survives (does not fail) up to time $t$. Under the exponential distribution assumption:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

The probability of failure $P_f(t)$ is the complement of reliability. Since a component either survives or fails (exhaustive, mutually exclusive events summing to 1):

$$
P_f(t) = 1 - R(t)
$$

Substituting the exponential reliability expression into the complement equation:

$$
P_f(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

This is the **exact** form. Now, for small values of the product $\lambda t$ (typically when $\lambda t \ll 1$, which is the case for low-failure-rate components over short intervals), we can apply the **Taylor series expansion** of the exponential function:

$$
e^{-\lambda t} = 1 - \lambda t + \dfrac{(\lambda t)^2}{2!} - \dfrac{(\lambda t)^3}{3!} + \cdots
$$

Truncating after the first-order term (valid when higher-order terms become negligibly small):

$$
e^{-\lambda t} \approx 1 - \lambda t \quad \text{for } \lambda t \ll 1
$$

Substituting this approximation back into the failure probability expression:

$$
P_f(t) = 1 - (1 - \lambda t)
$$

Simplifying algebraically by removing the parentheses:

$$
P_f(t) = 1 - 1 + \lambda t
$$

Canceling the 1's to obtain the final working formula:

$$
P_f(t) = \lambda t \quad \text{(for } \lambda t \ll 1\text{)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic of the last step:** The first "1" represents the total probability space, the subtracted "1" represents the approximation of the exponential term, and their difference yields the first-order surviving probability perturbation, which equals the failure probability directly.

## Derivation 2: System Failure Rate for Components in Series

**Given:** A safety system comprising $n$ independent components connected in **series** such that the failure of any single component causes system failure (e.g., a triple-redundant sensor chain where any one sensor loss is detected but the channel is degraded).

The reliability of $n$ independent components in series is the product of their individual reliabilities (probability multiplication rule for independent events):

$$
R_{sys}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}
$$

Combining the exponentials using the rule $e^a \cdot e^b = e^{a+b}$:

$$
R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t}
$$

Comparing this to the standard form $R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda_{sys} t}$, we identify the equivalent system failure rate by matching exponents:

$$
\lambda_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** This derivation demonstrates that series-connected components **add** their failure rates. Adding more components always increases system failure rate, reducing overall reliability — a fundamental principle in safety-instrumented system design that motivates redundancy.

## Derivation 3: Data Confidence Factor (DCF) for Input Validation

**Given:** A safety study team has collected $N_{total}$ input data points, of which $N_{verified}$ have been independently cross-checked against primary sources.

The Data Confidence Factor is defined as the ratio:

$$
DCF = \dfrac{N_{verified}}{N_{total}}
$$

**Worked example with full calculation:**

Suppose $N_{total} = 80$ input requirements, and $N_{verified} = 60$ have been validated. Compute the DCF:

$$
DCF = \dfrac{60}{80}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator:

$$
DCF = \dfrac{6}{8} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

Expressing as a percentage for engineering communication:

$$
DCF = 0.75 \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A DCF of 75% means 25% of inputs are unverified. Per KTU 2024 scheme best practice, a DCF below **80%** triggers a mandatory data-gathering review and documentation of conservative assumptions for the unverified portion.

## Analytical Matrix: Input Requirement Quality vs. Risk Outcome

| **Input Quality Level** | **DCF Range** | **Resulting Risk Assessment Reliability** | **Required Action** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Excellent | $\geq 95\%$ | High confidence; defensible in regulatory audit | Proceed with standard analysis |
| Good | 85% – 94% | Acceptable for most studies | Document sources; minor assumptions |
| Adequate | 80% – 84% | Marginal; needs sensitivity analysis | Add engineering judgment notes |
| Poor | 60% – 79% | Low confidence; results may be challenged | Conduct supplementary data collection |
| Unacceptable | $\leq 59\%$ | Unreliable; cannot be used for safety decisions | Reject study; restart data gathering |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram A: Input Requirements Hierarchy (IRH) Flow

This block diagram maps the cascading information flow from strategic context down to operational and verification inputs in a typical safety engineering project.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Strategic Inputs"] --> B["System Definition Inputs"]
    B --> C["Operational Inputs"]
    C --> D["Hazard and Failure Inputs"]
    D --> E["Human and Organizational Inputs"]
    E --> F["Environmental and External Inputs"]
    F --> G["Verification and Validation Inputs"]
    G --> H["Approved Safety Analysis Basis"]
    A --> A1["Corporate Policy"]
    A --> A2["Regulatory Jurisdiction"]
    A --> A3["Project Scope"]
    B --> B1["PFD and P and ID"]
    B --> B2["Equipment List"]
    B --> B3["System Boundaries"]
    C --> C1["Operating Parameters"]
    C --> C2["Startup Shutdown"]
    C --> C3["Maintenance Procedures"]
    D --> D1["MSDS Sheets"]
    D --> D2["FMEA Data"]
    D --> D3["FTA Event Library"]
    E --> E1["HEP Data"]
    E --> E2["Training Records"]
    F --> F1["Weather Climate Data"]
    F --> F2["Seismic Flood Data"]
    G --> G1["Inspection Records"]
    G --> G2["Near Miss Logs"]
```

## Diagram B: The GAR Quality Filter Process

This diagram illustrates how the three quality gates (Genuine, Accurate, Relevant) filter raw inputs into a refined, usable safety data set.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Raw Input Data"] --> Q1{"Genuine Check"}
    Q1 -->|Pass| Q2{"Accurate Check"}
    Q1 -->|Fail| R1["Reject or Flag Source"]
    Q2 -->|Pass| Q3{"Relevant Check"}
    Q2 -->|Fail| R2["Apply Tolerance or Reject"]
    Q3 -->|Pass| V["Validated Input Set"]
    Q3 -->|Fail| R3["Replace with Context Appropriate Data"]
    V --> S["Feed Into Safety Analysis"]
```

## Diagram C: Input-to-Output Mapping Matrix

This diagram shows how different input categories feed into the major safety analysis methodologies used in industry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYS["System Definition Data"] --> HAZOP["HAZOP Study"]
    SYS --> FMEA["FMEA Analysis"]
    SYS --> FTA["Fault Tree Analysis"]
    MAT["Material Property Data"] --> HAZOP
    MAT --> QRA["Quantitative Risk Assessment"]
    OPR["Operating Parameters"] --> LOPA["Layer of Protection Analysis"]
    OPR --> SIS["Safety Instrumented System Design"]
    FAIL["Failure Rate Data"] --> FTA
    FAIL --> LOPA
    HF["Human Factors Data"] --> HAZOP
    HF --> LOPA
    REG["Regulatory Standards"] --> SIS
    REG --> QRA
    HIST["Historical Incident Data"] --> HAZOP
    HIST --> QRA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid node naming compliance:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `node1`) and avoid reserved Mermaid keywords. Labels use clean uppercase text only, with no embedded markdown formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Input Requirements in the context of Advanced Safety Engineering. List any four categories. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Input Requirements are the comprehensive set of qualitative and quantitative data, parameters, specifications, and boundary conditions systematically gathered and validated prior to initiating any safety analysis, risk assessment, or safety management system design. They form the foundational information base upon which all safety decisions are predicated.

**Four Categories (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **System/Process Definition Data** — PFDs, P&IDs, equipment lists.
2. **Physical & Chemical Property Data** — MSDS, toxicity, flammability.
3. **Failure & Reliability Data** — Component failure rates, MTBF, OREDA database.
4. **Regulatory & Standards-Based Data** — OSHA, NFPA, IS, IEC, API standards.

### Question 2: What is the GAR Triad for input quality? Explain briefly. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **GAR Triad** is a three-gate quality filter applied to every input requirement before it is accepted into a safety analysis dataset:

- **G — Genuine (1 mark):** The data must originate from a verified, primary source such as a manufacturer datasheet, regulatory database, or direct measurement — not hearsay or unverified secondary sources.
- **A — Accurate (1 mark):** The data must lie within acceptable error bounds, typically $\pm 5\%$ for engineering calculations, and be dimensionally correct.
- **R — Relevant (1 mark):** The data must be contextually appropriate to the system under analysis; data from a different process or scale is unsuitable even if it is genuine and accurate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A):** Students often write vague, generic definitions without naming specific categories or quality criteria. You **must** list distinct categories with examples (e.g., "MSDS for chemical data") to secure full marks. Avoid one-sentence answers.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Input Requirements and Quality Validation

**(a)** Explain in detail the seven categories of Input Requirements used in Advanced Safety Engineering. For each category, give **two examples** of specific data items. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety study team has collected 120 input requirements for a process plant risk assessment. Of these, 96 have been independently verified against primary sources. Calculate the **Data Confidence Factor (DCF)** and comment on the suitability of proceeding with the analysis. If unsuitable, what corrective actions should be taken? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Seven Categories of Input Requirements (7 marks; 1 mark per category)**

1. **System/Process Definition Data (1 mark):** Describes the system under analysis (SUA), its boundaries, and intended functions.
   - *Example 1:* Process Flow Diagram (PFD) showing all major equipment and stream connections.
   - *Example 2:* Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) detailing valves, instruments, and control loops.

2. **Physical & Chemical Property Data (1 mark):** Provides hazard characteristics of materials handled.
   - *Example 1:* Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS/SDS) for a solvent like toluene (flash point, LEL, UEL).
   - *Example 2:* Toxicological data such as LC50 (lethal concentration 50%) and TLV (Threshold Limit Value).

3. **Operating & Environmental Parameters (1 mark):** Defines the normal and abnormal operating envelope.
   - *Example 1:* Design pressure of 25 bar and design temperature of 200°C for a reactor.
   - *Example 2:* Ambient climate data (max ambient temperature = 45°C, relative humidity = 85%).

4. **Failure & Reliability Data (1 mark):** Quantifies component and system reliability.
   - *Example 1:* Failure rate of a centrifugal pump seal: $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour.
   - *Example 2:* MTBF of a control valve: 100,000 hours (from OREDA database).

5. **Human Factors & Ergonomic Data (1 mark):** Accounts for human performance limitations.
   - *Example 1:* Human Error Probability (HEP) for misreading a gauge: HEP = 0.01 (1%).
   - *Example 2:* Operator shift schedule (12-hour shifts, 4-person crew rotation pattern).

6. **Regulatory & Standards-Based Data (1 mark):** Ensures compliance with applicable codes.
   - *Example 1:* OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals).
   - *Example 2:* IEC 61511 (Functional safety — Safety Instrumented Systems for process industries).

7. **Historical & Empirical Data (1 mark):** Provides lessons from past events.
   - *Example 1:* Near-miss incident log from the past 5 years (e.g., 12 recorded valve leaks).
   - *Example 2:* Post-incident investigation report (e.g., CSB report on the BP Texas City refinery, 2005).

**Part (b) — DCF Calculation and Recommendation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $N_{total} = 120$ input requirements, $N_{verified} = 96$ inputs.

**Step 1: Write the DCF formula (1 mark):**

$$
DCF = \dfrac{N_{verified}}{N_{total}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (1 mark):**

$$
DCF = \dfrac{96}{120}
$$

**Step 3: Perform the division (1 mark):**

$$
DCF = 0.80 \quad \text{(or 80%)}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret against the quality matrix (2 marks):**

A DCF of exactly **80%** falls at the **lower boundary of "Adequate"** in the analytical matrix (range 80%–84%). It indicates that **20% of inputs (24 data points) remain unverified**.

**Step 5: Recommend corrective actions (2 marks):**

- Conduct a **supplementary data-gathering review** for the 24 unverified inputs.
- For inputs that cannot be verified, apply **engineering judgment** and document **conservative assumptions** (e.g., upper-bound failure rates, worst-case environmental conditions).
- Perform a **sensitivity analysis** to determine how the unverified inputs influence final risk estimates.
- Reject the study only if critical safety inputs (e.g., material toxicity, explosion limits) remain unverified.

**Final conclusion:** The DCF of 80% is at the **marginal acceptance threshold**; proceeding is conditionally acceptable provided conservative assumptions and sensitivity analysis accompany the results.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Input Requirements Hierarchy and Failure Data

**(a)** Draw and explain the **Input Requirements Hierarchy (IRH)** with all seven levels. For each level, give **one industrial example** of the type of data collected. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure safety valve (PSV) on a reactor has a manufacturer-specified failure rate of $\lambda = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. Calculate the probability of failure over a **6-month inspection interval** (assume 1 month = 720 hours). State whether the failure probability satisfies the typical target of $P_f \leq 10^{-2}$ (1%). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Input Requirements Hierarchy Explanation (7 marks; 1 mark per level)**

The IRH is a structured, top-down framework that organizes safety engineering inputs from strategic context to granular verification data. It ensures that **no critical input category is omitted** during data gathering.

1. **Level 1 — Strategic Inputs (1 mark):** Top-level context defining the project. *Industrial example:* Corporate "Zero Harm" safety policy and ISO 45001 alignment requirements.

2. **Level 2 — System Definition Inputs (1 mark):** Defines the system under analysis. *Industrial example:* Block Flow Diagram (BFD) of a crude oil distillation unit showing feed, product, and intermediate streams.

3. **Level 3 — Operational Inputs (1 mark):** Normal and abnormal operating parameters. *Industrial example:* Reactor operating temperature = 180°C, operating pressure = 15 bar, flow rate = 50 m³/h.

4. **Level 4 — Hazard & Failure Inputs (1 mark):** Material hazards and reliability data. *Industrial example:* FMEA worksheet identifying "pump seal leak" as a failure mode with severity rating S = 4.

5. **Level 5 — Human & Organizational Inputs (1 mark):** Operator performance and training. *Industrial example:* THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) value of 0.003 for correctly closing a manual valve under time pressure.

6. **Level 6 — Environmental & External Inputs (1 mark):** External factors affecting safety. *Industrial example:* Seismic zone factor Z = 0.36 (Zone V, IS 1893) for site location in northern Kerala.

7. **Level 7 — Verification & Validation Inputs (1 mark):** Post-installation feedback data. *Industrial example:* Six-monthly inspection reports showing zero non-conformities on emergency shutdown valves.

**Part (b) — Failure Probability Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**

$$
\lambda = 3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

**Step 1: Convert inspection interval to hours (1 mark):**

$$
t = 6 \text{ months} \times 720 \text{ hours/month}
$$

**Step 2: Multiply (1 mark):**

$$
t = 4320 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: State the appropriate formula (1 mark):**

Since the product $\lambda t$ is expected to be very small (low failure rate, moderate interval), use the first-order approximation:

$$
P_f(t) = \lambda t \quad \text{(valid when } \lambda t \ll 1\text{)}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the product $\lambda t$ (1 mark):**

$$
\lambda t = (3 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4320
$$

Performing the multiplication (separating coefficients and powers of 10):

$$
\lambda t = 3 \times 4320 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the numerical coefficient (1 mark):**

$$
3 \times 4320 = 12960
$$

**Step 6: Final value of $\lambda t$ (1 mark):**

$$
\lambda t = 12960 \times 10^{-6} = 1.296 \times 10^{-2} = 0.01296
$$

**Step 7: Verify approximation validity and interpret (1 mark):**

Since $\lambda t = 0.01296 \ll 1$, the first-order approximation is valid, so:

$$
P_f = 0.01296 \approx 1.30\%
$$

**Comparison with target:** The target is $P_f \leq 1\% = 0.01$.

Since $P_f = 0.01296 > 0.01$, the **target is NOT satisfied**. The inspection interval should be reduced, or a higher-reliability valve selected.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):** Common mark-losing mistakes include:
> 1. Forgetting to convert **months to hours** — this is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. Using the wrong formula $P_f = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ without the first-order approximation when $\lambda t \ll 1$ — acceptable but ensure you show the simplification step.
> 3. **Not commenting on the comparison** with the target threshold — always state whether the criterion is satisfied.
> 4. Skipping units in intermediate steps — always write "failures/hour × hours = dimensionless."

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Input Requirements = Foundation Layer:** Every safety analysis (HAZOP, LOPA, FMEA, FTA, QRA) is only as good as its inputs. GIGO principle applies: **Garbage In, Garbage Out**.
- **Seven Categories to Memorize:** System Definition, Material Data, Operating Parameters, Failure Data, Human Factors, Regulatory Data, Historical Data.
- **GAR Triad = Quality Filter:** Genuine, Accurate, Relevant — apply to every input before acceptance.
- **Input Requirements Hierarchy (IRH):** Seven-level top-down framework; Strategic → System → Operational → Hazard → Human → Environmental → Verification.
- **Key Formulas to Master:**
  - $P_f = \lambda t$ (for $\lambda t \ll 1$)
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (exponential reliability)
  - $\lambda_{sys} = \sum \lambda_i$ (series components)
  - $DCF = N_{verified} / N_{total}$ (input confidence)
  - $R = P \times C$ (basic risk score)
- **DCF Threshold:** Below **80%** triggers mandatory supplementary review and conservative assumptions.
- **Series Reliability:** Adding components **always increases** system failure rate — motivates redundancy and diversity in safety design.
- **ALARP Principle:** All input-driven risk must be reduced to **As Low As Reasonably Practicable** before acceptance.
- **Documentation is Mandatory:** Every input must have a traceable source citation in a Safety Analysis Basis document.
- **Examiner's Hot Topic:** DCF calculations, $\lambda t$ conversions, and IRH categorization are the **most frequently asked** 3-mark and 7-mark sub-questions in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Analysis of Automatic Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reliability Analysis of Automatic Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Reliability** of an automatic control system is formally defined as the *probability that a system, component, or process performs its intended function under stated conditions, for a specified period of time*. In the context of safety engineering, it specifically refers to the dependability of **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** — comprising sensors, logic solvers, and final control elements — to execute their safety functions when demanded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Reliability is the characteristic of an item expressed by the probability that it will perform a required function under stated conditions for a stated period of time. The standard governing this domain in process industries is **IEC 61508** (functional safety) and **IEC 61511** (process industry sector).

### 1.2 Core Reliability Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mean Time To Failure** | **MTTF** | Average time until first failure (non-repairable) | Useful life of sensors, ICs, mechanical parts |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | **MTBF** | Average time between consecutive failures (repairable) | Used for controllers, valves, pumps |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | **MTTR** | Average downtime to restore function | Maintenance crew effectiveness |
| **Failure Rate** | **λ** | Failures per unit time | Spans the **bathtub curve** phases |
| **Availability** | **A** | Fraction of time system is operational | Long-term readiness of plant |
| **Probability of Failure on Demand** | **PFD** | Probability system fails when safety demanded | Core SIL metric |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Three-Layer Shield

Imagine your car's **braking system** as an automatic control loop:
- The **brake pedal sensor** measures your input (like a process sensor measuring pressure).
- The **brake controller (ECU)** decides the braking force (like a logic solver in a Safety PLC).
- The **brake caliper** applies the force to the wheel (like a final control element / shut-down valve).

Now, an *emergency brake assist* (your **Safety Instrumented System**) must work the *one time* you slam the pedal in panic. The *reliability* of that safety chain is what stops the accident. If any of the three layers fails, the system is unreliable. This is the heart of reliability analysis of automatic control systems — quantifying the probability of that chain holding when needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Reliability Matters in Control Systems:** Modern plants have thousands of control loops. A single undetected failure in a level control loop can lead to tank overfill → fire → catastrophe (e.g., Buncefield 2005, Texas City 2005). Reliability analysis quantifies and reduces this risk.

### 1.4 The Reliability Function R(t) — Fundamental Concept

The **reliability function** R(t) gives the probability that a system survives (does not fail) up to time *t*:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where **T** is the random variable representing time-to-failure. Its complement is the **cumulative distribution function** (unreliability):

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = P(T \leq t)$$

The **probability density function** of failure is:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bathtub Curve — Life Cycle of a Control Component

The **failure rate λ(t)** of most engineered control components follows a characteristic **bathtub curve** with three distinct regions:

**Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):**
- Decreasing failure rate due to manufacturing defects, poor solder joints, improper commissioning.
- Mitigation: factory burn-in, factory acceptance tests (FAT), site acceptance tests (SAT).
- Duration: weeks to a few months.

**Region II — Useful Life (Constant Failure Rate):**
- Failure rate is **constant** at λ ≈ λ₀. This is the period where the **exponential distribution** applies and reliability is mathematically tractable.
- Failures are random (memoryless process).
- This region dominates the design life of most safety systems.

**Region III — Wear-out:**
- Increasing failure rate due to corrosion, fatigue, seal degradation, calibration drift.
- Mitigation: preventive replacement, condition-based monitoring, mid-life refurbishment.

> [!NOTE]
> For **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)**, the design life is almost always within Region II, which is why the exponential reliability model dominates SIL calculations.

### 2.2 Exponential Reliability Model — The Workhorse

For a constant failure rate **λ** (Region II), the reliability is:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Derivation Logic:** The probability of surviving an infinitesimally small interval *dt* is (1 − λ·dt). For finite time *t*, this integrates as:
$$R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda t}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Key Property — Memorylessness:**
$$P(T > s+t \mid T > s) = \frac{R(s+t)}{R(s)} = e^{-\lambda t} = R(t)$$

A system that has survived *s* hours has the *same* future reliability as a brand-new system. This is **unique to the exponential distribution**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam critical**. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Name | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential reliability | λ in failures/hour, t in hours |
| 2 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative failure probability | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Failure density | Per unit time |
| 4 | $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean time to failure | Hours |
| 5 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} \approx \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean time between failures | Hours (MTTR ≪ MTTF) |
| 6 | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Steady-state availability | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 7 | $A_{\text{inst}}(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda + \mu)t}$ | Instantaneous availability | λ, μ in /hour |
| 8 | $R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Series system reliability | All must work |
| 9 | $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Parallel / redundant reliability | At least one must work |
| 10 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | Probability of failure on demand | λ_D in /hr, T_proof in hr |
| 11 | $\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ (dangerous undetected) | SIL-component failure rate | Per hour |

> **Notation Note:** λ = failure rate, μ = repair rate (μ = 1/MTTR), λ_D = dangerous failure rate, T_proof = proof test interval.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Safety:** Determining SIL for emergency shutdown (ESD), fire & gas (F&G), and burner management systems (BMS).
- **Avionics:** Reliability block diagrams for flight control systems (e.g., 2-out-of-3 voting on Boeing 777 primary flight computer).
- **Nuclear Industry:** IEC 61508 SIL-3 / SIL-4 compliance for reactor trip systems.
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 (functional safety for road vehicles) — analogous Asil A to D levels.
- **Manufacturing:** MTBF-based preventive maintenance scheduling for PLCs and robotic arms.

### 2.5 Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) — Series vs Parallel Logic

A control loop's **reliability** is modeled as a chain. Two fundamental configurations:

**Series (Worst-case) Configuration:**
All components must work for the system to work. The **weakest link** dictates reliability.

$$R_{\text{system}} = R_{\text{sensor}} \times R_{\text{controller}} \times R_{\text{actuator}} \times R_{\text{power}} \times R_{\text{link}}$$

**Parallel (Redundant) Configuration:**
System works if **at least one** branch works. Dramatically increases reliability but at the cost of complexity and common-cause failures.

$$R_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When two identical components (R each) are in parallel, R_parallel = 2R − R². For low R (≈ 0.01), this gives ≈ 0.02, doubling unreliability reduction; but for high R (≈ 0.99), R_parallel ≈ 0.9999 — a 100× reliability improvement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Exponential Reliability from Constant Hazard

We begin with the definition of the **hazard rate (failure rate) function**:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This rearranges to a first-order separable ODE:

$$-\lambda(t) \cdot dt = \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Integrate both sides from 0 to *t* (using the boundary condition R(0) = 1, i.e., a new system is 100% reliable at start):

$$\int_{0}^{t} -\lambda(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{R(\tau)} \, dR(\tau)$$

**Step 1:** Apply the integration. The right-hand side gives $\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = \ln R(t)$ since $R(0) = 1$ and $\ln 1 = 0$.

**Step 2:** For Region II (constant failure rate), $\lambda(\tau) = \lambda$:

$$-\lambda t = \ln R(t)$$

**Step 3:** Exponentiate both sides to obtain the **canonical reliability function**:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \qquad \blacksquare$$

This single equation is the foundation of every SIL calculation in the world.

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

By definition, the expected value of the time-to-failure random variable T is:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1:** Integrate by parts. Let $u = t$, $dv = f(t)dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = F(t) = 1 - R(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ t \cdot (1 - R(t)) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (1 - R(t)) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the boundary term. At $t \to \infty$, $R \to 0$, so $t(1-R)$ is finite; combined with the rapidly vanishing exponential, this term is **zero** for bounded MTTF. At $t = 0$, the term is $0 \cdot (1-1) = 0$.

**Step 3:** The remaining integral simplifies using $R(\infty) = 0$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the exponential reliability:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Steady-State Availability of a Repairable System

A repairable system has two states: **UP** (operating) and **DOWN** (failed). Transition rates are:
- Failure rate λ: UP → DOWN
- Repair rate μ: DOWN → UP

The **birth–death (Markov) equations** in steady state (dP/dt = 0):

$$\lambda \cdot P_{\text{UP}} = \mu \cdot P_{\text{DOWN}}$$

Combined with the conservation equation $P_{\text{UP}} + P_{\text{DOWN}} = 1$:

**Step 1:** From the balance equation, $P_{\text{DOWN}} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_{\text{UP}}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the conservation equation:

$$P_{\text{UP}} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_{\text{UP}} = 1$$

$$P_{\text{UP}} \left(1 + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right) = 1$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $P_{\text{UP}}$ (the **steady-state availability** A):

$$A = P_{\text{UP}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$ and $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$:

$$A = \frac{1/\text{MTTR}}{1/\text{MTBF} + 1/\text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A pump with MTBF = 10,000 hr and MTTR = 50 hr has $A = 10000/10050 = 0.995$ (99.5% available). A safety-critical system typically requires A > 0.999 (often called "three-nines" availability).

### 3.4 Derivation: PFD for a 1-out-of-1 (1oo1) System

For a single-channel safety system, with dangerous failure rate λ_D and proof test interval $T_{\text{proof}}$, the system is **not detected** as failed between proof tests. The average probability of failure on demand (simplified IEC 61508 formula) is:

**Step 1:** Probability of failure as a function of time since last proof test, assuming dangerous failures occur at rate λ_D:

$$PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t \quad \text{(for } \lambda_D t \ll 1\text{)}$$

**Step 2:** Average this over one proof test cycle (0 to $T_{\text{proof}}$):

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} \lambda_D \, t \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T_{\text{proof}}} \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} = \frac{\lambda_D \, T_{\text{proof}}}{2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Halving the proof test interval $T_{\text{proof}}$ halves the PFD. This is why industries with SIL-3 requirements adopt online diagnostics to reduce $T_{\text{proof}}$ to weeks instead of years.

### 3.5 KTU Numerical-Style Worked Example (Typical 7-mark sub-question)

**Problem:** A control system has three components in series with reliabilities $R_A = 0.95$, $R_B = 0.92$, $R_C = 0.98$ for a mission time of 1000 hours. Calculate:
- (a) System reliability.
- (b) System unreliability (probability of failure).

**Solution:**

**(a)** Series system reliability:
$$R_{\text{sys}} = R_A \times R_B \times R_C = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98$$

**Step 1:** Multiply the first two:
$$0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by the third:
$$R_{\text{sys}} = 0.874 \times 0.98 = 0.85652 \approx 0.857$$

**(b)** System unreliability:
$$F_{\text{sys}} = 1 - R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - 0.857 = 0.143$$

**Conclusion:** The series control system has an 85.7% probability of completing its 1000-hour mission without failure, and a 14.3% probability of failure.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Voting Architectures in SIS

The KTU syllabus emphasizes **voting logic** (M-out-of-N). For **N** identical channels each with reliability R:

| Architecture | Notation | Reliability Formula | PFD Formula (simplified) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1-out-of-1 | 1oo1 | $R$ | $\frac{\lambda_D T_{proof}}{2}$ |
| 1-out-of-2 | 1oo2 | $2R - R^2$ | $\frac{(\lambda_D T_{proof})^2}{3}$ |
| 2-out-of-2 | 2oo2 | $R^2$ | $\lambda_D T_{proof}$ |
| 2-out-of-3 | 2oo3 | $3R^2 - 2R^3$ | $\frac{(\lambda_D T_{proof})^2}{3}$ (duplex) |
| 1-out-of-3 | 1oo3 | $3R - 3R^2 + R^3$ | $\frac{(\lambda_D T_{proof})^3}{4}$ (triplex) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why 2oo3 is industry-preferred:** It tolerates one failed channel (still operates safely with the second vote) AND detects any single failure (voting mismatch triggers alarm). This is called **fault tolerance = 1** with **diagnostic coverage = high**.

### 3.7 SIL Determination (Per IEC 61508)

| SIL Level | PFD_avg Required | RRF Required | Risk Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | Low |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1,000 | Medium |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 | High |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 | Very high |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Automatic Control Loop with SIS Overlay

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    A1[Process Variable] --> B1[Sensor]
    B1 --> C1[Controller / Logic Solver]
    C1 --> D1[Final Control Element]
    D1 --> E1[Process Actuator]
    E1 --> A1

    S1[Safety Demand Event] --> SA[SIS Sensor]
    SA --> SB[SIS Logic Solver]
    SB --> SC[SIS Final Element]
    SC --> SD[Safe State Achieved]

    S1 -.Triggers.-> SA

    classDef basic fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    class A1,B1,C1,D1,E1 basic
    class S1,SA,SB,SC,SD safety
<!-- mermaid -->

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: Two-State Markov Model (Repairable System)

<!-- mermaid -->
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    state UPstate as UP Operating
    state DOWNstate as DOWN Failed
    UPstate --> DOWNstate : Failure event rate lambda
    DOWNstate --> UPstate : Repair event rate mu
    note right of UPstate : P UP equals steady state availability A
    note right of DOWNstate : P DOWN equals 1 minus A
<!-- mermaid -->

### 4.3 Mermaid Reliability Block Diagram: Series vs Parallel

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TB
    subgraph SERIES[Series System All must work]
        direction LR
        sA[Component A] --> sB[Component B] --> sC[Component C]
    end

    subgraph PARALLEL[Parallel System At least one must work]
        direction LR
        pA[Component A] --> pJ[OR Junction]
        pB[Component B] --> pJ
        pC[Component C] --> pJ
    end

    classDef seriesNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef parallelNode fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000
