## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the term 'reliability' of an automatic control system. State the standard formula for exponential reliability and identify its assumption.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Reliability is the probability that a system, component, or device will perform its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, where λ is the (constant) failure rate per unit time.
- **Key Assumption (1 mark):** The system is in its **useful life (Region II of the bathtub curve)**, where the failure rate is **constant** and the system is **memoryless**.

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Distinguish between MTBF and MTTF. Why is MTTR often neglected in MTBF calculations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MTBF (1 mark):** Mean Time Between Failures — applicable to **repairable** systems; it includes both operational time and the average repair interval.
- **MTTF (1 mark):** Mean Time To Failure — applicable to **non-repairable** components (e.g., sensors, ICs); equal to the average time of first failure.
- **Why MTTR is neglected (1 mark):** For high-reliability control systems, MTTR (typically 4–50 hours) is **orders of magnitude smaller** than MTTF (typically 10,000–100,000 hours). Hence MTBF ≈ MTTF, simplifying computations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** A safety shutdown system in a chemical reactor has three identical sensors connected in **1-out-of-2 (1oo2)** voting architecture. Each sensor has a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof test interval is $T_{proof} = 8760$ hours (1 year). Calculate:
- (i) The probability of failure on demand (PFD_avg).
- (ii) The achieved Safety Integrity Level.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **bathtub curve** and discuss its three regions in the context of automatic control system reliability. State which region is used for SIL calculations and why.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For a **1oo2 architecture**, the system fails on demand only if **both** sensors fail dangerously. Since the failures are independent:

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx \frac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof})^2}{3}$$

This is the standard IEC 61508 simplified formula for voted architectures.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute numerical values:

$$\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760 = 0.0438$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Square the result and divide by 3:

$$(\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof})^2 = (0.0438)^2 = 1.9184 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \frac{1.9184 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 6.395 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compare with the SIL table:
- $6.395 \times 10^{-4}$ lies between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-4}$ → **SIL 3** is achieved.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** The Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) is:

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{6.395 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1564$$

This is in the SIL 3 band of 1,000 – 10,000, confirming the result.

---

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Sketch (3 marks):** A graph with failure rate (λ) on Y-axis and time (t) on X-axis, showing three regions — initial decreasing curve, flat middle, and rising tail.

**Explanation of regions (3 marks):**
- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Failure rate **decreases** with time. Caused by manufacturing defects, poor solder joints, inadequate commissioning. Mitigated by factory burn-in and Site Acceptance Testing (SAT).
- **Region II — Useful Life:** Failure rate is **constant** at λ₀. Failures are random and statistically independent. This is the operating regime of mature, well-engineered control systems.
- **Region III — Wear-out:** Failure rate **increases** with time. Caused by corrosion, fatigue, seal degradation, calibration drift. Mitigated by preventive replacement.

**Why Region II is used for SIL calculations (1 mark):**
Safety Instrumented Systems are designed to operate within their useful life, with **preventive replacement** scheduled before wear-out begins. This makes the **constant failure rate assumption** valid, allowing the use of the mathematically tractable exponential distribution and the IEC 61508 PFD formulas.

---

> **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]

**(a) [7 marks]** A control system consists of **three components in series** with individual reliabilities $R_A = 0.95$, $R_B = 0.90$, $R_C = 0.99$ over a mission time of 500 hours. Calculate:
- (i) The system reliability.
- (ii) The system unreliability.
- (iii) The mean time to failure if each component has constant failure rate (use $\text{MTTF} = -\frac{t}{\ln R(t)}$).

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain with a labelled block diagram the concept of **Markov analysis** for a repairable control system. Derive the steady-state availability expression.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For a series system, the system reliability is the **product** of individual reliabilities:

$$R_{\text{sys}} = R_A \times R_B \times R_C = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.99$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Multiply step by step:
$$0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855$$
$$0.855 \times 0.99 = 0.84645$$

So $R_{\text{sys}} \approx 0.846$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Unreliability:
$$F_{\text{sys}} = 1 - R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - 0.846 = 0.154 \text{ (or 15.4%)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** For the system as a whole, since reliability is exponential:
$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{sys}} = -\frac{t}{\ln R_{\text{sys}}} = -\frac{500}{\ln(0.846)}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Compute the logarithm:
$$\ln(0.846) = -0.1671$$
$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{sys}} = -\frac{500}{-0.1671} = 2992 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Per-component MTTFs (optional but instructive):
$$\text{MTTF}_A = -\frac{500}{\ln 0.95} = 9740 \text{ hr}, \quad \text{MTTF}_B = 4750 \text{ hr}, \quad \text{MTTF}_C = 49750 \text{ hr}$$

The system MTTF (2992 hr) is **lower than the weakest component** (B at 4750 hr), illustrating that series reliability is bottlenecked by the chain.

---

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram / Description (3 marks):** The Markov model for a single repairable component has two states:
- **State 1: UP** (system operating normally)
- **State 2: DOWN** (system failed)

The transition from UP → DOWN occurs at rate **λ** (failure rate).
The transition from DOWN → UP occurs at rate **μ** (repair rate = 1/MTTR).

The probabilities $P_{\text{UP}}(t)$ and $P_{\text{DOWN}}(t)$ satisfy the differential equations:
$$\frac{dP_{\text{UP}}}{dt} = \mu P_{\text{DOWN}} - \lambda P_{\text{UP}}$$
$$\frac{dP_{\text{DOWN}}}{dt} = \lambda P_{\text{UP}} - \mu P_{\text{DOWN}}$$

**Derivation of Steady-State Availability (3 marks):**

In steady state, $\frac{dP}{dt} = 0$. From the balance equation:
$$\lambda P_{\text{UP}} = \mu P_{\text{DOWN}}$$

With conservation: $P_{\text{UP}} + P_{\text{DOWN}} = 1$. Substituting $P_{\text{DOWN}} = 1 - P_{\text{UP}}$:
$$\lambda P_{\text{UP}} = \mu (1 - P_{\text{UP}})$$
$$P_{\text{UP}} (\lambda + \mu) = \mu$$
$$A = P_{\text{UP}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{1/\text{MTTR}}{1/\text{MTBF} + 1/\text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The steady-state availability depends only on the ratio of repair to failure rates. For a control system with MTBF = 50,000 hr and MTTR = 24 hr:
$$A = \frac{50000}{50024} = 0.99952 \text{ (four-nines availability)}$$

This is the typical benchmark for safety-critical process control loops.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Mixing failure rates in *per hour* with proof test intervals in *years* (or vice versa) without conversion. Always convert $T_{proof}$ to the same time unit as λ before multiplication.
> 2. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF:** In repairable systems (e.g., valves, controllers), use MTBF. In non-repairable systems (e.g., sensors, ICs), use MTTF. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for misuse.
> 3. **Exponential vs. Weibull:** If a question mentions "wear-out" or "infant mortality," the exponential model is **invalid**. The Weibull distribution $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ with shape parameter β ≠ 1 must be used.
> 4. **Forgetting Common-Cause Failures (β-factor):** In redundant systems, never compute R_parallel without applying a β-factor (typically 5–10%) to account for CCF. Full credit requires a brief mention of β in the answer.
> 5. **Confusing PFD with PFH:** PFD applies to **low-demand** mode (≤ 1 demand/year). PFH (probability of failure per hour) applies to **high-demand / continuous** mode. KTU questions specify which.
> 6. **Architectural Confusion:** 1oo2 is **NOT** the same as 2oo2. 1oo2 = 1-out-of-2 voting (one channel is enough) → high availability, lower PFD. 2oo2 = 2-out-of-2 voting (both must agree) → fails on first disagreement. Students frequently interchange these.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Reliability Definition:** Probability of performing intended function under stated conditions for a stated period of time. Standard: **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**.
- **Exponential Model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — valid only in **Region II** (constant failure rate). Memoryless property is unique to this distribution.
- **MTTF = 1/λ** (for exponential, non-repairable); **MTBF ≈ 1/λ** (for repairable, MTTR ≪ MTTF).
- **Series Reliability:** $R_{\text{sys}} = \prod R_i$ — **weakest-link** behavior. System is always less reliable than its worst component.
- **Parallel Reliability:** $R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$ — redundancy gains. Two identical components: $R = 2R_0 - R_0^2$.
- **Availability:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — long-term fraction of operational time. Three-nines (A = 0.999) is industry benchmark.
- **Bathtub Curve:** Region I (infant mortality, ↓λ) → Region II (useful life, const λ) → Region III (wear-out, ↑λ). **SIS design targets Region II.**
- **PFD for 1oo1:** $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$. Halving $T_{proof}$ halves the PFD.
- **PFD for 1oo2 / 2oo3:** $\text{PFD} \approx \dfrac{(\lambda_D T_{proof})^2}{3}$ — **square-law** improvement due to redundancy.
- **Voting Architectures:** 1oo1 (single), 1oo2 (1-of-2 OR), 2oo2 (2-of-2 AND), 2oo3 (2-of-3 voted) — each has distinct PFD and diagnostic behaviour.
- **SIL Levels (IEC 61508):** SIL 1 ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$), SIL 2 ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$), SIL 3 ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$), SIL 4 ($10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$). Lower PFD = higher SIL = higher risk reduction.
- **Common-Cause Failures (β-factor):** In real redundant systems, only (1 − β) fraction of failures are truly independent. Always apply β = 5–10% in industrial calculations.
- **Markov Analysis:** Two-state (UP/DOWN) model for repairable systems. Steady-state availability $A = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ governs long-term behaviour.
- **High-Demand vs Low-Demand:** Use **PFD** for low-demand (≤ 1 demand/year); use **PFH** for continuous / high-demand operation.
- **SIS Layers:** Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element. **All three layers** must be evaluated for SIL compliance — failure of any one compromises safety.

> **Final KTU Board Mantra:** *"Reliability is multiplicative in series, complementary in parallel, and remember the bathtub curve is your constant companion."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PES Safety System Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PES Safety System Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# PES Safety System Development

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Programmable Electronic System (PES)** — As defined by **IEC 61508**, a PES is an electronic system used to perform safety functions, based on one or more programmable electronic devices (PEDs), including all elements of the system such as power supplies, sensors, input/output devices, communication interfaces, and application software.

**PES Safety System Development** — A systematic, lifecycle-oriented engineering process that follows a structured framework (per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**) to design, implement, validate, and maintain a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) using PES technology, ensuring that the **risk reduction** target meets a specified **Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1 to SIL 4)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Highlight: The syllabus expects students to internalize the **safety lifecycle phases**, **risk graph methodology**, and **hardware/software SIL requirements** as per the IEC 61508 framework.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Airbag" Mental Model

Imagine an airbag system in a modern car:

- The **crash sensor (input)** is the "eyes" of the system.
- The **ECU / microcontroller (PES logic solver)** is the "brain" — it decides *when* and *how fast* to deploy.
- The **inflator (final element)** is the "muscle" that actually saves the passenger.
- The **wiring, power supply, and connectors** are the "nervous system."

PES Safety System Development is the **engineering discipline that ensures this entire chain never fails catastrophically**. Just as an airbag is *designed*, *tested*, *documented*, *monitored*, and *periodically inspected* throughout its life, a PES safety system follows a **16-step safety lifecycle** from concept to decommissioning.

> [!NOTE]
> The probability of failure on demand (PFD) of an airbag's electronic firing circuit must be astronomically low — on the order of **10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁸ per hour**. This is what *functional safety* engineering guarantees through rigorous PES development.

---

## 1.3 PES in the Functional Safety Hierarchy

| Layer | Standard | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **L1** | IEC 61508 | Generic Functional Safety (all industries) |
| **L2** | IEC 61511 | Process Industry sector standard |
| **L3** | IEC 62061 | Machinery sector standard |
| **L4** | IEC 61513 | Nuclear sector standard |

PES development sits at **Layer 1 & 2**, providing the umbrella methodology for all safety-related electronic systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The IEC 61508 Safety Lifecycle (16 Phases, Grouped)

The standard mandates a **cradle-to-grave** lifecycle. For the KTU exam, the phases are typically grouped into **3 macro-phases**:

### **Macro-Phase A: Analysis (Phases 1–5)**

1. **Concept & Scope Definition** — Identify the Equipment Under Control (EUC) and the hazards.
2. **Hazard & Risk Analysis** — Determine hazards, their consequences, and the level of risk reduction.
3. **Allocation of Safety Functions to PES** — Decide which protective layers are E/E/PE-based.
4. **Overall Safety Requirements** — Specify the Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) and target SIL.
5. **SIL Determination** — Compute the required risk reduction factor (RRF).

### **Macro-Phase B: Realisation (Phases 6–13)**

6. **Overall Operation & Maintenance Planning**
7. **Overall Safety Validation Planning**
8. **E/E/PES System Safety Requirements Specification**
9. **E/E/PES System Design & Development**
10. **E/E/PES System Integration**
11. **E/E/PES System Installation & Commissioning**
12. **E/E/PES System Safety Validation**
13. **E/E/PES System Operation, Maintenance & Modification**

### **Macro-Phase C: Operation & Modification (Phases 14–16)**

14. **E/E/PES System Decommissioning or Disposal**
15. **Verification of Safety Lifecycle Activities**
16. **Management of Functional Safety, Change & Documentation**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Mapping:** "List the phases of safety lifecycle" → Direct 3-mark question. Memorize the grouping **Analysis → Realisation → Operation**.

---

## 2.2 The Core Risk Reduction Equation

The fundamental functional safety equation is:

$$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$$

Where:
- **RRF** = Risk Reduction Factor (dimensionless)
- **PFD_avg** = Average Probability of Failure on Demand (dimensionless, between 0 and 1)

The **required SIL** is then determined by mapping the **target PFD_avg** to one of four discrete bands:

| SIL Level | Target PFD_avg (Low Demand) | Target PFH (High/Continuous) | RRF Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | 10 to 100 |
| **SIL 2** | $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ | 100 to 1,000 |
| **SIL 3** | $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 |
| **SIL 4** | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | $\geq 10^{-9}$ to $< 10^{-8}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Use `\vert` Instead of `\mid` Where Required)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When writing `PFD = \vert 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \vert` or similar absolute-value forms on a board, use the word "mod" or write `( )` with a sign condition. The KTU valuation script does **not** parse LaTeX.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $RRF = 1 \div PFD_{avg}$ | Risk reduction required | dimensionless |
| 2 | $PFD_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} PFD_i$ | Sum of PFDs of independent protection layers | dimensionless |
| 3 | $PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$ | Average PFD for a single channel (low demand) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $PFH = \lambda_{DU}$ | Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour (high demand) | per hour (h⁻¹) |
| 5 | $SFF = (\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}) \div (\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD})$ | Safe Failure Fraction | % |
| 6 | $\beta = \lambda_{DD} \div \lambda_{D}$ | Diagnostic Coverage fraction | dimensionless |
| 7 | $MTBF = 1 \div \lambda$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| 8 | $MTTR = 1 \div \mu$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| 9 | $DC = \lambda_{DD} \div (\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU})$ | Diagnostic Coverage | % |
| 10 | $HFT_{req} = SIL - 1 + DC$ | Minimum Hardware Fault Tolerance (for Type B) | integer (0, 1, 2) |

> [!NOTE]
> **$\lambda_{DU}$** = dangerous undetected failure rate; **$\lambda_{DD}$** = dangerous detected failure rate; **$\lambda_{S}$** = safe failure rate. Their sum is the total dangerous failure rate $\lambda_{D} = \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}$.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the SIL Architecture

- **Why four SILs?** Because risk is a continuum, but verification/testing is discrete. Mapping into 4 bands allows for tractable, auditable engineering.
- **Why redundancy (HFT)?** Because any single electronic component has a non-zero failure rate, and the **law of large numbers in failure** means that without redundancy, the PFD can never reach $10^{-5}$ or lower.
- **Why diagnostic coverage (DC)?** Because failures that are **detected automatically** (e.g., by watchdog timers, comparator logic) can be repaired *before* a demand occurs, effectively shrinking $\lambda_{DU}$ (the dangerous part that escapes detection).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Average PFD for a Single Non-Redundant Channel

We start with the assumption that a single channel is **operational** at $t = 0$ and remains operational until a **dangerous undetected failure** occurs at time $t$. The failure is described by an exponential distribution.

**Step 1 — Probability of surviving (still operational) up to time $t$:**

$$P_{survive}(t) = e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}$$

This is the classical reliability function for components with constant failure rate.

**Step 2 — Probability density of failing at time $t$:**

$$f(t) = \lambda_{DU} \cdot e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}$$

This is the derivative of $(1 - P_{survive}(t))$ with respect to $t$.

**Step 3 — Average time to failure (MTTF), assuming the system is not yet failed at $t = 0$:**

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}}$$

The integral is a standard gamma integral of order 2.

**Step 4 — Now consider the realistic safety scenario:** A proof test occurs every $T_{proof}$ hours. The system is tested, and any dangerous failure is repaired (system restored to "as new"). The demand can occur uniformly anywhere in the interval $[0, T_{proof}]$.

**Step 5 — Average probability of failure on demand, conditional on demand arriving uniformly in the interval:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}\right) dt$$

The integrand is "1 minus reliability," which is the probability the system has *already* failed by time $t$.

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \left[ t + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}}{\lambda_{DU}} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the limits:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \left[ T_{proof} + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}}{\lambda_{DU}} - 0 - \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} \right]$$

**Step 8 — Simplify:**

$$PFD_{avg} = 1 + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}} - 1}{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}$$

**Step 9 — Apply Taylor series for small $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$** (typical case, since $\lambda_{DU}$ is $\sim 10^{-6}$/h and $T_{proof}$ is $\sim 8760$ h):

$$e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}} \approx 1 - \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 10 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$PFD_{avg} \approx 1 + \frac{\left(1 - \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{2}\right) - 1}{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}$$

$$PFD_{avg} \approx 1 + \frac{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{2}}{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}$$

$$PFD_{avg} \approx 1 - 1 + \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

**Final Closed-Form Result:**

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: SIL Determination for a PES

**Problem:** A chemical reactor has a runaway reaction hazard. The unmitigated event frequency is $F = 10^{-1}$ events/year, the consequence severity is "High," and the plant has been occupied for less than 10 years. Determine the target SIL.

**Given (using the IEC 61511 risk graph):**
- Consequence parameter: $C = 4$ (High)
- Frequency parameter: $F = 4$ (Frequent)
- Occupancy: $P = 1$ (Low)
- Possibility of avoidance: $W = 1$ (Low)

**Step 1 — Compute the unmitigated risk score (proxy):**

$$Risk_{unmitigated} \propto C \cdot F \cdot P \cdot W = 4 \cdot 4 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 16$$

**Step 2 — Apply the IEC 61511 risk graph to map the score to a required SIL:**

| Risk Score Range | Required SIL |
|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | No safety requirement |
| 5 – 10 | SIL 1 |
| 11 – 25 | SIL 2 |
| 26 – 50 | SIL 3 |
| > 50 | SIL 4 |

Since $16$ falls in the range $[11, 25]$, the **required SIL is 2**.

**Step 3 — Compute the target PFD_avg:**

For SIL 2: $PFD_{avg} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$. Choose a conservative mid-band value:

$$PFD_{avg, target} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required RRF:**

$$RRF = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200$$

**Step 5 — Verify hardware fault tolerance requirements (Type B subsystem):**

For SIL 2 with DC $\geq 60\%$, the minimum HFT is:

$$HFT_{req} = SIL - 1 + DC_{qualifier} = 2 - 1 + 0 = 1$$

So a **1-out-of-2 (1oo2)** voted architecture is required at minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many students lose marks by quoting the SIL table incorrectly. Always quote the **PFD_avg range** *and* the **PFH range** when stating a SIL — examiners specifically look for this duality.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the PES Architecture

| Architecture | Voting | HFT | Typical PFD (per channel $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$ = 0.01) | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1oo1** | Single | 0 | $5 \times 10^{-3}$ | Low | SIL 1 only |
| **1oo2D** | 1-of-2 with Diagnostics | 0 | $\sim 10^{-4}$ | Medium | SIL 2 |
| **2oo2** | 2-of-2 | 1 | $\sim 10^{-4}$ | Medium | SIL 2 (low-spurious) |
| **2oo3** | 2-of-3 | 1 | $\sim 10^{-5}$ | High | SIL 3 |
| **1oo2D with diverse redundancy** | 1-of-2 voted | 1 | $\sim 10^{-5}$ | Very High | SIL 3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 The IEC 61508 Safety Lifecycle — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    phaseA1["Phase 1: EUC and Hazard Identification"] --> phaseA2["Phase 2: Hazard and Risk Analysis"]
    phaseA2 --> phaseA3["Phase 3: Overall Safety Requirements"]
    phaseA3 --> phaseA4["Phase 4: Safety Requirements Allocation to E/E/PES"]
    phaseA4 --> phaseA5["Phase 5: SIL Determination and Target PFD"]
    phaseA5 --> phaseB1["Phase 6: Operation and Maintenance Plan"]
    phaseB1 --> phaseB2["Phase 7: Safety Validation Plan"]
    phaseB2 --> phaseB3["Phase 8: E/E/PES Safety Requirements Spec"]
    phaseB3 --> phaseB4["Phase 9: E/E/PES System Design"]
    phaseB4 --> phaseB5["Phase 10: PES Implementation and Integration"]
    phaseB5 --> phaseB6["Phase 11: Installation and Commissioning"]
    phaseB6 --> phaseB7["Phase 12: Safety Validation"]
    phaseB7 --> phaseB8["Phase 13: Operation Maintenance and Modification"]
    phaseB8 --> phaseC1["Phase 14: Decommissioning"]
    phaseB4 --> phaseV1["Verification Phase 15: Cross-Phase Audit"]
    phaseB5 --> phaseV1
    phaseB7 --> phaseV1
    phaseV1 --> phaseC2["Phase 16: Functional Safety Management"]
    phaseC2 --> phaseB3
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PES Safety Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    process["PROCESS UNDER CONTROL"] --> sensor1["SENSOR 1 PT100"]
    process --> sensor2["SENSOR 2 Redundant"]
    sensor1 --> inputA["INPUT MODULE PES"]
    sensor2 --> inputA
    inputA --> solver["PES LOGIC SOLVER CPU PLC"]
    solver --> diag["DIAGNOSTIC ENGINE"]
    diag --> solver
    solver --> outputA["OUTPUT MODULE PES"]
    outputA --> final1["FINAL ELEMENT Valve 1"]
    outputA --> final2["FINAL ELEMENT Valve 2"]
    final1 --> process
    final2 --> process
    solver --> hmi["OPERATOR HMI"]
    solver --> alarm["ANNUNCIATOR PANEL"]
```

---

## 4.3 Risk Reduction Layer (Onion) Model — Block Representation

```mermaid
graph TD
    inner["INNermost: PROCESS EUC"] --> L1["Layer 1: Process Design Basic Controls BPCS"]
    L1 --> L2["Layer 2: Alarm and Operator Intervention"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3: Safety Instrumented System SIS"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4: Mechanical Relief Devices"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5: Plant Emergency Response"]
    L5 --> outer["OUTER: Community Emergency Plans"]
    style L3 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style solver fill:#ff9966
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:** The **PES-based Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is *Layer 3*. The KTU syllabus emphasises that PES should **not** be the first or only line of defense — it is added only after intrinsic safety and BPCS layers are exhausted.

---

## 4.4 SIL Determination Workflow (Mermaid Decision Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["START Identify Hazard"] --> consec["Estimate Consequence Severity C"]
    consec --> freq["Estimate Event Frequency F"]
    freq --> occ["Determine Occupancy P"]
    occ --> avoid["Assess Possibility of Avoidance W"]
    avoid --> calc["Compute Risk Parameter C F P W"]
    calc --> map{"Map to SIL Band"}
    map --> sil1["SIL 1: PFD 10 to power neg 2 to neg 1"]
    map --> sil2["SIL 2: PFD 10 to power neg 3 to neg 2"]
    map --> sil3["SIL 3: PFD 10 to power neg 4 to neg 3"]
    map --> sil4["SIL 4: PFD 10 to power neg 5 to neg 4"]
    sil1 --> arch1["Architecture 1oo2D"]
    sil2 --> arch2["Architecture 1oo2D or 2oo3"]
    sil3 --> arch3["Architecture 2oo3 with Diagnostics"]
    sil4 --> arch4["Architecture 2oo4D with Diversity"]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define a Programmable Electronic System (PES) as per IEC 61508. List the three main elements of a PES-based safety loop.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Programmable Electronic System (PES)** per **IEC 61508** is a system used to perform one or more safety functions, based on one or more programmable electronic devices, including all associated elements such as power supplies, sensors, actuators, input/output devices, and application software.

The **three main elements of a PES-based safety loop** are:

1. **Sensing / Input Element** — e.g., pressure transmitter, temperature sensor.
2. **Logic Solver (PES core)** — e.g., PLC, DCS-based logic solver, or safety-certified controller.
3. **Final Control / Output Element** — e.g., solenoid valve, circuit breaker, motor contactor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students wrongly call it a "PLC" — *PLC is the hardware, PES is the safety system.* Use the term **PES** in all answers, not "controller."

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between low-demand mode and high-demand (continuous) mode of operation in PES safety systems. What metric is used in each mode?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Low-Demand Mode | High-Demand / Continuous Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Demand frequency** | $\leq 1$ demand per year | $> 1$ demand per year or continuous |
| **Metric used** | **PFD_avg** (Probability of Failure on Demand) | **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour) |
| **Formula** | $PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | $PFH = \lambda_{DU}$ |
| **Application** | Emergency shutdown (ESD), fire & gas systems | Burner management, continuous control loops |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing just "PFD" without the **"average"** subscript loses a mark. Always write $PFD_{avg}$ for low-demand.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Pattern: Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means you can answer **either** Question A or Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **E/E/PES safety lifecycle** as per IEC 61508. List any **eight** lifecycle phases with their purpose.

**Model Answer:**

The IEC 61508 safety lifecycle is a **16-phase, cradle-to-grave framework** that ensures functional safety is engineered systematically rather than left to chance.

**Block Diagram (text-rendered for board exam):**

```
CONCEPT → ANALYSIS → REALISATION → OPERATION → DECOMMISSION
    ↓          ↓           ↓              ↓            ↓
 Phase1     Phase2-5    Phase6-13       Phase14      Phase16
 EUC &     Hazard &   Design, Build,   Removal     Management
 Hazards    Risk       Install, Validate            of Change
```

**Eight Phases (write any 8):**

1. **Phase 1 — Concept & Scope:** Define EUC and its physical/operational boundaries.
2. **Phase 2 — Hazard & Risk Analysis:** Identify hazards, estimate consequences, frequencies.
3. **Phase 3 — Overall Safety Requirements:** Specify what safety functions are needed.
4. **Phase 4 — Allocation to E/E/PES:** Decide which protection layers are PES-based.
5. **Phase 5 — SIL Target:** Determine required Safety Integrity Level per SIF.
6. **Phase 8 — E/E/PES Safety Requirements Specification:** Document functional & integrity specs.
7. **Phase 9 — E/E/PES System Design:** Hardware architecture, redundancy, diagnostics, software.
8. **Phase 12 — Safety Validation:** Prove the installed system meets the SIL target.

**(b) [7 marks]** A PES sensor has $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$/h and proof test interval $T_{proof} = 8760$ h. Compute $PFD_{avg}$ and identify the achievable SIL.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 2:**

$$PFD_{avg} = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with SIL bands:**

| SIL | PFD Range |
|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |

Since $2.19 \times 10^{-2}$ lies in $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$, the **achievable SIL is 1**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to **divide by 2** and end up with $4.38 \times 10^{-2}$, which would also fall in SIL 1 — they get the right SIL by luck. Always show the full division step.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** and **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**. Why are they important in PES design?

**Model Answer:**

**Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT):** HFT is the *minimum number of faults that can occur in a hardware subsystem without loss of the safety function.* For example, a 2-out-of-3 voted system has HFT = 1 — one channel can fail completely and the safety function still operates.

$$HFT = N - K$$

where $N$ is the number of channels and $K$ is the number needed for safe operation.

**Safe Failure Fraction (SFF):** SFF is the ratio of safe failures plus detected dangerous failures to the total failure rate:

$$SFF = \frac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

**Importance in PES Design:**

1. **HFT quantifies redundancy** required to survive a single failure.
2. **SFF quantifies the quality of diagnostic coverage** — high SFF means most dangerous failures are caught and annunciated.
3. Together, they **constrain the architecture allowed for a given SIL** (per Tables 2 & 3 of IEC 61508).
4. They are the **two non-negotiable engineering levers** to reduce PFD without resorting to excessively low failure-rate components.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 1oo2D architecture is proposed for a SIL 2 application. The single-channel PFD_avg is $5 \times 10^{-3}$, and $\lambda_{DU}$ per channel is $2 \times 10^{-6}$/h. Compute the system PFD and PFH.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the architecture properties:**

For a **1oo2D** (1-out-of-2 with diagnostics), HFT = 0 but high diagnostic coverage reduces effective $\lambda_{DU}$.

**Step 2 — System PFD_avg formula for 1oo2D:**

$$PFD_{sys, 1oo2D} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot T_{proof}^{2}$$

(The factor 1/3 accounts for the average over the proof test interval, and the squared term accounts for the need for *both* channels to fail undetected.)

**Step 3 — System PFH formula for 1oo2D:**

$$PFH_{sys, 1oo2D} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot T_{proof}$$

Wait — the more common simplified form for PFH of 1oo2D is:

$$PFH_{sys, 1oo2D} = 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot T_{proof}$$

Assuming $\beta = 1$ (perfect DC), the second-order term goes to zero, so the PFH is dominated by diagnostic shutdown.

**Step 4 — Compute PFD with $T_{proof} = 8760$ h:**

$$PFD_{sys} = \frac{1}{3} \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (8760)^{2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{3} \times 4 \times 10^{-12} \times 7.674 \times 10^{7}$$

$$= \frac{1}{3} \times 3.07 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$PFD_{sys} \approx 1.02 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Map to SIL:**

Since $1.02 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in $[10^{-4}, 10^{-3}]$, the **architecture meets SIL 3**, exceeding the SIL 2 target — design is acceptable with margin.

---

## Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **PES = Programmable Electronic System**, not just a PLC — it is a complete safety system per IEC 61508.
- The **safety lifecycle has 16 phases**, grouped into **3 macro-phases**: Analysis (1–5), Realisation (6–13), Operation (14–16).
- **PFD_avg** is for **low-demand** mode; **PFH** is for **high-demand / continuous** mode.
- The master equation: $RRF = 1 \div PFD_{avg}$.
- For a single non-redundant channel: $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} \div 2$.
- **SIL 1** = PFD $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$; **SIL 2** = $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$; **SIL 3** = $[10^{-4}, 10^{-3}]$; **SIL 4** = $[10^{-5}, 10^{-4}]$.
- **HFT** = number of faults tolerated without safety function loss.
- **SFF** = $(\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}) \div (\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU})$.
- **Diagnostic coverage (DC)** reduces $\lambda_{DU}$ by catching dangerous failures.
- The **"Onion Model"** has 5 layers; PES-based **SIS sits at Layer 3**, never as the first line of defense.
- Common architectures: **1oo1, 1oo2D, 2oo2, 2oo3, 1oo2D diverse** — choose based on SIL target and spurious trip rate.
- Always quote the **PFD *and* PFH** values when stating a SIL — examiners reward the duality.
- For a 1oo2D system: $PFD_{sys} = (1/3) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot T_{proof}^{2}$ — always shows up in **Part B derivations**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logic Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logic Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Safety Engineering and Management**, a **Logic Diagram** is a structured, graphical analytical tool that employs standardized logical symbols (gates) to represent the **Boolean relationship between initiating events, intermediate events, and the top undesired event** (the accident scenario). It is the foundational building block of deductive safety analysis techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A Logic Diagram is a top‑down deductive representation that uses **AND, OR, and NOT gates** to model how combinations of lower‑level basic events (component failures, human errors, environmental conditions) logically combine through Boolean algebra to produce a higher‑level undesired Top Event.

The two principal types of Logic Diagrams in safety engineering are:

| Type | Direction | Purpose |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Fault Tree (FT)** | Deductive (Top‑Down) | Identifies *how* a Top Event can occur |
| **Event Tree (ET)** | Inductive (Bottom‑Up) | Identifies *what* can happen after an initiating event |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Electrical Household Circuit

Imagine the wiring of your house. A **series connection** (two switches one after the other) means the bulb glows **only if BOTH switches are ON** → this is the **AND** gate. A **parallel connection** (two separate switches feeding the same bulb) means the bulb glows if **EITHER switch is ON** → this is the **OR** gate.

> A **Logic Diagram does exactly this for safety**: it asks the question — *"Under what combinations of component failures will the accident (Top Event) occur?"* and maps the answer as a Boolean circuit.

### 1.3 Core Symbols in a Logic Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Primary Gates (Mandatory for KTU)**
> 1. **AND Gate** → Output event occurs *only if ALL* input events occur simultaneously.
> 2. **OR Gate** → Output event occurs *if ANY ONE* input event occurs.
> 3. **NOT Gate (Inhibit)** → Output event occurs *if the input event does NOT occur*.

Standard **boundary events** used in fault trees:

- **Rectangle** → Resultant / Intermediate Event (developed further)
- **Circle** → Basic Event (component failure, leaves of the tree)
- **Diamond** → Undeveloped Event (insufficient data or outside scope)
- **House** → External / Normal Event (a condition assumed to exist)
- **Oval** → Conditioning Event (e.g., "if the system is in mode X")

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High‑Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Why and How

A logic diagram operates on **Boolean algebra**, where every event is either **TRUE (occurred)** with probability $p$ or **FALSE (did not occur)** with probability $(1-p)$.

**The 'Why' (Purpose):**
- To systematically identify *all* minimal combinations of failures leading to a Top Event.
- To quantify the probability of the Top Event using component failure data.
- To pinpoint the *critical components* (those contributing the most to risk).

**The 'How' (Methodology):**
1. **Define the Top Event** (e.g., "Reactor overpressure leading to rupture").
2. **Identify immediate causes** (one level below the Top Event).
3. **Apply logic gates** to connect causes to the Top Event.
4. **Continue deductive decomposition** until only Basic Events remain.
5. **Write the Boolean expression** for the Top Event.
6. **Compute probabilities** using AND/OR formulas.

### 2.2 Boolean Algebra of the Three Primary Gates

Let $A$ and $B$ be two independent events with probabilities $P(A)$ and $P(B)$.

**AND Gate Logic (Intersection):**

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

> *Physical meaning:* Both events must occur together. In a **series safety system**, every component must fail for the system to fail (worst case — most dangerous configuration).

**OR Gate Logic (Union):**

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A)\cdot P(B)$$

For small probabilities ($P < 0.1$), the rare‑event approximation holds:

$$P(A \cup B) \approx P(A) + P(B)$$

> *Physical meaning:* Either event alone is sufficient. In a **parallel redundant safety system**, the system fails only when all redundant components fail (best case — most protective).

**NOT Gate (Complement):**

$$P(\bar{A}) = 1 - P(A)$$

### 2.3 KTU High‑Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE KTU 2024 LOGIC DIAGRAM CHEAT SHEET — Memorize Before Exam**

| # | Gate / Concept | Boolean Form | Probability Formula | Real‑World Example |
|---|----------------|--------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | AND Gate (2 inputs) | $A \cdot B$ | $P_T = P_A \cdot P_B$ | Two valves in series, both must close |
| 2 | AND Gate (n inputs) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | $P_T = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Complete power‑off requires all circuit breakers to fail |
| 3 | OR Gate (2 inputs) | $A + B$ | $P_T = P_A + P_B - P_AP_B$ | Either sensor trips the shutdown |
| 4 | OR Gate (n inputs) | $1 - \prod(1-A_i)$ | $P_T = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-P_i)$ | Redundant fire detectors |
| 5 | NOT Gate | $\bar{A}$ | $P_T = 1 - P_A$ | "Pump does NOT start" |
| 6 | XOR Gate | $A \oplus B$ | $P_T = P_A + P_B - 2P_AP_B$ | One of two sensors must fail (different modes) |
| 7 | Minimal Cut Set | $C_{min}$ | $P(C_{min}) = \prod P_i$ | Smallest group of failures → top event |
| 8 | Top Event (Cut Set Union) | $T = \bigcup_j C_j$ | $P_T = 1 - \prod(1 - P_{C_j})$ | All minimal cut sets combined via OR |

### 2.4 Real‑World Utility in Engineering

- **Nuclear Industry:** Reactor SCRAM (Safety Control Rod Activation Mechanism) logic.
- **Chemical Process Safety:** Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) + Rupture Disk redundancy logic.
- **Aviation:** Flight Control System redundancy (multiple hydraulics, computers).
- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** Blowout Preventer (BOP) stack logic — multiple shear rams AND hydraulic accumulators.
- **Fire Protection:** Detection (multiple sensors OR'd) AND Suppression (multiple sprinklers AND'd).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step‑By‑Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Three Independent Failure Modes

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A critical cooling system has three independent components:
> - Pump A (failure probability $P_A = 0.02$)
> - Pump B (failure probability $P_B = 0.03$)
> - Pipe Blockage (failure probability $P_C = 0.05$)
>
> The system fails if **(A AND B both fail) OR (C occurs)**. Construct the Logic Diagram and compute the top event probability.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the Top Event and its immediate logical structure.**

The Top Event is **"Cooling System Failure"** ($T$).
The Boolean structure of the problem statement is:

$$T = (A \cdot B) + C$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Logic Diagram (textual representation).**

```
        [TOP EVENT: Cooling System Failure]
                       |
                +------+------+
                |             |
              ( OR )          |
            gate             [C: Pipe Blockage]
                |
        +-------+--------+
        |                |
   ( AND ) gate         [C input]
        |
   +----+----+
   |         |
[A]       [B]
Pump A    Pump B
```

**Step 3 — Define the Intermediate Event M (AND of A and B).**

The AND gate output $M$ requires both pumps to fail:

$$P(M) = P(A \cdot B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

Substituting the given values:

$$P(M) = 0.02 \times 0.03 = 0.0006 = 6 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the OR gate output (Top Event probability).**

The OR gate combines $M$ and $C$. Since $M$ and $C$ arise from independent physical phenomena (pumps vs. pipe), we treat them as independent:

$$P(T) = P(M) + P(C) - P(M)\cdot P(C)$$

Substituting:

$$P(M) \cdot P(C) = 0.0006 \times 0.05 = 0.00003 = 3 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$P(T) = 0.0006 + 0.05 - 0.00003 = 0.05057$$

**Step 5 — Apply the rare‑event approximation for engineering check.**

Because $P_C = 0.05$ is small (less than 0.1):

$$P(T) \approx P(M) + P(C) = 0.0006 + 0.05 = 0.0506$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

The dominant contributor to the Top Event is the **pipe blockage** (contributes $0.05 / 0.0506 \approx 98.8\%$). The pump failure combination contributes only $\approx 1.2\%$. The Pareto Principle (80/20 rule) indicates that **engineering resources should focus on pipe integrity**, not on pump redundancy.

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$\boxed{P(T) = 0.05057 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 5.057 \times 10^{-2} \;\; (\approx 1 \text{ in } 19.7)}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Configuration Types

| System Configuration | Logic Gate | Probability Formula | Numerical Value (each = 0.1) | Failure Rate (each = $10^{-3}$/hr) |
|----------------------|------------|---------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| 2 Components in **Series** | AND | $P_1 \cdot P_2$ | $0.01$ | $2 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 3 Components in **Series** | AND | $P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3$ | $0.001$ | $3 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 2 Components in **Parallel** | OR | $1 - (1-P_1)(1-P_2)$ | $0.19$ | $1.99 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 3 Components in **Parallel** | OR | $1 - \prod(1-P_i)$ | $0.271$ | $3.99 \times 10^{-9}$ |
| 2‑out‑of‑3 Voting (2oo3) | Mixed | $3P^2 - 2P^3$ | $0.028$ | $2.998 \times 10^{-6}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 2oo3 voting system is widely used in **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** per IEC 61508/61511. It offers a balance between **availability** (parallel logic) and **safety** (avoids dangerous failure‑to‑operate through redundancy).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block‑Level Fault Tree Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeTopEvent[RECTANGLE: TOP EVENT REACTOR RUPTURE]
    nodeGateOR{GATE OR}
    nodeIntOverpress[RECTANGLE: OVERPRESSURE SCENARIO]
    nodeGateAND{GATE AND}
    nodeIntLossCool[RECTANGLE: LOSS OF COOLING]
    nodeIntBlockPipe[RECTANGLE: BLOCKED PIPE]
    nodeBasicA1[CIRCLE: PUMP A FAILS]
    nodeBasicA2[CIRCLE: PUMP B FAILS]
    nodeBasicB1[CIRCLE: VALVE 1 CLOSED]
    nodeBasicB2[CIRCLE: VALVE 2 CLOSED]
    nodeBasicC1[CIRCLE: PIPE BLOCKAGE]
    nodeBasicD1[CIRCLE: PRV STICKS OPEN]
    nodeBasicD2[CIRCLE: SENSOR FAILS]

    nodeTopEvent --> nodeGateOR
    nodeGateOR --> nodeIntOverpress
    nodeGateOR --> nodeIntLossCool
    nodeIntOverpress --> nodeGateAND
    nodeGateAND --> nodeBasicD1
    nodeGateAND --> nodeBasicD2
    nodeIntLossCool --> nodeBasicA1
    nodeIntLossCool --> nodeBasicA2
    nodeIntLossCool --> nodeBasicB1
    nodeIntLossCool --> nodeBasicB2
    nodeIntLossCool --> nodeBasicC1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Event Tree Diagram (Forward Logic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeInit[INITIATING EVENT: GAS LEAK DETECTED]
    nodeQ1{STEP 1: SENSOR RESPONSE}
    nodePath1A[BRANCH 1A: SENSOR OK]
    nodePath1B[BRANCH 1B: SENSOR FAILS]
    nodeQ2{STEP 2: SHUTDOWN VALVE}
    nodePath2A[BRANCH 2A: VALVE CLOSES]
    nodePath2B[BRANCH 2B: VALVE STICKS]
    nodeQ3{STEP 3: IGNITION SOURCE}
    nodePath3A[SAFE: NO IGNITION]
    nodePath3B[UNSAFE: IGNITION OCCURS]
    nodeSafe[OUTCOME: SAFE SHUTDOWN]
    nodeFire[OUTCOME: UNCONTROLLED FIRE]

    nodeInit --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|Success| nodePath1A
    nodeQ1 -->|Failure| nodePath1B
    nodePath1A --> nodeQ2
    nodePath1B --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ2 -->|Success| nodeSafe
    nodeQ2 -->|Failure| nodeQ3
    nodeQ3 -->|No| nodeSafe
    nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodeFire
```

### 4.3 Boolean Logic Gate Symbol Reference

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeInputA1[A] --> nodeGateAND1{GATE AND OUTPUT}
    nodeInputA2[B] --> nodeGateAND1
    nodeInputA3[A] --> nodeGateOR1{GATE OR OUTPUT}
    nodeInputA4[B] --> nodeGateOR1
    nodeInputA5[A] --> nodeGateNOT1{GATE NOT OUTPUT BAR A}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Tree:**
> - **Rectangle (text label)** = Intermediate / Top Event.
> - **Circle (text label)** = Basic Event (component failure).
> - **Diamond shape `{ }`** = Logic Gate (AND/OR) — the *shape* of the gate in the diamond indicates the Boolean operation.
> - **Connecting lines** → Direction of logical deduction (Top‑Down for Fault Tree, Left‑Right for Event Tree).

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow of Logic Diagram Analysis

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStepA[STEP 1: DEFINE SYSTEM BOUNDARIES]
    nodeStepB[STEP 2: SELECT TOP EVENT]
    nodeStepC[STEP 3: CONSTRUCT FAULT TREE]
    nodeStepD[STEP 4: IDENTIFY MINIMAL CUT SETS]
    nodeStepE[STEP 5: OBTAIN FAILURE DATA]
    nodeStepF[STEP 6: QUANTITATIVE EVALUATION]
    nodeStepG[STEP 7: IMPORTANCE ANALYSIS]
    nodeStepH[STEP 8: SAFETY RECOMMENDATIONS]

    nodeStepA --> nodeStepB
    nodeStepB --> nodeStepC
    nodeStepC --> nodeStepD
    nodeStepD --> nodeStepE
    nodeStepE --> nodeStepF
    nodeStepF --> nodeStepG
    nodeStepG --> nodeStepH
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Alert — Logic Diagram Questions**
> 1. **Confusing AND with OR:** Students often invert the gate logic, especially in parallel systems. *Always read the gate label first, then identify the connecting events.*
> 2. **Forgetting independence assumption:** Boolean multiplication ($P_A \cdot P_B$) is only valid for *independent* events. Common cause failures (CCF) require the **beta‑factor model**.
> 3. **Arithmetic slip in OR gate:** $P(A \cup B) \neq P(A) + P(B)$ in general — students must subtract $P(A) \cdot P(B)$.
> 4. **Missing cut set decomposition:** Writing a Boolean expression is only half the answer; the **minimal cut sets** must be listed for full marks.
> 5. **Not drawing the diagram:** A textual Boolean expression without the Fault Tree diagram attracts partial marking only.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a Logic Diagram and list the three primary logical gates used in fault tree analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Logic Diagram** is a deductive graphical analytical tool that uses Boolean logic gates to represent the causal combinations of basic events that lead to an undesired top event. The three primary gates are:

- **AND Gate:** The output event occurs only if **all** input events occur simultaneously. (1 mark)
- **OR Gate:** The output event occurs if **any one or more** input events occur. (1 mark)
- **NOT Gate (Inhibit):** The output event occurs if the input event does **not** occur. (1 mark)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] With a suitable example, explain the concept of a Minimal Cut Set in fault tree analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is the smallest combination of basic events whose *simultaneous occurrence* will cause the top event, and which cannot be reduced further without losing its top‑event‑causing property. (1.5 marks)

**Example:** For a system with logic $T = A\cdot B + C \cdot D$, the minimal cut sets are $\{A,B\}$ and $\{C,D\}$. (1.5 marks)
The cut set $\{A, B, C\}$ is **not minimal** because removing $C$ still leaves $\{A,B\}$ capable of causing the top event.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Fault Tree Construction & Evaluation**

##### **(a) Construct a Fault Tree for the following scenario: (7 Marks)**
*"A chemical reactor will experience overpressure (Top Event) if either (i) the Pressure Relief Valve fails to open AND the Rupture Disk is also blocked, OR (ii) the emergency cooling system fails to operate, OR (iii) both the high‑pressure sensor and the operator alarm fail."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the Top Event:** $T$ = "Reactor Overpressure".

**Step 2 — Identify the top‑level gate structure:** From the problem statement, three immediate pathways are joined by **OR** (because any one of them is sufficient):

$$T = (A \cdot B) + C + (D \cdot E)$$

Where:
- $A$ = PRV fails to open
- $B$ = Rupture Disk blocked
- $C$ = Emergency cooling fails
- $D$ = High‑pressure sensor fails
- $E$ = Operator alarm fails

**Step 3 — Develop the immediate logic:**

```
                 [T: REACTOR OVERPRESSURE]
                            |
                         ( OR )
            +---------------+---------------+
            |               |               |
   ( A AND B )            [C]          ( D AND E )
   PRV & RD           COOLING         SENSOR & ALARM
```

**Step 4 — Identify Minimal Cut Sets:**

$$\{A, B\}, \quad \{C\}, \quad \{D, E\}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the top event probability** (using $P_A = 0.01$, $P_B = 0.005$, $P_C = 0.02$, $P_D = 0.015$, $P_E = 0.01$):

$$P(AB) = 0.01 \times 0.005 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$P(DE) = 0.015 \times 0.01 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$P(T) \approx P(AB) + P_C + P(DE) = 5\times10^{-5} + 0.02 + 1.5\times10^{-4} \approx 0.0202$$

(7 marks — distributed as: 2 for structure, 2 for Boolean expression, 2 for cut sets, 1 for probability)

---

##### **(b) For the above system, identify the dominant minimal cut set and suggest a safety recommendation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compare cut set probabilities:**

| Cut Set | Probability | Order |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $\{A, B\}$ | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | Second |
| $\{C\}$ | $2 \times 10^{-2}$ | First (Dominant) |
| $\{D, E\}$ | $1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ | Third |

**Step 2 — Dominant cut set:** The single‑event cut set $\{C\}$ contributes $0.02 / 0.0202 \approx 99\%$ of the total top event probability. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Importance measure (Birnbaum):** Because $\{C\}$ is a single‑event cut set, its failure will *alone* cause the top event. This is the most safety‑critical component. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Safety recommendations:**

1. Add a **redundant emergency cooling loop** (parallel pump + secondary water supply) to eliminate the single‑point‑of‑failure. *(1 mark)*
2. Install an **independent high‑integrity pressure protection system** (SIL‑3 certified per IEC 61511) to detect and actuate before PRV. *(1 mark)*
3. Implement **proof testing** every 6 months to verify PRV and rupture disk operability. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Event Tree Analysis & Boolean Systems**

##### **(a) Explain the construction of an Event Tree Diagram with a suitable safety engineering example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** An **Event Tree (ET)** is an *inductive* (forward‑looking) logic diagram that starts with an **initiating event** and proceeds through a sequence of **safety function successes or failures**, branching at each step to display all possible outcome pathways.

**Construction Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Identify the initiating event** (e.g., pipeline leak).
2. **Identify safety functions** in chronological order (sensor → isolation → depressurization → firefighting).
3. **For each function, create a binary branch:** UP = Success (safety function operates), DOWN = Failure.
4. **Continue** until all functions are evaluated.
5. **Read the final column** for outcome classification (Safe / Mitigated / Severe accident).

**Suitable Example — LPG Storage Tank Leak (3 marks):**

| Initiating Event | Sensor | Isolation Valve | Water Spray | Outcome |
|------------------|--------|-----------------|------------|---------|
| LPG Leak | ✓ (S) | ✓ (S) | ✓ (S) | **Safe** |
| LPG Leak | ✓ (S) | ✓ (S) | ✗ (F) | Localized pool fire |
| LPG Leak | ✓ (S) | ✗ (F) | ✓ (S) | Major release → Vapour Cloud |
| LPG Leak | ✗ (F) | — | — | Undetected leak → Major Accident |

Each row is one **sequence path**; the probability of the overall accident is the product of the branch probabilities along the corresponding path, summed across all unsafe paths.

---

##### **(b) Derive the Boolean expression and Top Event probability for a system where: (7 Marks)**
*"Two pumps operate in parallel; the system delivers flow if at least one pump works. Flow reaches the process only if at least one of two valves (in series) is open AND the pipe is not blocked."*
*Given: $P_{PA} = 0.02$, $P_{PB} = 0.03$, $P_{VA} = 0.01$, $P_{VB} = 0.015$, $P_{block} = 0.005$*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the Boolean Structure (3 marks):**

- **Pumps (Parallel) = OR logic:**
$$M_1 = PA + PB$$

- **Valves (Series) — wait, re‑read: "at least one of two valves is open" → Parallel (OR):**
$$M_2 = VA + VB$$

- **Pipe NOT blocked:**
$$M_3 = \overline{BLOCK}$$

- **Top Event (Flow reaches process):**
$$T = M_1 \cdot M_2 \cdot M_3 = (PA + PB)(VA + VB)\overline{BLOCK}$$

**Step 2 — Expand (Optional, full Boolean):**

$$T = PA\cdot VA\cdot \bar{B} + PA\cdot VB\cdot \bar{B} + PB\cdot VA\cdot \bar{B} + PB\cdot VB\cdot \bar{B}$$

**Step 3 — Compute intermediate probabilities (2 marks):**

$$P(M_1) = 1 - (1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.03) = 1 - (0.98 \times 0.97) = 1 - 0.9506 = 0.0494$$
$$P(M_2) = 1 - (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.015) = 1 - (0.99 \times 0.985) = 1 - 0.97515 = 0.02485$$
$$P(M_3) = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995$$

**Step 4 — Compute Top Event Probability (2 marks):**

$$P(T) = P(M_1) \cdot P(M_2) \cdot P(M_3) = 0.0494 \times 0.02485 \times 0.995$$
$$\boxed{P(T) = 1.2213 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00122 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 1.22 \times 10^{-3}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** If a student writes the valves as "in series" using AND logic, they lose 2 marks immediately. The phrase **"at least one of two valves is open"** is the unambiguous indicator of **OR (parallel)** logic.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH‑DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Logic Diagram (Module 1)**

- ✅ Logic Diagram = Boolean graphical model of accident causation.
- ✅ **Two flavours:** Fault Tree (deductive) and Event Tree (inductive).
- ✅ **AND gate** = all inputs needed → **series** failure logic.
- ✅ **OR gate** = any one input sufficient → **parallel/redundant** failure logic.
- ✅ **NOT gate** = complement of an event.
- ✅ Fault tree symbols: **Rectangle** (intermediate), **Circle** (basic), **Diamond** (undeveloped), **House** (external/normal), **Oval** (conditioning).
- ✅ Boolean AND probability: $P(A \cdot B) = P_A \cdot P_B$ (independent events).
- ✅ Boolean OR probability: $P(A + B) = P_A + P_B - P_AP_B$ (general form).
- ✅ Rare‑event approximation for OR: $P \approx P_A + P_B$ when $P < 0.1$.
- ✅ **Minimal Cut Set** = smallest failure group causing the top event.
- ✅ **Single‑event cut sets** = single‑point‑of‑failures → highest priority for safety improvement.
- ✅ **Voting systems (e.g., 2oo3)** balance safety and availability in Safety Instrumented Systems (IEC 61508/61511).
- ✅ **Independence assumption** must always be stated before applying Boolean formulas.
- ✅ **Birnbaum Importance:** A basic event with high derivative $\partial P_T / \partial P_i$ is most critical.
- ✅ **Event Tree** is constructed **left to right**; **Fault Tree** is constructed **top to bottom**.
- ✅ Always **draw the diagram AND write the Boolean expression** for full credit in KTU ESE.
- ✅ Common cause failures require the **beta‑factor model** ($\beta \in [0.01, 0.10]$ typical).

---

> **End of Module 1 — Logic Diagram Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> *Mapped to CO1: Apply deductive and inductive logic tools for hazard identification and risk quantification in process and industrial systems.*

topics.title: System Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**System Analysis** in the context of Advanced Safety Engineering is a structured, systematic, and disciplined engineering methodology used to study a system's structure, behavior, interactions, and failure pathways in order to identify, evaluate, and control hazards throughout the system's lifecycle. According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with international standards (IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 / OSHA Process Safety Management), system analysis decomposes an engineered system into its constituent subsystems, components, and human–machine interfaces, and then evaluates the relationships between them.

A *system* is defined as a set of interrelated elements (people, procedures, hardware, software, facilities) that collectively perform a defined function within an operational environment. System analysis defines:

- **System Boundaries** — the physical and logical perimeter separating the system from its environment.
- **Subsystem Decomposition** — breaking the parent system into manageable hierarchical units.
- **Functional Flow** — the sequence of operations that transforms inputs into outputs.
- **Failure Modes** — the manner in which a component or subsystem may fail to perform its intended function.
- **Hazard Coupling** — the propagation mechanism through which a hazard in one subsystem affects adjacent subsystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** System Analysis forms the foundational framework upon which all subsequent hazard identification, risk evaluation, and risk control techniques (FMEA, FTA, ETA, HAZOP) are built. Mastery of system decomposition is therefore a prerequisite for Modules 2 and 3.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an automobile as a "system." The car has many interacting parts — engine, brakes, steering, electrical wiring, and the driver. Each part is a subsystem. If you only check the engine (a single subsystem), you may miss a brake failure (another subsystem). But if you map the **whole system** — every connection, every input (driver actions, fuel, road), and every output (motion, exhaust, heat) — you can predict where failures might originate and how they cascade.

**System Analysis is exactly this mapping process for safety.** Just as a doctor maps the entire human body — bones, organs, blood vessels, nerves — before diagnosing a disease, a safety engineer maps the entire engineered system before diagnosing its hazards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** In the *Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)*, failure of a single subsystem (the vent gas scrubber) cascaded through the entire plant system because engineers had not performed a complete **system-level hazard analysis**. Modern System Analysis techniques are direct descendants of lessons learned from such catastrophes.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in System Safety Analysis

The following metrics are universally applied and are direct KTU high-yield items:

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** the expected time interval between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. Units: **hours**.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** the expected time until a non-repairable component fails. Units: **hours**.
- **Failure Rate ($\lambda$):** the frequency at which a component fails, expressed in failures per hour.
- **System Reliability $R(t)$:** the probability that a system performs its intended function under designated operating conditions for a specified time interval $[0, t]$.
- **System Unavailability $Q(t)$:** the probability that the system is NOT in a functioning state at time $t$.
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN):** used in FMEA, defined as $RPN = S \times O \times D$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

System Analysis is conducted in **five sequential engineering phases**:

**Phase 1 — System Definition**
- Identify the mission statement, objectives, and operational envelope of the system.
- Specify environmental conditions: temperature, pressure, humidity, vibration, human factors.
- Establish the **system boundaries** that distinguish internal components from external influences.

**Phase 2 — Functional Decomposition**
- Break down the system hierarchically: **System → Subsystem → Sub-subsystem → Component**.
- Define the function of each element (e.g., "valve V-101 regulates flow of cooling water").
- Document interfaces: electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, software, and human–machine.

**Phase 3 — Hazard Identification (HAZID)**
- Apply techniques such as **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)**, **What-If Analysis**, **Checklist Analysis**, and **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)**.
- For each hazard, document the cause, consequence, and existing safeguards.

**Phase 4 — Risk Evaluation**
- Estimate **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, and **Detection (D)** ratings.
- Calculate the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.
- Compare against an **Acceptable Risk Matrix** to determine whether mitigation is required.

**Phase 5 — Risk Control & Verification**
- Apply the **hierarchy of controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Control → Administrative Control → PPE.
- Re-analyze the system after control implementation to verify residual risk is tolerable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these steps?** The hierarchy ensures that engineers do not jump to solutions (e.g., PPE) without first understanding the *root cause*. This is a direct KTU board exam expectation under Course Outcome CO1 (Apply systems thinking to safety problems).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes all essential equations required for the KTU Module 1 examination.

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Boundary** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of survival up to time $t$ for constant failure rate | $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ |
| Unavailability | $Q(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Probability of failure at time $t$ | $0 \leq Q(t) \leq 1$ |
| Mean Time To Failure | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Average lifetime for non-repairable items | Hours |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} + MTTR$ | For repairable systems | Hours |
| Series Reliability | $R_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All components must function | $\vert R_{sys} \vert \leq \min(R_i)$ |
| Parallel Reliability | $R_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | At least one path must function | $R_{sys} \geq \max(R_i)$ |
| Fault Tree — AND Gate | $P_{top} = \prod P_i$ | All events must occur | Independent basic events |
| Fault Tree — OR Gate | $P_{top} = 1 - \prod (1-P_i)$ | Any event causes top event | Independent basic events |
| RPN (FMEA) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Severity $\times$ Occurrence $\times$ Detection | $S,O,D \in [1,10]$ |
| Cut-set Sum (rare event approx.) | $P_{top} \approx \sum P_i$ | For mutually exclusive cuts | First-order approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The series/parallel reliability equations are the mathematical foundation of **redundancy design** in safety-critical systems. Nuclear reactors, aircraft flight controls, and emergency shutdown (ESD) valves all rely on these equations to certify **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)**.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

System Analysis is universally applied in:

- **Process Industries** — Refineries, petrochemical plants, pharmaceutical manufacturing.
- **Aerospace** — Aircraft design and certification (FAA / EASA).
- **Nuclear Power** — Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA).
- **Automotive** — ISO 26262 functional safety.
- **Construction & Mining** — Job Safety Analysis (JSA) and strata control analysis.

In every domain, the same logic applies: *understand the system first, identify hazards, evaluate risk, then apply controls.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series System Reliability

Consider $n$ independent components arranged in a series configuration. The system functions **only if every component functions**.

**Step 1:** Let $R_i$ be the reliability of component $i$ (probability that it functions at time $t$).

**Step 2:** Since components are statistically independent, the joint probability of all components functioning simultaneously is the product of individual probabilities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{series}(t) &= P(C_1 \cap C_2 \cap \dots \cap C_n) \\
&= P(C_1) \cdot P(C_2) \cdot \dots \cdot P(C_n) \\
&= \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the exponential reliability model $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$:

$$
R_{series}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \dots + \lambda_n)t}
$$

**Step 4:** Hence the equivalent failure rate of a series system is the **sum of individual failure rates**:

$$
\lambda_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i
$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion: Adding components in series **always decreases** total system reliability. This is why critical safety functions require redundancy.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel System Reliability

For $n$ independent components arranged in parallel, the system functions if **at least one** component functions. The system fails only when **all** components fail.

**Step 1:** The unreliability of component $i$ is $Q_i = 1 - R_i$.

**Step 2:** The system fails when all components fail simultaneously. By independence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{parallel}(t) &= P(\overline{C_1} \cap \overline{C_2} \cap \dots \cap \overline{C_n}) \\
&= \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i(t) \\
&= \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i(t))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore the parallel system reliability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{parallel}(t) &= 1 - Q_{parallel}(t) \\
&= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i(t))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** For the special case of two identical components each with reliability $R$:

$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 2R - R^2
$$

**Step 5:** Engineering insight: Adding parallel redundant components **always increases** system reliability but at the cost of complexity and capital expenditure.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fault Tree OR-Gate Probability

The OR gate represents a logical condition where the top event occurs if **any** of the input events occur.

**Step 1:** Let $P_i$ be the probability of occurrence of basic event $i$ under the OR gate.

**Step 2:** The probability that none of the input events occur is the product of their non-occurrence probabilities:

$$
P(none) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)
$$

**Step 3:** The probability that the top event occurs is one minus the probability that none of the inputs occur:

$$
P_{OR} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)
$$

**Step 4:** For two events with $P_1 = 0.01$ and $P_2 = 0.02$:

$$
P_{OR} = 1 - (0.99)(0.98) = 1 - 0.9702 = 0.0298
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

The following matrix maps typical exam problem types to the equations used:

| **Problem Type** | **Primary Equation** | **Typical Given Data** | **What the Examiner Asks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Series system with components | $R = \prod R_i$ | Component reliabilities | Total system reliability |
| Parallel redundancy | $R = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$ | Component reliabilities, $n$ | Improvement from redundancy |
| OR-gate fault tree | $P = 1 - \prod(1-P_i)$ | Basic event probabilities | Top event probability |
| AND-gate fault tree | $P = \prod P_i$ | Basic event probabilities | Top event probability |
| RPN calculation | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Severity/Occurrence/Detection | Risk ranking & prioritization |
| Failure rate from MTTF | $\lambda = 1/MTTF$ | Lifetime data | Failure rate per hour |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A safety shutdown system has two pressure sensors in parallel. Each sensor has reliability $R = 0.95$. The system activates a valve that has reliability $R_v = 0.99$ in series with the sensor pair. Calculate total system reliability.

**Step 1 — Parallel sensor pair:**

$$
R_{pair} = 1 - (1 - 0.95)^2 = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975
$$

**Step 2 — Series combination with valve:**

$$
R_{total} = R_{pair} \times R_v = 0.9975 \times 0.99 = 0.987525
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on result:** The parallel redundancy raised the sensor section reliability from $0.95$ to $0.9975$, demonstrating the engineering value of redundancy in safety-instrumented systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Analysis Methodology Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Define System Mission and Boundaries]) --> nodeA[Functional Decomposition]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Identify Subsystems and Interfaces]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Select Hazard Identification Technique]
    nodeC --> nodeD{PHA / HAZOP / FMEA / FTA / ETA}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Document Hazards and Failure Modes]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Estimate Severity, Occurrence, Detection]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute RPN or Fault Tree Probability]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Risk Above Acceptable Threshold?}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Apply Hierarchy of Controls]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Verify Residual Risk]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeK[Accept and Document]
    nodeJ --> nodeL([System Certified Safe])
    nodeK --> nodeL
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of System Analysis

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SYS[Plant Boundary]
        nodeSYS[Process Plant System]
        nodeSYS --> nodeSUB1[Mechanical Subsystem]
        nodeSYS --> nodeSUB2[Electrical Subsystem]
        nodeSYS --> nodeSUB3[Instrumentation and Control]
        nodeSYS --> nodeSUB4[Human Operator Interface]
    end
    nodeSUB1 --> nodeINT1[Mechanical Interfaces]
    nodeSUB2 --> nodeINT2[Power and Signal Interfaces]
    nodeSUB3 --> nodeINT3[Sensor and Actuator Interfaces]
    nodeSUB4 --> nodeINT4[Human Machine Interfaces]
    nodeINT1 --> nodeHAZ[Hazard Identification Layer]
    nodeINT2 --> nodeHAZ
    nodeINT3 --> nodeHAZ
    nodeINT4 --> nodeHAZ
    nodeHAZ --> nodeRISK[Risk Evaluation Matrix]
    nodeRISK --> nodeCTRL[Control Recommendation Engine]
    nodeCTRL --> nodeOUT([Safe Operating State])
```

### 4.3 Series vs Parallel Reliability Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SER[Series Configuration]
        sA[A] --> sB[B] --> sC[C] --> sOUT[System Output]
    end
    subgraph PAR[Parallel Configuration]
        pA[A2] --> pOUT2[System Output]
        pB[B2] --> pOUT2
        pC[C2] --> pOUT2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the *Series* block, all three blocks must operate for the system to produce output — failure of any single block halts the system. In the *Parallel* block, the system produces output as long as at least one path is intact. This visual contrast is precisely what the KTU examiner tests in questions on redundancy design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define a *system* in the context of safety engineering. List any four elements that may constitute a system.

**Model Answer:**
A system is a set of interrelated and interacting elements designed to perform a defined function within an operational environment. The four elements are:

1. **People** — operators, maintenance crew, supervisors.
2. **Procedures** — standard operating procedures, emergency plans.
3. **Hardware** — equipment, piping, vessels, instruments.
4. **Software** — process control logic, safety interlocks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN)? Write its formula and explain how it is used in FMEA.

**Model Answer:**
The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a numerical measure used in Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) to prioritize failure modes for corrective action. Its formula is:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

where $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection ranking. Each parameter is rated on a scale of 1 to 10. A higher RPN indicates greater risk and requires priority attention. Typically RPN values above **100** trigger mandatory corrective action in industry practice.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students lose 3 to 4 marks by **(a)** confusing series and parallel reliability formulas, **(b)** failing to state the assumption of **statistical independence**, and **(c)** not writing the units of the final answer. Always state assumptions explicitly and present numerical answers with units.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the five-step methodology of System Analysis as applied to safety engineering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety system consists of three identical components in series, each with reliability $R = 0.9$. Compute the system reliability. If the engineer introduces a parallel redundant component for one of the elements, recompute the new system reliability. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Five-Step System Analysis:**

1. **System Definition:** Identify mission, boundaries, environment, operational envelope.
2. **Functional Decomposition:** Break system into subsystems and components. Document functions and interfaces.
3. **Hazard Identification (HAZID):** Apply PHA, HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, ETA. List hazards with causes and consequences.
4. **Risk Evaluation:** Estimate $S$, $O$, $D$. Compute RPN or fault tree probability. Compare with acceptable risk criteria.
5. **Risk Control & Verification:** Apply the hierarchy of controls. Re-analyze to verify that residual risk is tolerable.

> **[Mark allocation: 1 mark each step + 2 marks for the block diagram]**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Original series system reliability:**

$$
R_{original} = R \times R \times R = 0.9 \times 0.9 \times 0.9 = 0.729
$$

**Step 2 — Modified configuration:** Two of the three components are now in series, and the third has been replaced by a parallel pair (two identical $R = 0.9$ components).

**Step 3 — Reliability of the parallel pair:**

$$
R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - 0.9)^2 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99
$$

**Step 4 — New total series reliability:**

$$
R_{new} = 0.9 \times 0.9 \times 0.99 = 0.8019
$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** The redundancy improved system reliability from **0.729 to 0.8019** (an increase of approximately **10.04%**). This demonstrates the engineering principle that **redundancy is the most effective technique to enhance safety-critical system reliability**.

> **[Mark allocation: Step 1 = 2 marks, Step 2 = 1 mark, Step 3 = 2 marks, Step 4 = 1 mark, Step 5 = 1 mark]**

---
