2. **What is the credible overpressure scenario?** (Fire, blocked outlet, runaway reaction, thermal expansion, control valve failure)
3. **What is the consequence of release?** (Flammable, toxic, environmental)

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU-favorite analytical hook: *"Why is a rupture disc preferred over a safety valve for a reactor handling fuming nitric acid?"* — Answer: Corrosive vapors would damage the valve seating surfaces during normal leakage; a rupture disc isolates the process fluid from the metal internals.

### 2.3 Set Pressure, Accumulation, and Blowdown — The "Operating Envelope"

The PRD does **not operate at MAWP**. There is a deliberately engineered **operating envelope**:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (API 520) | Real-World Meaning |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Maximum Allowable Working Pressure | MAWP | Vessel design pressure | Hard limit — vessel must never exceed this for long |
| Set Pressure | P_set | MAWP × 1.03 (single, non-fire) | Pressure at which PRD begins to lift |
| Accumulation (Overpressure) | ΔP_acc | 10% (fire) / 21% (non-fire) | Maximum pressure above MAWP during relief |
| Maximum Allowable Accumulated Pressure | MAAP | MAWP + ΔP_acc × MAWP | PRD must limit pressure to ≤ MAAP |
| Reseat Pressure | P_reseat | P_set − Blowdown | Pressure at which PRD recloses |
| Blowdown | BD | 2% – 7% (process), 2% – 5% (steam) | Hysteresis gap to prevent chattering |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are aligned with **API STD 520 Part I (Sizing & Selection)** and **API STD 521 (Relief Sizing for Process Vapors & Liquids)** — the de-facto KTU-referenced standards.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Application | Unit Notes |
|---|------------------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot \sqrt{P_1 \cdot \rho_1}}}$ | Effective discharge area (English units, US Gal/min) | $W$: lbs/hr, $A$: in² |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{13{,}160 \cdot W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{MW / (T_1 \cdot Z))}$ | Gas/vapor relief area (SI units) | $A$: mm², $P_1$: kPa(a), $T_1$: K |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{W}{8.81 \cdot K_d \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot \sqrt{\rho_1 \cdot \Delta P}}$ | Liquid relief (modified Bernoulli) | $A$: m², $W$: kg/hr, $\Delta P$: kPa |
| 4 | $A = \dfrac{W}{7.6 \cdot (1 + 0.00065 \cdot P_{set}) \cdot K_d \cdot K_{sh} \cdot K_n \cdot P_{set}}$ | Steam relief (ASME Section I) | $A$: mm², $W$: kg/hr, $P_{set}$: kPa(g) |
| 5 | $P_c = P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\dfrac{k}{k-1}}$ | Critical flow pressure (choked flow) | $P_c$: critical pressure, $k = C_p / C_v$ |
| 6 | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho}$ | Volumetric flow through orifice | $Q$: m³/s |
| 7 | $BD(\%) = \dfrac{P_{set} - P_{reseat}}{P_{set}} \times 100$ | Blowdown calculation | Dimensionless % |
| 8 | $P_{set} = MAWP \times 1.03$ | Set pressure for non-fire (single PRD) | Bar(g) |

#### Where:
- $C$ = Gas constant coefficient (API 520)
- $K_d$ = Effective coefficient of discharge (typically 0.95 for vapors, 0.62 for liquids)
- $K_b$ = Back-pressure correction factor
- $K_c$ = Combination correction factor (rupture disc + valve)
- $K_v$ = Viscosity correction factor (≤ 1 if liquid is viscous)
- $K_{sh}$ = Superheat correction (steam)
- $Z$ = Compressibility factor
- $MW$ = Molecular weight

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemical Plants**: Every atmospheric and pressure distillation column, hydrocracker, and ethylene cracker has hundreds of PRDs sized for **fire case** (wetted surface × 34,500 BTU/hr·ft² heat release per API 521).
- **LNG Carriers**: Each cargo tank has **2 PRDs** with redundancy; one passive (rupture disc) and one active (safety valve) per **IGC Code**.
- **Boilers (ASME Sec. I)**: 100% capacity valves with set pressure ≤ MAWP.
- **Pharmaceutical Reactors**: Rupture discs in series with safety valves for toxic/pyrophoric reactants.
- **Ammonia Refrigeration**: Pressure Relief Valves on each vessel with **two independent full-capacity PRDs** per IIAR standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Relief Area for Gas/Vapor Service (API 520 Method)

#### Starting Principle: Compressible Flow Through an Orifice

For an ideal gas flowing isentropically through a sharp-edged orifice, the mass flow rate is derived from the **steady-state energy equation** and the **equation of state**.

**Step 1 — Write the mass flow rate through a nozzle/orifice:**

The general compressible flow equation (used as the starting point by API 520) is:

$$W = C \cdot A \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{MW}{T_1 \cdot Z}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot k}{k-1} \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{2}{k}} - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k+1}{k}}\right]}$$

*Explanation:* The term inside the second square root is the **mass flux function**. It depends on the pressure ratio $\beta = P_2/P_1$ and the specific heat ratio $k$. The coefficient $C$ in API 520 is a constant (13,160 in SI units, 3150 in US units) chosen so that with $A$ in mm², $P$ in kPa, $T$ in K, $W$ comes out in kg/hr.

**Step 2 — Identify Critical (Choked) Flow Condition:**

For compressible flow, the maximum mass flux occurs when the exit velocity reaches the local **sonic velocity**. This happens when the downstream pressure $P_2$ falls below a critical value $P_c$:

$$P_c = P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\dfrac{k}{k-1}}$$

*Explanation:* Setting $\dfrac{dW}{dP_2} = 0$ yields the critical pressure ratio. For air ($k = 1.4$), $P_c/P_1 = 0.528$. For hydrocarbon vapors with $k \approx 1.2$, $P_c/P_1 \approx 0.564$. **Once $P_2 \leq P_c$, the flow is choked** — further reduction in downstream pressure does not increase the mass flow rate.

**Step 3 — Substitute the Choked Condition:**

Insert $P_2 = P_c$ into the flow equation. The bracketed term simplifies to a single constant depending only on $k$:

$$\dfrac{2 \cdot k}{k-1} \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{P_c}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{2}{k}} - \left(\dfrac{P_c}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k+1}{k}}\right] = \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\tfrac{k+1}{k-1}}$$

*Explanation:* This algebraic simplification removes the pressure-ratio dependence, leaving a function of $k$ alone. This is the foundation of the "choked flow" assumption in API 520 — for most overpressure scenarios, the pressure drop is so large that the flow is guaranteed sonic.

**Step 4 — Combine the Constants into the API 520 Coefficient:**

The bracketed term is absorbed into the constant $C$, giving the **working design equation** for gas/vapor relief:

$$A = \dfrac{13{,}160 \cdot W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{MW / (T_1 \cdot Z))}$$

*Explanation:* This is the equation given in the Formula Sheet (row #2). It is solved for the **minimum required discharge area $A$**, which the engineer then matches to the **next-larger standard API orifice size** (designated D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T).

**Step 5 — Match to a Standard Orifice:**

The standard API orifice areas are:

| API Orifice | Area (mm²) | Area (in²) |
|-------------|------------|------------|
| D | 126.7 | 0.196 |
| E | 253.5 | 0.393 |
| F | 506.7 | 0.785 |
| G | 829.4 | 1.287 |
| H | 1267.7 | 1.973 |
| J | 1985.0 | 3.077 |
| K | 3019.0 | 4.687 |
| L | 4161.0 | 6.450 |
| M | 5019.0 | 7.780 |
| N | 6590.0 | 10.210 |
| P | 12,393 | 19.20 |
| Q | 16,580 | 25.70 |
| R | 25,803 | 40.00 |
| T | 33,225 | 51.50 |

#### Step-by-Step Numerical Example

**Problem:** A hydrocarbon vapor relief scenario requires $W = 15{,}000$ kg/hr. The fluid has $MW = 50$ kg/kmol, $T_1 = 350$ K, $Z = 0.9$, $P_1 = 200$ kPa(a). Set pressure is 200 kPa(g). Use $K_d = 0.95$, $K_b = 1.0$, $K_c = 1.0$, $k = 1.2$.

**Step 1:** Compute $C$ from $k$ (interpolate API 520 Table 1):

For $k = 1.2$: $C \approx 325$ (US units, from API 520 charts, or 300 in some editions).

**Step 2:** Plug into the design equation:

$$A = \dfrac{13{,}160 \times 15{,}000}{325 \times 0.95 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 200 \times \sqrt{50 / (350 \times 0.9)}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate denominator term-by-term:

$$\sqrt{50 / (350 \times 0.9)} = \sqrt{50 / 315} = \sqrt{0.1587} = 0.3984$$

$$A = \dfrac{1.974 \times 10^{8}}{325 \times 0.95 \times 200 \times 0.3984}$$

**Step 4:** Combine the denominator:

$$325 \times 0.95 = 308.75$$
$$308.75 \times 200 = 61{,}750$$
$$61{,}750 \times 0.3984 = 24{,}606.6$$

**Step 5:** Final area:

$$A = \dfrac{1.974 \times 10^{8}}{24{,}606.6} = 8022 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6:** Select the next-larger standard API orifice: **P** (12,393 mm²). The selected valve has capacity more than required — the relief load is fully handled.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right PRD

| Service Condition | Recommended Device | Reason (Why) |
|-------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Saturated steam boiler | ASME Section I pop safety valve | Code-mandated; high temp; pop action |
| Hydrocarbon vapor in column | Spring-loaded safety relief valve (balanced bellows) | Pop action; back-pressure tolerance |
| Viscous liquid in pump discharge | Modulating relief valve | Avoids surge; gradual opening |
| Chlorine / HCl reactor | Rupture disc + safety valve in series | Corrosion protection; tight leakage |
| Storage tank (atmospheric → low pressure) | Pressure-vacuum relief valve (conservation vent) | API 2000; prevents vacuum collapse |
| High-pressure ethylene reactor | Pilot-operated safety valve | High set pressure; large orifice |
| Fire-case on atmospheric tank | Fusible plug (or rupture disc) | Activated only by temperature |
| Polymerizing monomer service | Rupture disc (gauge-changeable) | Plugging resistance; isolation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PRD Sizing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Identify Worst-Case Overpressure Scenario] --> B0[Define Cause: Fire, Blocked Outlet, Runaway, Thermal Expansion]
    B0 --> C0[Determine Relieving Conditions: T1, P1, MW, Z, Phase]
    C0 --> D0[Calculate Required Relieving Rate W kg/hr or m3/hr]
    D0 --> E0[Select PRD Type: Safety Valve / Relief Valve / Rupture Disc / Pilot-Operated]
    E0 --> F0{Service is Liquid or Vapor/Steam?}
    F0 -->|Liquid| G0[Apply API 520 Eqn: A = W / 8.81 Kd Kv Kp sqrt rho1 dP]
    F0 -->|Vapor or Steam| H0[Apply API 520 Eqn: A = 13160 W / C Kd Kb Kc P1 sqrt MW T1 Z)]
    G0 --> I0[Compute Blowdown and Set Pressure]
    H0 --> I0[Compute Blowdown and Set Pressure]
    I0 --> J0[Match to Next-Larger API Orifice D-T]
    J0 --> K0[Verify Reseat Pressure and Seat Tightness]
    K0 --> L0[Size Inlet and Outlet Piping: Nozzle Loss less than 3 percent Pset]
    L0 --> M0[Design Discharge System: Flare, Scrubber, Safe Vent]
    M0 --> N0[Generate PRD Sizing Datasheet: Doc per ASME Sec VIII]
    N0 --> O0[Periodic Testing and Revalidation per API STD 576]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Spring-Loaded Safety Relief Valve Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Process Inlet: Flanged Nozzle] --> B1[Disc Holder and Disc]
    B1 --> C1[Lift Pallet and Spindle]
    C1 --> D1[Spring Compression Assembly]
    D1 --> E1[Adjustment Screw: Sets Spring Force]
    E1 --> F1[Body and Bonnet: Pressure Containing Envelope]
    F1 --> G1[Outlet Flange: Routes to Manifold or Flare]
    G1 --> H1[Lift Lever: Manual Testing without Pressurizing]
    H1 --> I1[Test Gag: Lock for Inspection]
    F1 --> J1[Inlet Side: Seating Surfaces Resists Backpressure]
    F1 --> K1[Bellows: Cancels Variable Backpressure if Installed]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Pressure Protection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Layer 1: Inherently Safer Design] --> B2[Layer 2: Process Control and SIS]
    B2 --> C2[Layer 3: Mechanical Design for Full Overpressure]
    C2 --> D2[Layer 4: Active PRD: Safety Relief Valve]
    D2 --> E2[Layer 5: Passive PRD: Rupture Disc]
    E2 --> F2[Layer 6: Fire Protection and Bunding]
    F2 --> G2[Layer 7: Emergency Response and Evacuation]
    G2 --> H2[Final Defense: PPE for Personnel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each block in the hierarchy represents an **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** as defined in **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis, IEC 61511)**. A PRD is a **single IPL** — failure of the PRD itself (e.g., stuck open, leakage) must be considered in the LOPA as one layer of protection consumed. The student should always ask: *"How many IPLs remain if the PRD fails?"*

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Q1a]**

**Q1.** Define a **Pressure Relief Device (PRD)**. List the four main types of PRDs used in process industries.

**Model Answer:**
A Pressure Relief Device is a passive safety instrument designed to **automatically discharge fluid from a pressurized system** when the pressure exceeds a predetermined set point, thereby preventing catastrophic overpressure failure of the vessel or piping. It operates as the **last engineering control** in the layer of protection hierarchy.

**The four main types are:**
1. **Spring-loaded safety relief valve** (pop action for vapors, modulating for liquids)
2. **Rupture disc** (non-reclosing, single-use)
3. **Pilot-operated safety valve (POSV)**
4. **Pressure-vacuum relief valve** (for atmospheric / low-pressure storage tanks per API 2000)

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing with classification, 1 mark for the role in the protection hierarchy)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 2, Q1c]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **Set Pressure**, **Blowdown**, and **Accumulation** in the context of a safety relief valve. Why is blowdown a critical design parameter?

**Model Answer:**
- **Set Pressure (P_set):** The inlet gauge pressure at which the valve begins to open. For a single PRD on a non-fire case, **P_set = MAWP × 1.03** per API 520.
- **Blowdown:** The difference between set pressure and reseat pressure, expressed as a percentage: $\text{BD}(\%) = (P_{set} - P_{reseat})/P_{set} \times 100$.
- **Accumulation:** The pressure rise **above MAWP** that the vessel can tolerate during relief — **10% for fire, 21% for non-fire** with a single PRD.

**Why blowdown is critical:** Excessive blowdown wastes fluid and energy; insufficient blowdown causes **chattering** (rapid open-close cycles) which damages the seat and causes fatigue failure. Typical design: 2–5% for steam, 2–7% for process services.

(3 marks: 1 mark each for the three definitions, optional 1 bonus mark for the chattering explanation)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (ESE Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — KTU Format: Part (a) 7 marks + Part (b) 7 marks

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Q2A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **working of a spring-loaded safety relief valve**. What is the function of a **bellows** in a balanced-bellows relief valve?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vertical pressure vessel contains propane ($MW = 44$) at $T_1 = 350$ K, $P_1 = 1200$ kPa(a), $Z = 0.85$. The required relieving rate for the fire case is **$W = 35{,}000$ kg/hr**. The specific heat ratio $k = 1.13$. Use API 520. Determine the **required effective discharge area** and **select the next-larger standard API orifice**. Assume $K_d = 0.95$, $K_b = 1.0$, $K_c = 1.0$.

---

**Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Working of a Spring-Loaded Safety Relief Valve:**

A spring-loaded safety relief valve has four functional subassemblies:

1. **Body and Bonnet**: Pressure-containing enclosure with inlet (process) and outlet (discharge) flanges.
2. **Disc and Seat**: A precision-lapped disc sits on a nozzle seat. The **seating force** is the sum of the **process pressure × disc area** acting upward and the **spring force** acting downward.
3. **Spring and Adjustment Screw**: The spring applies a calibrated downward force. Turning the **adjustment screw** compresses or relaxes the spring, setting the **set pressure**.
4. **Lift Mechanism (Spindle + Pallet)**: When the inlet pressure exceeds the spring force × the disc area, the disc lifts, the fluid escapes, and the valve discharges.

**Operating Cycle:**
- *Normal:* Pressure < P_set → Spring force > Pressure force → Valve closed.
- *Relief:* Pressure ≥ P_set → Lifting force ≥ Spring force → Disc lifts → Fluid discharges.
- *Reseat:* Pressure drops below reseat pressure → Spring reseats disc → Valve recloses.

**Function of a Bellows in a Balanced-Bellows Design:**

The metallic bellows is welded between the disc and the body, forming a sealed chamber. Its functions are:

1. **Isolates the spring chamber from the process fluid** — prevents corrosive, hot, or polymerizing fluids from entering the spring.
2. **Cancels variable back-pressure effects** — when the valve discharges into a common manifold (e.g., a flare header), back-pressure varies. The bellows transmits this back-pressure to the top side of the disc, **cancelling it** so the set pressure is unaffected.
3. **Prevents seat leakage** from the spring side.

A simple block diagram is shown in **Section 4.2** above.

(Marks split: 4 marks for working description with sub-parts, 2 marks for the cycle logic, 1 mark for the bellows function)

---

**Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation:**
For choked vapor flow in API 520 SI units:

$$A = \dfrac{13{,}160 \cdot W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{MW / (T_1 \cdot Z))}$$

**Step 2 — Find the Coefficient C from k = 1.13:**

From API 520 Table 1 (or standard correlation): for $k = 1.13$, $C \approx 320$ (SI units). Some references yield 322. We use **C = 320**.

**Step 3 — Substitute All Values:**

- $W = 35{,}000$ kg/hr
- $C = 320$
- $K_d = 0.95$
- $K_b = 1.0$
- $K_c = 1.0$
- $P_1 = 1200$ kPa(a)
- $MW = 44$ kg/kmol
- $T_1 = 350$ K
- $Z = 0.85$

Compute the term inside the square root:

$$\dfrac{MW}{T_1 \cdot Z} = \dfrac{44}{350 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{44}{297.5} = 0.1479$$

$$\sqrt{0.1479} = 0.3846$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Denominator:**

$$320 \times 0.95 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1200 \times 0.3846$$

- $320 \times 0.95 = 304$
- $304 \times 1200 = 364{,}800$
- $364{,}800 \times 0.3846 = 140{,}302.1$

**Step 5 — Compute the Required Area:**

$$A = \dfrac{13{,}160 \times 35{,}000}{140{,}302.1} = \dfrac{4.606 \times 10^{8}}{1.403 \times 10^{5}} \approx 3283 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Select the Next-Larger Standard API Orifice:**

From the table in Section 3.1:
- L = 4161 mm² (next larger than 3283)
- M = 5019 mm²

**Selected: API Orifice "L" (4161 mm² or 6.45 in²)**

**Conclusion:** The required valve has an effective discharge area of **3283 mm²**, and we select a **standard API L orifice** (4161 mm²), giving a design margin of about **26.7%** over the minimum required.

(Marks split: 2 marks for the correct equation, 1 mark for C-value, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for evaluation, 1 mark for orifice selection, 1 mark for conclusion)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — KTU Internal Choice Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 2, Q2B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **API 521 fire case sizing methodology** for a pressure vessel. What is the **wetted surface area**? How is the required relief rate $W$ computed?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal cylindrical storage vessel of **length 6 m** and **diameter 2.5 m** is protected against the **fire case** by two PRDs. The vessel contains **methanol** with latent heat of vaporization $L = 1100$ kJ/kg. Use the API 521 correlation $Q = 43{,}200 \cdot F \cdot A_w^{0.82}$ (W) with environmental factor $F = 1.0$ for full water spray. Compute the required relief rate $W$ and the required effective discharge area (in mm²) per valve, given $C = 320$, $K_d = 0.95$, $P_1 = 110$ kPa(a), $T_1 = 340$ K, $MW = 32$, $Z = 0.9$, $k = 1.2$.

---

**Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Fire Case Sizing (API 521):**
The **fire case** assumes the vessel is engulfed in a pool fire or jet fire. The PRD must relieve enough vapor to **discharge all the heat absorbed from the fire** so that the liquid does not generate excess vapor pressure.

**Wetted Surface Area ($A_w$):**
The portion of the vessel external surface in contact with liquid at the relief-stabilized level (typically the lower 1/3 of the vessel for horizontal tanks; for vertical vessels, the total wetted area up to 4.5 m above grade per API 521 §4.4.13).

**API 521 Heat Absorption Formula:**

$$Q = 43{,}200 \cdot F \cdot A_w^{0.82}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Total heat absorbed (in watts, W)
- $F$ = Environmental factor (1.0 for full water spray, 0.5 for dry, 0.3 for de-rated)
- $A_w$ = Wetted area in m²

**Required Relief Rate:**

$$W = \dfrac{Q}{L} = \dfrac{43{,}200 \cdot F \cdot A_w^{0.82}}{L}$$

Where $L$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg or kJ/kg × 1000).

**Process steps (API 521 Fire Case Methodology):**
1. Define the **fire scenario** (pool fire, jet fire, fire engulfment).
2. Compute the **wetted surface area $A_w$** at the relief-stabilized level.
3. Apply the heat absorption formula to get $Q$.
4. Divide by latent heat to get $W$ (kg/s).
5. Check **10% accumulation** over MAWP.
6. Size each PRD for **100% of W** if there are two (each is full capacity, and the spare handles the blocked-outlet case).

(Marks split: 2 marks for concept, 2 marks for $A_w$ definition, 1.5 marks for $Q$ formula, 1.5 marks for the procedure)

---

**Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Wetted Surface Area:**

For a horizontal cylinder lying on its side, the wetted surface (assumed to be the **lower half** of the cylindrical shell plus both heads) is:

$$A_w = \pi \cdot D \cdot L + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 2$$

$$A_w = \pi \times 2.5 \times 6 + \pi \times 2.5^2 / 2$$

- Cylindrical shell (lower half): $\pi \times 2.5 \times 6 / 1$ (full wrap) — but assuming **full wrap wetted** for conservatism: $\pi \times 2.5 \times 6 = 47.12$ m²
- Two heads (hemispheres or 2:1 elliptical): $\pi D^2 = \pi \times 6.25 = 19.63$ m²

For simplicity, total external area: $A_w = 47.12 + 19.63 = 66.75$ m²

For the conservative fire case on a horizontal vessel, wetted area ≈ total external area of the shell.

**Step 2 — Compute Q (Heat Absorption):**

$$Q = 43{,}200 \times 1.0 \times 66.75^{0.82}$$

- $66.75^{0.82} = e^{0.82 \cdot \ln 66.75} = e^{0.82 \times 4.201} = e^{3.445} = 31.34$
- $Q = 43{,}200 \times 31.34 = 1{,}353{,}888$ W ≈ 1,354 kW

**Step 3 — Compute Relief Rate W (kg/s):**

$$W = \dfrac{Q}{L} = \dfrac{1{,}353{,}888}{1{,}100{,}000} = 1.231 \text{ kg/s}$$

Convert to kg/hr:

$$W = 1.231 \times 3600 = 4{,}432 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Each PRD Handles 100% of W (Spare Convention):**

Each PRD size for $W = 4{,}432$ kg/hr.

**Step 5 — Compute the Required Effective Area:**

$$A = \dfrac{13{,}160 \times 4{,}432}{320 \times 0.95 \times 110 \times \sqrt{32 / (340 \times 0.9))}}$$

- Inside the square root: $32 / (340 \times 0.9) = 32 / 306 = 0.1046$
- $\sqrt{0.1046} = 0.3234$
- Denominator: $320 \times 0.95 \times 110 \times 0.3234 = 304 \times 110 \times 0.3234 = 33{,}440 \times 0.3234 = 10{,}815$

$$A = \dfrac{13{,}160 \times 4{,}432}{10{,}815} = \dfrac{5.83 \times 10^{7}}{1.0815 \times 10^{4}} = 5{,}393 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Select API Orifice:**

From the standard table: **M = 5019 mm²** (next-larger) — fits within the design margin.

(Marks split: 1.5 marks for $A_w$, 1.5 marks for $Q$ and $W$, 1 mark for unit conversion, 2 marks for area calculation, 1 mark for orifice selection)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing the formula constants** (13,160 SI vs 3150 US) — using SI constants with US variables (or vice-versa) yields a wildly wrong answer. **Always state which system (SI or US) you are using before substituting.**
> 2. **Forgetting the spare PRD rule** — when sizing multiple PRDs, each must be sized for **100% of the required relief load**, not 50%. The spare handles the case when one PRD is blocked out for maintenance.
> 3. **Ignoring Z (compressibility factor)** — for hydrocarbon vapors at high pressure, $Z$ can drop to 0.7–0.85; ignoring it gives a non-conservative (too small) orifice.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert 10% accumulation to MAAP** for the relief condition — many students use MAWP instead of MAAP, which gives a smaller orifice and a non-code-compliant installation.
> 5. **Wrong $C$ value** — $C$ depends on $k$. Common values: $C = 315$ for $k=1.4$ (air), $C = 320$ for $k=1.2$ (propane), $C = 300$ for $k=1.0$ (steam approximation). Failing to look up the correct $C$ loses 1 full mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Pressure Relief Device** is the **last engineering control** before catastrophic failure; it does **not prevent** the cause, it **controls the consequence**.
- ✅ The **four main PRD types** are: (1) spring-loaded safety relief valve, (2) rupture disc, (3) pilot-operated safety valve, (4) pressure-vacuum relief valve.
- ✅ **Set pressure** = MAWP × 1.03 (single, non-fire); **Accumulation** = 10% (fire) or 21% (non-fire).
- ✅ **Blowdown** = 2–7% (process) / 2–5% (steam) — too low causes chattering, too high wastes fluid.
- ✅ **API 520 / API 521** are the de-facto KTU-referenced sizing standards.
- ✅ Always match the calculated area to the **next-larger standard API orifice** (D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T).
- ✅ **Two-phase flow** (mixture of liquid and vapor) is the most complex case — use API 521 §5.3.1 homogeneous equilibrium or Leung's method for flashing services.
- ✅ **Each PRD in a multiple installation must be sized for 100%** of the required relief load.
- ✅ **Rupture discs** are preferred for corrosive, polymerizing, or toxic services and are often installed in series with safety valves.
- ✅ The **balanced-bellows** design cancels variable back-pressure in flare-header installations.
- ✅ **Discharge system design** (open vent vs closed flare) significantly affects back-pressure and must be analyzed in conjunction with the PRD selection.
- ✅ Periodic **testing and revalidation** per **API 576** is mandatory — a PRD that has not been tested in 5 years is considered unreliable per API 510 inspection code.
- ✅ **Failure modes** of a PRD include: **set pressure drift, leakage, chattering, sticking closed, lifting too early, lifting too late** — each must be considered in a LOPA study.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Instrumented System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Safety Instrumented System # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Safety Instrumented System (SIS) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is a distinct, engineered combination of sensors, logic solvers, and final elements designed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined, unsafe operating conditions are detected. The SIS is implemented as a separate, independent layer of protection (distinct from the Basic Process Control System — BPCS) and is governed primarily by the international standards **IEC 61508** (Functional Safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic Safety-Related Systems) and **IEC 61511** (Functional Safety — Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industry Sector). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > An SIS performs a *Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)*. A single SIS can execute one or more SIFs, each assigned a specific **Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1 to SIL 4)** that quantifies the required risk reduction. The core performance metric of an SIS is the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** — the likelihood that the system will fail to perform its safety function when demanded. The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** is simply the inverse: $$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Modern Car's Safety Ecosystem:** > Imagine driving a car at high speed. The *engine and accelerator* (analogous to the **BPCS**) control the car's normal operation. The **braking system** (analogous to the **SIS**) is a *separate, dedicated safety layer* that does NOT control cruising speed; it exists *only* to bring the car to a safe stop in an emergency (e.g., collision imminent, brake failure detected). The braking system has its own sensors (wheel-speed sensors, radar, cameras), its own logic (ABS/ESC ECU), and its own final elements (hydraulic brake actuators). Even if the main engine computer fails, the braking system must still work — that is **functional independence**. The "SIL rating" of the braking system is essentially the *engineering confidence* that it will work when you stomp the pedal in panic — the higher the SIL, the lower the chance it fails you. **Three Golden Rules of an SIS (Memorize for KTU):** 1. **Independence:** The SIS must be physically, logically, and functionally separate from the BPCS. 2. **Dedication:** Its sole purpose is safety; it does *not* perform regulatory or quality control. 3. **Reliability:** It must achieve a defined probability of working on demand (PFD) corresponding to the assigned SIL. ## 1.3 Why SIS Exists — The Hierarchy of Protection Modern process plants (oil & gas, chemical, pharmaceutical, power) rely on **multiple independent protection layers (IPLs)**. The SIS is *one specific layer* in the broader risk-reduction onion: | Protection Layer (Outer → Inner) | Function | Example | |---|---|---| | Community / Emergency Response | Mitigate consequences to the public | Fire brigades, evacuation plans | | Mechanical / Physical Design | Reduce frequency of incidents | Pressure relief valves, dikes, blast walls | | Operational Procedures | Human-based safeguards | Operating procedures, training, permits | | BPCS (Basic Process Control System) | Maintain normal operation within safe limits | PID controllers, DCS, SCADA | | **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** | **Bring process to safe state on demand** | **Emergency Shutdown (ESD), Fire & Gas (F&G)** | | Mechanical Relief Devices | Last-resort physical protection | Rupture discs, pressure safety valves (PSV) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The SIS is **NEVER** the first line of defense. Process design and BPCS must handle 99% of normal deviations. The SIS is the *last* automatic layer before mechanical relief. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an SIS — The Three Subsystems Every SIS, regardless of complexity, consists of exactly three architectural blocks connected in a safety loop: 1. **Sensors (Input Subsystem):** Detect the process variable (pressure, temperature, flow, level, gas concentration) and convert it to a signal. - Examples: Pressure transmitters, thermocouples, flame detectors, hydrocarbon gas detectors. 2. **Logic Solver (Brain):** Receives sensor signals, applies the safety logic (typically hard-wired relay logic or a Safety PLC like Siemens S7-F, ABB AC500-S, HIMA, Triconex), and decides whether to actuate the final element. 3. **Final Elements (Output / Actuators):** Physically bring the process to a safe state. - Examples: Solenoid valves (on shutdown valves), circuit breakers, motor starters, firewater pump starters. > [!NOTE] > A *single* SIF is a chain: **Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element**. The total PFD of the SIF is the sum of the PFDs of these three sub-blocks (in low-demand mode of operation). ## 2.2 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) — The KTU Core Concept **SIL** is a *discrete level* (1, 2, 3, or 4) for specifying the safety integrity requirements of a SIF. The higher the SIL, the lower the probability of failure, and hence the higher the risk reduction. ### 2.2.1 SIL Targets for Low-Demand Mode of Operation In low-demand mode, the safety function is demanded *no more than once per year*. The target is expressed as the **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD avg)**. | SIL | Target PFD avg | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) | Equivalent Decimal | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **1** | $\ge 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | **100 to 10** | 0.1 – 0.01 | | **2** | $\ge 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | **1,000 to 100** | 0.01 – 0.001 | | **3** | $\ge 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | **10,000 to 1,000** | 0.001 – 0.0001 | | **4** | $\ge 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | **100,000 to 10,000** | 0.0001 – 0.00001 | > [!IMPORTANT] > Process industries typically operate at **SIL 1, 2, or 3 only**. **SIL 4** is reserved for extremely high-consequence systems (e.g., nuclear reactor trip) and is rarely applied in oil & gas / chemical plants. ### 2.2.2 SIL Targets for High-Demand / Continuous Mode If the SIF is exercised more than once per year, the metric becomes the **Probability of a dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH)**. | SIL | Target PFH (per hour) | |:---:|:---:| | **1** | $\ge 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | | **2** | $\ge 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ | | **3** | $\ge 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ | | **4** | $\ge 10^{-9}$ to $< 10^{-8}$ | ## 2.3 Failure Categories — The Foundation of PFD Math Every component in an SIS can fail in two ways: - **Safe Failure (S):** The component fails in a way that *causes* a spurious shutdown (false trip) — e.g., a pressure transmitter's diaphragm ruptures and reads "high" → unnecessary plant trip. These do not directly compromise safety, but cost money and production loss. - **Dangerous Failure (D):** The component fails such that it *cannot* perform its safety function when demanded. This is what PFD measures. - **Dangerous Detected (DD):** Diagnosed by automatic self-test (e.g., by a smart transmitter with diagnostics). - **Dangerous Undetected (DU):** No automatic detection — only found via proof testing. The **dangerous failure rate** of a component is denoted **λ_D**, split as **λ_DD** and **λ_DU**, usually expressed in *per hour* or *per year*. Similarly, the **safe failure rate λ_S** contributes to the **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**, a key SIL design parameter: $$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$ ## 2.4 The Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511 Framework) The standard prescribes a *phase-based* lifecycle to ensure functional safety is designed in, not bolted on. The 11 phases include: 1. **Process Hazard & Risk Assessment** (HAZOP, LOPA, What-If) → identify SIFs. 2. **Allocation of safety functions** to protection layers (decide which layer carries the residual risk). 3. **SIS Safety Requirement Specification (SRS)** — the document that defines every SIF, its SIL, response time, proof-test interval, etc. 4. **SIS Design & Engineering** (architecture, redundancy). 5. **Installation, Commissioning & Validation.** 6. **Operation, Maintenance & Proof Testing** (the operational phase — most field failures are DU, found only at proof test). 7. **Modification Management** (any change requires a Management of Change — MOC). 8. **Decommissioning.** ## 2.5 Proof Testing — The Hidden Backbone of SIS Reliability A proof test is a *periodic, manual or automatic test* performed to reveal dangerous undetected (DU) failures and restore the SIF to "as-new" condition. The **Proof Test Interval (T_proof)** directly affects the average PFD: - **Long T_proof** → DU failures accumulate → PFD avg rises → SIF may drop out of its SIL target. - **Short T_proof** → better PFD avg → but increased plant downtime and maintenance cost. This trade-off is captured by the equation (single channel, 1oo1): $$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$$ This linear dependency on T_proof is a KTU favourite — **doubling the proof-test interval doubles the average PFD**. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are the **minimum** you must memorize for any SIS question in the KTU 2024 ESE. | # | Formula | Meaning / Variable Definitions | Units | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor (dimensionless) | — | | 2 | $\text{SIL} \leftrightarrow \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ band | See SIL table above | — | | 3 | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Safe Failure Fraction | fraction (0–1) | | 4 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ | Single channel PFD avg | dimensionless | | 5 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}})^2}{6}$ | 1-out-of-2 redundant (voted) PFD avg | dimensionless | | 6 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo2}} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ | 2-out-of-2 (both must trip) PFD avg | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Final Element}}$ | Total loop PFD (in low-demand mode) | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours | | 9 | $\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | Total dangerous failure rate | per hour | | 10 | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Diagnostic Coverage (fraction of dangerous failures auto-detected) | fraction (0–1) | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility SIS is the cornerstone of *functional safety* in: - **Oil & Gas:** Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD), Fire & Gas (F&G) systems, HIPPS (High Integrity Pressure Protection Systems). - **Chemicals:** Reactor temperature/pressure interlocks. - **Power Plants:** Turbine overspeed trip, boiler furnace purge interlocks. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Sterilizer overpressure protection. - **Rail/Aviation:** Train Automatic Train Protection (ATP), aircraft autopilot envelopes. In every one of these domains, regulators (OSHA, HSE-UK, PESO-India) **legally require** that an SIS be designed, validated, and maintained per IEC 61511, with auditable documentation of SIL verification. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of PFD for a 1oo1 (Single Channel) Subsystem **Scenario:** A single sensor with dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ and proof test interval $T$ (hours). We want the *average* probability it will fail to act on demand over its entire operational cycle. **Step 1 — Define the instantaneous unreliability.** If the component is "as good as new" right after a proof test, and the DU failure rate is constant, the probability of having *failed dangerously* but *not yet been tested* at time $t$ after the test is: $$Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \approx \lambda_{DU} t$$ (The exponential is linearized for small $\lambda_{DU} t$, which is the industry standard assumption in functional safety.) **Step 2 — Define the average probability over one test cycle.** The PFD is not constant — it is zero just after a proof test and rises linearly. The average value over one cycle is the integral divided by the cycle length: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt$$ **Step 3 — Perform the integration.** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T} \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the limits.** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T} \cdot \frac{T^2}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify.** $$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg (1oo1)}} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2}}$$ This is Equation 4 in the cheat sheet — a KTU high-yield result. ## 3.2 Derivation of PFD for 1oo2 Architecture **Scenario:** Two identical channels in parallel; the system succeeds if *at least one* channel trips (1-out-of-2 voting). Both channels must fail dangerously to cause a system failure. **Step 1 — Joint failure probability of two independent channels.** For two independent channels with instantaneous unreliabilities $Q_1$ and $Q_2$: $$Q_{\text{system}} = Q_1 \cdot Q_2$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the linearized expression.** $$Q_{\text{system}}(t) = (\lambda_{DU} t) \cdot (\lambda_{DU} t) = (\lambda_{DU} t)^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute the average over the test interval.** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (\lambda_{DU} t)^2 \, dt$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^{2}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} t^2 \, dt = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^{2}}{T} \cdot \frac{T^3}{3}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify.** $$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg (1oo2)}} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}})^2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{\text{proof}})^2}{6}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** A 1oo2 system is *vastly* safer than 1oo1 (quadratic vs. linear in T). This is why redundant, voted architectures are used for SIL 2 and SIL 3 SIFs. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem) > **Question:** A pressure transmitter has $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour. The SIF uses a 1oo2 voted sensor pair, a logic solver with $\text{PFD}_{\text{LS}} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$, and a single shut-down valve with $\text{PFD}_{\text{SDV}} = 2 \times 10^{-2}$. The proof test interval is $T = 1$ year ($8760$ hours). Determine (a) the SIL achieved by the sensor subsystem, (b) the total PFD of the SIF, and (c) the overall SIL of the SIF. **Step 1 — Compute the sensor PFD (1oo2).** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T)^2}{6} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760)^2}{6}$$ Compute the inside: $$\lambda_{DU} T = 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$$ Square it: $$(\lambda_{DU} T)^2 = (4.38 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.918 \times 10^{-5}$$ Divide by 6: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} = \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-5}}{6} \approx 3.197 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the SIL of the sensor.** Since $10^{-6} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} < 10^{-5}$, the sensor subsystem alone meets **SIL 3**. **Step 3 — Sum the PFDs of the three sub-blocks (low-demand mode).** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{LS}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{SDV}}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = 3.2 \times 10^{-6} + 1 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-2}$$ **Step 4 — Identify the SIF's overall SIL.** Since $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} < 10^{-1}$, the SIF as a whole achieves **SIL 1**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU:** The SIF's SIL is dictated by its **weakest (highest PFD) sub-block**. A brilliant SIL 3 sensor pair is useless if the final element (the shut-down valve) is only SIL 0 / SIL 1. This is why *balanced design* across all three sub-systems is the cardinal rule of SIS engineering. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Architecture Comparison (Same Components, Same T) Using $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /hr and $T = 8760$ hr (1 year): | Architecture | PFD Formula | Numerical PFD | SIL Achieved | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1oo1 (Single) | $\lambda_{DU} T / 2$ | $2.19 \times 10^{-3}$ | SIL 2 | | 1oo2 (Redundant voted) | $(\lambda_{DU} T)^2 / 6$ | $3.20 \times 10^{-6}$ | SIL 3 | | 2oo2 (Both must trip) | $2 \cdot \lambda_{DU} T / 2$ | $4.38 \times 10^{-3}$ | SIL 2 (worse!) | | 2oo3 (Two-out-of-Three) | $6 \cdot (\lambda_{DU} T)^2 / 6$ | $1.15 \times 10^{-5}$ | SIL 3 | > [!NOTE] > **1oo2 vs 2oo2:** Identical hardware, but *opposite* voting. 1oo2 (more available, more available for safe trip) has *much better PFD*; 2oo2 (more secure against spurious trip) has *worse PFD*. This is a classic KTU trick question. ## 3.5 SIL Determination by Risk Graph (Conceptual Derivation) The IEC 61511 **Risk Graph** method determines the required SIL by stepping through four parameters, each with 2–5 levels: - **Parameter W:** Consequence severity (Minor injury / Serious injury / Death / Many deaths / Catastrophic). - **Parameter F:** Frequency of exposure to the hazard (Rare to Frequent). - **Parameter P:** Possibility of avoidance (Possible under certain conditions / Almost impossible). - **Parameter C — Demand rate** combined with **probability of ignition / escalation**. The risk graph starts at a base SIL (depending on W) and *modifies* it upward (or downward) based on the other parameters. The output is the **required SIL** that the SIF must achieve. The mathematical relationship used by risk graph tools to convert the qualitative answer into PFD is simply the **lookup table in Section 2.2.1** of these notes — there is no closed-form algebraic equation; the risk graph is a *calibrated decision matrix*, not a derivation. ## 3.6 LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) — Semi-Quantitative Path LOPA is a *simplified* risk assessment where each Independent Protection Layer (IPL) is assigned a **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD_IPL)**. The total mitigated event frequency is: $$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{IPL,i}$$ The required SIF PFD is then: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \le \frac{f_{\text{target}}}{f_{\text{initiating}} \cdot \prod_{i \ne \text{SIF}} \text{PFD}_{IPL,i}}$$ Typical PFD values used in LOPA: | Protection Layer | Typical PFD | |:---|:---:| | BPCS (Control loop) | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ | | Operator response to alarm | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ to $1 \times 10^{-2}$ | | Relief Valve | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ to $1 \times 10^{-1}$ | | Dike / Bund | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ to $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | | SIF (SIL 1) | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ to $1 \times 10^{-2}$ | | SIF (SIL 2) | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ to $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | | SIF (SIL 3) | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SIS Architecture — Sensor-Logic-Final Element Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Process Plant
e.g. Pressure Vessel] -->|PV rises| S1 P -->|PV rises| S2 subgraph SENS["SENSOR SUBSYSTEM"] S1[Pressure TX 1
PT-1001A] S2[Pressure TX 2
PT-1001B] end subgraph LOGIC["LOGIC SOLVER"] L1[Safety PLC
S7-F or Triconex] end subgraph FINAL["FINAL ELEMENT SUBSYSTEM"] V1[Shutdown Valve SDV-101
Solenoid A] V2[Shutdown Valve SDV-101
Solenoid B] end S1 -->|4-20 mA| L1 S2 -->|4-20 mA| L1 L1 -->|Trip Command| V1 L1 -->|Trip Command| V2 V1 -->|Close| P V2 -->|Close| P style P fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S1 fill:#A0DFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style S2 fill:#A0DFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style L1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style V1 fill:#C1FFC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style V2 fill:#C1FFC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style SENS fill:#E6F7FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style LOGIC fill:#FFF0E6,stroke:#663300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style FINAL fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The process variable (pressure) is sensed by *two* redundant transmitters feeding a *single* Safety PLC. The PLC sends a trip signal to *two* solenoid valves on a single shutdown valve body. This is a classic **1oo2 sensor / 1oo2 final element** architecture, used for SIL 2/3 SIFs. ## 4.2 Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) Onion ```mermaid flowchart TB C[Consequence to Public
Environment or Personnel] --> C1 C1[Layer 1: Community
Emergency Response] --> C2 C2[Layer 2: Mechanical Design
Relief Valves Dikes] --> C3 C3[Layer 3: Operational Procedures
Alarms Operator Action] --> C4 C4[Layer 4: BPCS
Basic Process Control] --> C5 C5[Layer 5: SAFETY INSTRUMENTED SYSTEM
The SIS] --> C6 C6[Layer 6: Physical Relief
Rupture Discs PSV] --> C7 C7[Layer 7: Ultimate
Plant Layout Siting] --> P[Safe State] style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style C2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style C3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style C4 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style C5 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF style C6 fill:#C1FFC1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style C7 fill:#C1FFC1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style P fill:#80C080,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR PH1[Phase 1
Process Hazard
and Risk Analysis] --> PH2[Phase 2
Allocation of
Safety Functions] PH2 --> PH3[Phase 3
SIS Safety
Requirement Spec] PH3 --> PH4[Phase 4
SIS Design
and Engineering] PH4 --> PH5[Phase 5
Installation
and Commissioning] PH5 --> PH6[Phase 6
Operation
and Maintenance] PH6 --> PH7[Phase 7
Modification
Management] PH7 --> PH8[Phase 8
Decommissioning] PH6 -.->|Periodic| PT[Proof Testing
Interval T proof] PT -.->|Verifies| PH6 style PH1 fill:#A0DFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style PH2 fill:#A0DFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style PH3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style PH4 fill:#A0DFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style PH5 fill:#A0DFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style PH6 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style PH7 fill:#A0DFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style PH8 fill:#A0DFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 style PT fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2.5px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 4.4 PFD vs. Proof-Test Interval — Behavioural Map ```mermaid flowchart LR T0[T proof = 0] --> T1[1oo1 PFD = 0] T1 --> T2[As T increases PFD rises linearly 1oo1] T2 --> T3[Quadratic rise 1oo2] T3 --> T4[T reaches T proof schedule] T4 --> T5[Proof Test Performed] T5 -->|PFD resets to 0| T1 style T0 fill:#80C080,stroke:#000,color:#000 style T1 fill:#C1FFC1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style T2 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,color:#000 style T3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000 style T4 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF style T5 fill:#80C080,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] > **Define Safety Instrumented System (SIS). Mention its three main components.** **Model Answer:** A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is an engineered system composed of sensors, logic solvers, and final elements, designed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined unsafe conditions are detected. It is functionally independent of the Basic Process Control System (BPCS) and operates under the framework of IEC 61508 / IEC 61511. The three main components are: 1. **Sensor Subsystem** — measures process variables (pressure, temperature, flow, level, gas). 2. **Logic Solver Subsystem** — receives sensor input, applies safety logic, and decides whether to trip (e.g., Safety PLC or hard-wired relay logic). 3. **Final Element Subsystem** — physically brings the process to a safe state (e.g., shut-down valves, circuit breakers, firewater pumps). > **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark), Three components (2 marks). --- > ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] > **Distinguish between Safe Failure and Dangerous Failure in an SIS. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Safe Failure (S) | Dangerous Failure (D) | |:---|:---|:---| | Effect on safety function | Spurious / false trip — the system *trips when not needed* | System *fails to trip when needed* | | Impact on plant | Production loss, no safety risk | Potentially catastrophic — loss of containment / injury | | Detection | Often detected by diagnostic or operator | Detected only at proof test (for DU) | | Example | Pressure transmitter diaphragm ruptures, reads 0 (trips plant) | Solenoid on shutdown valve stuck open — cannot close on demand | > **Mark Distribution:** Definitions (1 mark), Contrast (1 mark), Examples (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT mix up BPCS and SIS functions.** If you write that the SIS controls the *normal* level in a tank, you will lose 2–3 marks immediately. The SIS only acts on *abnormal* conditions. > - **Always state the equation first, then substitute values** — examiners in KTU specifically test the *correct formula*. Writing only the final number without the formula yields 0 marks for the calculation step. > - **When asked "determine the SIL," you MUST state both the PFD value AND the corresponding SIL band from the table.** Half-mark answers are the most common reason students score 6/14 instead of 7/14. > - **Do not confuse architecture labels** — 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3 have *very* different PFDs. A single wrong label can invalidate the entire numerical answer. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Module 2 Pattern] **(a)** Explain the Safety Integrity Level (SIL) concept as per IEC 61511. With a neat sketch, describe the architecture of a typical Safety Instrumented System, clearly identifying the sensor, logic solver, and final element sub-systems. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A temperature SIF is designed with the following data: - 1oo2 voted RTD sensors, each with $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. - A safety PLC logic solver with $\text{PFD}_{\text{LS}} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$. - A 1oo2 voted pair of solenoid-operated valves, each with $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. - Proof-test interval $T_{\text{proof}} = 8760$ hours (1 year). Calculate (i) PFD of the sensor sub-system, (ii) PFD of the final element sub-system, (iii) the total PFD of the SIF, and (iv) the SIL achieved by the complete SIF. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **SIL Concept (3 marks):** Safety Integrity Level is a discrete level (1 to 4) defined in IEC 61511 to specify the required performance of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) in terms of risk reduction. In **low-demand mode**, the SIL is mapped to a target **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD avg)**. The four SIL levels correspond to PFD ranges: - SIL 1: $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ - SIL 2: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ - SIL 3: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ - SIL 4: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ The risk reduction factor is $\text{RRF} = 1/\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$. Higher SIL = lower PFD = greater risk reduction. **Architecture (4 marks):** A typical SIS is a closed loop of three independent sub-systems — refer to the diagram in Section 4.1. Key points to mention: - **Sensor Sub-system:** measures the process variable (e.g., pressure, temperature, level). Often uses redundant transmitters (1oo2 or 2oo3 voting) to achieve required SIL. - **Logic Solver:** Safety PLC (e.g., Triconex, Siemens S7-F) certified to IEC 61508 SIL 3. It receives the sensor signal, applies the safety logic (e.g., "trip if pressure > 10 barg"), and sends a trip command to final elements. - **Final Element Sub-system:** Typically a pneumatically or hydraulically operated shutdown valve with one or more solenoids, plus a manual reset. May also be motor starters, circuit breakers, etc. - The whole SIS is **physically and functionally independent** of the BPCS (which performs normal regulatory control). **Model Solution (b):** **(i) PFD of sensor sub-system (1oo2 architecture):** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}})^2}{6}$$ Substitute: $$\lambda_{DU} \cdot T = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} = \frac{(8.76 \times 10^{-3})^2}{6} = \frac{7.67 \times 10^{-5}}{6} \approx 1.28 \times 10^{-5}$$ **(ii) PFD of final element sub-system (1oo2 architecture):** $$\lambda_{DU} \cdot T = 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 2.628 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{final}} = \frac{(2.628 \times 10^{-2})^2}{6} = \frac{6.906 \times 10^{-4}}{6} \approx 1.15 \times 10^{-4}$$ **(iii) Total PFD of the SIF:** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{LS}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{final}}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = 1.28 \times 10^{-5} + 5 \times 10^{-3} + 1.15 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \approx 5.13 \times 10^{-3}$$ **(iv) SIL achieved by the SIF:** Comparing with the SIL table: $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \approx 5.13 \times 10^{-3} < 10^{-2}$. $$\boxed{\text{The SIF achieves SIL 2.}}$$ --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [Alternative — KTU Internal Choice] **(a)** What is the Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)? Explain, with the help of neat derivations, how the average PFD is calculated for (i) a 1oo1 single-channel system and (ii) a 1oo2 redundant voted system, in low-demand mode of operation. State the role of the proof-test interval in these expressions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** technique, a process unit has: - Initiating event frequency: $f_{\text{init}} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ events / year (heat exchanger tube rupture). - Existing IPLs: BPCS loop with PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$, Operator response to high-level alarm with PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$, Bund / dike with PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$. - Target mitigated event frequency: $f_{\text{target}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ / year. Determine (i) the frequency after each existing IPL, (ii) the **PFD required of the SIF** to meet the target, and (iii) the SIL of the SIF. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Definition of PFD (1 mark):** PFD is the probability that a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) will fail to perform its required safety action when a demand occurs. For low-demand mode (demand rate $\le 1$ per year), the metric of interest is the **average** PFD over the proof-test interval. **Derivation for 1oo1 (3 marks):** Refer to Section 3.1, Steps 1–5. The linearized instantaneous unreliability is $Q(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} t$. The average over one proof-test cycle $T$ is the integral of $Q(t)$ divided by $T$: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \lambda_{DU} t \, dt = \frac{\lambda_{DU} T}{2}$$ **Derivation for 1oo2 (3 marks):** Refer to Section 3.2. For two independent channels, system fails only if *both* fail dangerously. Joint unreliability is $(\lambda_{DU} t)^2$. The integral gives: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (\lambda_{DU} t)^2 \, dt = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T)^2}{6}$$ **Role of proof-test interval (additional credit):** The proof test resets the SIF to "as good as new." Therefore the PFD rises linearly (1oo1) or quadratically (1oo2) between tests. **Shorter T_proof → lower PFD avg**, but increases plant downtime and maintenance cost. The SIF's actual SIL is only maintained if T_proof is respected operationally. **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Frequency after each IPL (using LOPA multiplication):** After BPCS: $$f_1 = f_{\text{init}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} = 1 \times 10^{-1} \times 1 \times 10^{-1} = 1 \times 10^{-2} / \text{yr}$$ After Operator action: $$f_2 = f_1 \times \text{PFD}_{\text{op}} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times 10^{-1} = 1 \times 10^{-3} / \text{yr}$$ After Bund / dike: $$f_3 = f_2 \times \text{PFD}_{\text{bund}} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-2} = 1 \times 10^{-5} / \text{yr}$$ **(ii) Required PFD of the SIF:** $$f_{\text{target}} = f_3 \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}}$$ $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \frac{f_{\text{target}}}{f_3} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-5}} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$$ **(iii) SIL of the SIF:** A PFD of $1 \times 10^{-1}$ falls within the SIL 1 band ($10^{-1} \ge \text{PFD} > 10^{-2}$). $$\boxed{\text{The SIF must achieve SIL 1.}}$$ > **Mark Distribution Note:** In (b), (i) is worth 3 marks, (ii) is worth 2 marks, and (iii) is worth 2 marks. The LOPA equation $\text{PFD} = f_{\text{target}} / f_{\text{after IPLs}}$ is the single most-tested LOPA expression in KTU. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read this 3 times the night before the exam.** - **SIS Definition:** Independent, dedicated engineered system that takes a process to a *safe state* on demand. Governed by **IEC 61508** (cross-industry) and **IEC 61511** (process industry). - **Three sub-systems:** Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element. Memorize in this order. - **SIS is NEVER used for normal control.** It is the *last automatic* layer before mechanical relief. The BPCS does the day-to-day regulatory control. - **SIL Levels (Low-Demand Mode):** SIL 1 → PFD $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 2 → $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 3 → $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$; SIL 4 → $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$. - **Risk Reduction Factor:** RRF = 1 / PFD. SIL 3 ⇒ RRF between 1,000 and 10,000. - **Failure Categories:** Safe (S), Dangerous Detected (DD), Dangerous Undetected (DU). The DU rate $\lambda_{DU}$ drives the PFD math. - **Key PFD formulas:** - $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T / 2$ - $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = (\lambda_{DU} T)^2 / 6$ - $\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo2}} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU} T / 2 = \lambda_{DU} T$ (worse than 1oo1 in PFD terms — beware!) - $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{LS}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{final}}$ - **Proof-Test Interval T_proof:** Doubling T doubles 1oo1 PFD and quadruples 1oo2 PFD — always check operational compliance with the SRS. - **SIL of the SIF is dictated by the weakest sub-block.** A SIL 3 sensor is wasted if the final element is only SIL 0. - **SFF and DC:** Safe Failure Fraction and Diagnostic Coverage — both control which SIL a single-channel component can reach. - **LOPA equation:** $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_{IPL,i}$. The SIF is the last IPL before the target is met. - **Risk Graph** is the qualitative method; **LOPA** is the semi-quantitative method; both feed the **SIL target** for the SIF. - **IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle** has 11 phases — know at least the first 4 (Risk Assessment → Allocation → SRS → Design & Engineering) and Phase 6 (Operation, Maintenance, Proof Testing). - **Common Examiner Traps:** (1) Confusing SIS with BPCS; (2) using $\lambda_D$ instead of $\lambda_{DU}$ in PFD formulas; (3) forgetting the 1/2 factor in 1oo1; (4) reading "2oo2" as if it were "1oo2." - **Real-World Application:** ESD systems, F&G systems, HIPPS, turbine overspeed trips — every major process plant in the world has multiple SIL-rated SIFs and a periodic proof-test programme to maintain them. - **Mnemonic for Memorization:** "**S**ensors **L**isten, **L**ogic **L**ocks, **F**inal **E**lements **F**ire" — **SLLE F** = Sensor–Logic–Final Element. > **End of KTU Module 2 Notes — Safety Instrumented System (OESFT831).**

topics.title: Safety Instrumented Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety Instrumented Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** — A safety function implemented by a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) that is intended to reduce the risk of a specific hazardous event to an acceptable (tolerable) level by taking a process to a safe state when a hazardous condition is detected.

In the language of **IEC 61511** (the standard KTU expects you to quote):

> A **SIF** is any safety function that achieves the necessary risk reduction using an instrumentation-based path consisting of three distinct subsystems:
> **Sensor(s) → Logic Solver → Final Element(s)**

Each SIF is assigned a **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** — a discrete integer level (1, 2, 3, or 4) that defines the required **probability of failure on demand (PFDavg)** for that function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Fire Alarm" Analogy

Imagine your house has a **fire alarm system**:

| Component | Real-World Role | SIF Equivalent |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| Smoke detector | Detects the hazard | **Sensor** |
| Control panel / Siren | Decides what to do and alarms | **Logic Solver** |
| Sprinkler valve + water | Actually puts out the fire | **Final Element** |
| The entire alarm chain | "Detect → Decide → Act" | **Safety Instrumented Function** |

> [!NOTE]
> If the fire alarm is supposed to **reduce the risk** of people dying in a house fire from "once every 5 years" to "once every 500 years," then that risk-reduction job is the **SIF**. The hardware/software/people that deliver it form the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)**.

### 1.3 Why SIFs Exist — The Risk Reduction Equation

Every hazardous process has a **Tolerable Risk** target set by regulation or corporate policy. The SIF exists to bridge the gap:

$$\text{Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)} \;=\; \frac{\text{Frequency of Unmitigated Event}}{\text{Tolerable Frequency}}$$

The SIF must achieve a **Probability of Failure on Demand** such that:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \;\leq\; \frac{1}{\text{RRF}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **PFDavg** = average Probability of Failure on Demand. A "demand" is the moment the hazard appears and asks the SIF to act.

### 1.4 Key Terminology — Board-Ready Definitions

- **Demand**: An occurrence of a hazardous event (or condition) that requires the SIF to act.
- **Safe State**: A predetermined condition of the process where the hazard is neutralized (e.g., valve closed, pump stopped, reactor depressurized).
- **Dangerous Failure (λ_D)**: A failure that prevents the SIF from performing its safety action.
- **Safe Failure (λ_S)**: A failure that causes a spurious trip but does not prevent protection when needed.
- **Proof Test Interval (t_CE)**: The interval (typically 1–5 years) between manual checks that restore fault coverage.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)**: The average downtime of a channel after a dangerous detected failure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of a SIF

A SIF is **architecturally** a chain of three independent subsystems. Each subsystem independently contributes to the overall **PFDavg** of the SIF.

### Pillar 1 — Sensor Subsystem

- Detects the hazardous condition (pressure, temperature, flow, level, toxic gas, flame, etc.).
- Converts the physical variable into a standardized electrical/pneumatic signal.
- Examples: pressure transmitter, flame detector, level switch.

### Pillar 2 — Logic Solver

- Receives sensor input(s) and applies a **voting logic** (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3, etc.).
- Generates the trip command when the voting logic determines a hazardous state.
- Examples: Safety PLC (SIS controller), trip relay, hard-wired logic.

### Pillar 3 — Final Element

- Physically changes the process to the safe state.
- Examples: solenoid-operated shutdown valve, motor contactor, block valve with pneumatic actuator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independence Rule (IEC 61511):** The three pillars must be **independent** of the Basic Process Control System (BPCS). Sharing sensors, logic, or valves between control and safety functions is a **fatal audit finding** in any functional safety assessment.

## 2.2 Voting Logic — How Redundancy Works

The notation **MooN** means: *N* identical channels are installed; the system trips if at least *M* of them demand it.

| Notation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1oo1 | Single channel, no redundancy | Low SIL, simple loops |
| 1oo2 | Two channels, both must demand to trip | Avoid spurious trips |
| 2oo2 | Two channels, either one trips | High integrity, low availability |
| 2oo3 | Three channels, two-out-of-three must agree | Highest SIL + good availability |

## 2.3 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) — The PFDavg Targets

| SIL | Target PFDavg Range (low-demand mode) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1,000 |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU typically only requires SIL 1, SIL 2, and SIL 3 — most process plants do not operate SIL 4 SIFs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the formulas below — every numerical SIF question in KTU maps to one of these.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF} = 1/\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Frequency after protection | $f_{\text{after}} = f_{\text{before}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ | per year |
| 3 | SIF Total PFD (additive) | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Final}}$ | Each term dimensionless |
| 4 | PFD — 1oo1 architecture | $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \lambda_D \cdot t_{CE}$ | $\lambda_D$ in $\text{fit}$, $t_{CE}$ in hours |
| 5 | PFD — 1oo2 architecture | $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \dfrac{(\lambda_D \cdot t_{CE})^2}{3}$ | Both must fail |
| 6 | PFD — 2oo2 architecture | $\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo2}} = 2 \cdot (\lambda_D \cdot t_{CE})^2$ | Either can trip |
| 7 | PFD — 2oo3 architecture | $\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo3}} = 6 \cdot (\lambda_D \cdot t_{CE})^2$ | Common-mode considered |
| 8 | Unit conversion | $1 \; \text{fit} = 10^{-9} \; \text{failures/hour}$ | $1 \text{ fit} \approx 10^{-6}/\text{year}$ approx |
| 9 | Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$ | SFF ≥ 60 % required for SIL 2 (Type B) |
| 10 | Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) | From IEC 61508 table | See IEC 61508 Route 1_H |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of SIFs

- **Process Industry**: Overpressure protection on reactors, fired-heater flame-failure shutdown, gas-detector isolation trips.
- **Power Industry**: Turbine overspeed trip, boiler drum level protection, generator cooling trip.
- **Oil & Gas**: Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves, Blowdown systems, Fire & Gas (F&G) systems.
- **Pharma / Chemical**: Batch-reactor temperature interlocks, toxic-gas scrubber activation.
- **Transportation**: Railway signalling, aircraft autoland, automatic train stop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PFDavg of a 1oo1 Single Channel

**Premise**: A single channel that fails dangerously with constant failure rate $\lambda_D$ and is proof-tested every $t_{CE}$ hours.

The probability that a dangerous failure occurs **and is undetected until the next proof test** is, on average, half the test interval. The "average downtime" of the protection is $t_{CE}/2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} &= \lambda_D \cdot \frac{t_{CE}}{2} \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) \;+\; \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{t_{CE}}{2} \\
&\text{where } \lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) \text{ is the undetected dangerous failure rate.} \\
&\text{Assuming coverage } \text{DC} = 0 \text{ (worst case for KTU exam):} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} &= \lambda_D \cdot \frac{t_{CE}}{2} \\
&\text{In board exams, this is often simplified to:} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} &\approx \lambda_D \cdot t_{CE} \quad \text{(when } t_{CE} \text{ is taken as the full interval)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion step**: $1 \text{ fit} = 10^{-9}$ failures/hour. So a $\lambda_D = 5000$ fit sensor tested every year ($t_{CE} = 8760$ h) gives PFD ≈ $5000 \times 10^{-9} \times 8760 \approx 0.0438$ — which falls in **SIL 1**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFDavg of a 1oo2 Architecture (Two Channels, Both Must Fail)

In 1oo2, the system **only fails** when both channels fail dangerously **and** the test/condition of overlap allows simultaneous undetected failures.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &= \frac{(\lambda_D \cdot t_{CE})^2}{3} \\
\text{Derivation idea:} \\
&\text{Probability both channels fail in the same interval} \propto (\lambda_D t_{CE})^2 \\
&\text{Average overlap factor for two independent exponential failures} = 1/3 \\
&\therefore \text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \dfrac{(\lambda_D t_{CE})^2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example** — same sensor ($\lambda_D = 5000$ fit, $t_{CE} = 8760$ h):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &= \frac{(5000 \times 10^{-9} \times 8760)^2}{3} \\
&= \frac{(0.0438)^2}{3} \\
&= \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-3}}{3} \\
&= 6.39 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is in the **SIL 3** band — proving why redundancy gives step-change improvements in integrity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PFDavg of a 2oo3 Architecture

A 2oo3 system has three channels; the system fails only if **two of three** fail dangerously while the third is healthy but disagrees.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo3}} &= 6 \cdot (\lambda_D \cdot t_{CE})^2 \\
\text{Reasoning:} \\
&\text{There are } \binom{3}{2} = 3 \text{ ways to pick two failing channels.} \\
&\text{Each pair has failure probability } (\lambda_D t_{CE})^2. \\
&\text{The third channel must still appear healthy — adds a factor of 2 (either way).} \\
&\therefore \text{PFD}_{\text{2oo3}} = 3 \cdot 2 \cdot (\lambda_D t_{CE})^2 = 6 (\lambda_D t_{CE})^2
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total SIF PFD (Sensors + Logic + Final Elements)

Because the three subsystems are in **series** (any one failure defeats the SIF), their PFDs add:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF, total}} &= \text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Final}} \\
&= \lambda_{D,\text{Sensor}} \cdot t_{CE} + \lambda_{D,\text{Logic}} \cdot t_{CE} + \lambda_{D,\text{Final}} \cdot t_{CE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Typical allocation rule (board-typical):**

| Subsystem | Allocation | Reasoning |
|-----------|------------|-----------|
| Sensor | 35 % of PFD | Field environment harsh |
| Logic Solver | 15 % of PFD | High reliability electronics |
| Final Element | 50 % of PFD | Mechanical wear, sticking valves |

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Risk Graph Method (SIL Determination)

The **Risk Graph** (IEC 61511) uses four parameters to determine the required SIL:

- **C** — Consequence severity
- **F** — Frequency of exposure to hazard
- **P** — Possibility of avoidance
- **W** — Probability of occurrence of the demand

The required SIL is read from the calibrated risk graph table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Score} &= C + F + P + W \\
\text{Required SIL} &= f(\text{Risk Score})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calibrated KTU-style risk graph output:**

| Combination (C, F, P, W) | Required SIL |
|:---:|:---:|
| Minor, Rare, Avoidable, Slight | No SIF needed |
| Minor, Frequent, Avoidable, Slight | SIL 1 |
| Serious, Frequent, Unavoidable, Slight | SIL 2 |
| Extensive, Frequent, Unavoidable, High | SIL 3 |
| Extensive, Frequent, Unavoidable, High (catastrophic) | SIL 4 |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — End-to-End Worked Example

**Problem**: A reactor has an unmitigated runaway frequency of $f = 1 \times 10^{-2}$/year. Tolerable frequency is $1 \times 10^{-5}$/year. Design a SIF using a single pressure transmitter ($\lambda_D = 3000$ fit) proof-tested annually.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Compute RRF:} \quad \text{RRF} &= \frac{f_{\text{before}}}{f_{\text{tolerable}}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-5}} = 10^{3} \\
\text{Step 2 — Required SIL:} \quad \text{RRF} = 1000 &\Rightarrow \text{SIL 2} \;\;(\text{since } 10^{2} \le 10^{3} < 10^{4}) \\
\text{Step 3 — Convert } \lambda_D: \quad \lambda_D &= 3000 \times 10^{-9} = 3 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{/h} \\
\text{Step 4 — Test interval:} \quad t_{CE} &= 1 \text{ year} = 8760 \text{ h} \\
\text{Step 5 — Compute PFD (1oo1):} \quad \text{PFD} &= \lambda_D \cdot t_{CE} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \\
&= 2.628 \times 10^{-2} \\
\text{Step 6 — Check:} \quad \text{PFD} &= 2.628 \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{which is in } [10^{-2}, 10^{-1}) \\
\Rightarrow &\text{SIL 1 only — INSUFFICIENT} \\
\text{Step 7 — Upgrade to 1oo2:} \quad \text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &= \frac{(\lambda_D t_{CE})^2}{3} = \frac{(2.628 \times 10^{-2})^2}{3} \\
&= \frac{6.9 \times 10^{-4}}{3} = 2.3 \times 10^{-4} \\
\Rightarrow &\text{SIL 3 — EXCEEDS requirement (acceptable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs below are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`), and labels contain no markdown formatting tokens.

## 4.2 Block-Level SIF Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    process[Process Unit with Hazard] --> sensor1[Sensor Subsystem Channel A]
    process --> sensor2[Sensor Subsystem Channel B]
    sensor1 --> voting{Voting Logic in Logic Solver}
    sensor2 --> voting
    voting --> trip[Trip Command Generated]
    trip --> final1[Final Element Channel A Shutdown Valve]
    trip --> final2[Final Element Channel B Shutdown Valve]
    final1 --> safeState[Process Reaches Safe State]
    final2 --> safeState
    safeState --> outcome[Risk Reduced to Tolerable Level]

    classDef safeState fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef hazard fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef logic fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class process,hazard,outcome hazard
    class voting,trip logic
    class safeState safeState
```

## 4.3 SIL Determination Flow (Risk Graph Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Hazardous Event Identified]) --> assessFreq[Assess Frequency of Occurrence W]
    assessFreq --> assessCons[Assess Consequence Severity C]
    assessCons --> assessOcc[Assess Frequency of Occupancy F]
    assessOcc --> assessAvoid[Assess Possibility of Avoidance P]
    assessAvoid --> calcRRF[Compute Risk Reduction Factor RRF]
    calcRRF --> lookupSIL[Map RRF to SIL Band]
    lookupSIL --> designSIF[Design SIF Architecture MooN]
    designSIF --> validatePFD[Compute PFDavg and Verify against SIL Target]
    validatePIFD{Compliance Achieved?}
    validatePFD -->|Yes| document([SIF Documented in SRS])
    validatePFD -->|No| iterate[Increase Redundancy or Shorten Proof Test Interval]
    iterate --> designSIF

    classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef action fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef warningBox fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class validatePFD decision
    class start,document action
    class iterate warningBox
```

## 4.4 Independent Protection Layer (IPL) Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1[Layer 1 Process Design Inherently Safer] --> layer2[Layer 2 Basic Process Control System BPCS]
    layer2 --> layer3[Layer 3 Critical Alarms Operator Intervention]
    layer3 --> layer4[Layer 4 Safety Instrumented Function SIF]
    layer4 --> layer5[Layer 5 Physical Relief Devices PSV Rupture Disc]
    layer5 --> layer6[Layer 6 Emergency Response Firefighting Spill Containment]
    layer6 --> result[Residual Risk within Tolerable Limits]

    classDef layers fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef resultBox fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

```
