# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — **Q1 (3 Marks): Define Safety Instrumented Function. List its three architectural components.**

**Model Answer**:
A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is a safety function implemented by a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) that brings a process to a safe state upon detection of a hazardous condition, achieving a defined risk reduction. The three architectural components are: **Sensor Subsystem** (detects the hazard), **Logic Solver** (decides and commands the trip), and **Final Element** (physically executes the safe-state action). Each component independently contributes to the overall PFDavg.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — **Q2 (3 Marks): Differentiate between PFD and RRF.**

**Model Answer**:
**PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** is the probability that the SIF fails to perform its safety action when demanded; it is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1. **RRF (Risk Reduction Factor)** is the inverse of PFD and represents how much the SIF reduces the frequency of the hazardous event. Mathematically, $\text{RRF} = 1/\text{PFD}$. For SIL 2, PFD is in $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$ and RRF is in $[10^{2}, 10^{3})$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — SIF Design and SIL Determination `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

A natural gas compressor station has a release frequency of $5 \times 10^{-3}$/year for a major hydrocarbon leak. The tolerable frequency set by company policy is $5 \times 10^{-6}$/year.

**(a)** Determine the required SIL for the SIF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the chosen architecture is **1oo2** with identical flame detectors ($\lambda_D = 4000$ fit) proof-tested every 2 years, compute the achieved PFDavg and verify whether the design meets the SIL target. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RRF}_{\text{required}} &= \frac{f_{\text{before}}}{f_{\text{tolerable}}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^{3} \\
\text{Since } 10^{2} \le 10^{3} < 10^{4}, \quad &\text{Required SIL} = \mathbf{SIL\; 2} \\
\text{Target PFD range:} \quad &10^{-3} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_D &= 4000 \times 10^{-9} = 4 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{/h} \\
t_{CE} &= 2 \text{ years} = 2 \times 8760 = 17520\;\text{h} \\
\lambda_D \cdot t_{CE} &= 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 17520 = 7.008 \times 10^{-2} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &= \frac{(\lambda_D t_{CE})^2}{3} = \frac{(7.008 \times 10^{-2})^2}{3} \\
&= \frac{4.911 \times 10^{-3}}{3} \\
&= 1.637 \times 10^{-3} \\
\text{Check against SIL 2 range } [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}): \quad &\text{Yes, } 1.637 \times 10^{-3} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}) \\
\therefore &\text{Design MEETS SIL 2 target.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students commonly forget to convert **fit** to **failures/hour** (multiplication by $10^{-9}$) and lose 2 marks instantly. Also, $t_{CE}$ must be in **hours**, not years.

---

### QUESTION B — Risk Graph and PFD Computation `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a risk graph, explain how the Safety Integrity Level of a SIF is determined. Use a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SIF consists of a sensor ($\lambda_D = 2000$ fit), logic solver ($\lambda_D = 500$ fit), and a shutdown valve ($\lambda_D = 3000$ fit). Proof test interval is 1 year. Compute the total PFDavg for a 1oo1 architecture and identify the achieved SIL. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The Risk Graph (per IEC 61511) is a calibrated decision tool that determines required SIL from four parameters:

- **C** = Consequence severity (Minor / Serious / Extensive)
- **F** = Frequency of occupancy in the hazardous zone (Rare to Frequent)
- **P** = Possibility of avoidance (Avoidable / Unavoidable)
- **W** = Probability of demand occurrence (Slight to High)

**Worked example**: A reactor overpressure scenario:
- C = Extensive (potential fatality)
- F = Frequent (operator present 8 h/day)
- P = Unavoidable (reaction runaway is fast)
- W = Slight (initiating event rare)

Reading the calibrated risk graph: this combination yields **SIL 2** requirement.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{CE} &= 8760\;\text{h} \\
\lambda_{D,\text{Sensor}} &= 2000 \times 10^{-9} = 2 \times 10^{-6}/\text{h} \\
\lambda_{D,\text{Logic}} &= 500 \times 10^{-9} = 5 \times 10^{-7}/\text{h} \\
\lambda_{D,\text{Final}} &= 3000 \times 10^{-9} = 3 \times 10^{-6}/\text{h} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} &= 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 1.752 \times 10^{-2} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} &= 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^{-3} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{Final}} &= 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 2.628 \times 10^{-2} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{Total}} &= 1.752 \times 10^{-2} + 4.38 \times 10^{-3} + 2.628 \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 4.818 \times 10^{-2} \\
&\approx 4.82 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $4.82 \times 10^{-2} \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1})$, the achieved SIL = **SIL 1**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students compute each subsystem PFD correctly but forget to **add** them — they take the average or only report the largest. The correct operation is **addition** because subsystems are in series (any one fails → SIF fails). Marks lost: 3 of 7.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Safety Instrumented Function**

- ☐ SIF = Sensor + Logic Solver + Final Element (independence mandatory from BPCS).
- ☐ SIL bands: SIL 1 → PFD $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 → $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 → $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- ☐ Always remember: $1 \text{ fit} = 10^{-9}$ failures per hour.
- ☐ PFD formulas: 1oo1 = $\lambda_D t_{CE}$; 1oo2 = $(\lambda_D t_{CE})^2/3$; 2oo3 = $6 (\lambda_D t_{CE})^2$.
- ☐ Total SIF PFD = PFD(Sensor) + PFD(Logic) + PFD(Final) — **additive**, not averaged.
- ☐ RRF = 1/PFD; required RRF = $f_{\text{before}} / f_{\text{tolerable}}$.
- ☐ Risk Graph uses C, F, P, W parameters to derive required SIL.
- ☐ Independence of protection layers: SIF is **Layer 4** in the LOPA IPL stack.
- ☐ Increasing redundancy (1oo1 → 1oo2 → 2oo3) reduces dangerous failure probability but may increase spurious trip rate.
- ☐ Shorter proof test interval ($t_{CE}$) reduces PFD linearly — a key operational lever.
- ☐ SFF ≥ 60 % required for SIL 2 (Type B subsystems); SFF ≥ 90 % required for SIL 3.
- ☐ Safe state is **predefined**, not decided at the moment of demand.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Assignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Assignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Assignment** is the systematic engineering process used to determine the required level of risk reduction — quantified as a discrete target **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{avg}$)** — that a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) must achieve in order to bring an identified process hazard down to a tolerable risk level. The assignment is performed in accordance with the international functional-safety standard **IEC 61508** (generic) and **IEC 61511** (process industries).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *SIL Assignment is the procedure for classifying the safety function with respect to the integrity of its performance. Four discrete levels (SIL 1 to SIL 4) are defined, each representing a range of target probability of dangerous failure on demand.*

The four SIL bands prescribed by IEC 61508 are shown below:

| SIL Level | Target PFD$_{avg}$ (Low-Demand Mode) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|-----------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-4}$ | $10{,}000 \le RRF < 100{,}000$ |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-3}$ | $1{,}000 \le RRF < 10{,}000$ |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$ | $100 \le RRF < 1{,}000$ |
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-1}$ | $10 \le RRF < 100$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In process industries (KTU 2024 emphasis), **only SIL 1, SIL 2, and SIL 3** are practically applied. **SIL 4** is rarely assigned outside nuclear or aviation domains because achieving it requires very high integrity hardware, multiple redundancy, and severe testing regimes that are commercially infeasible for a typical refinery or chemical plant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Fire-Rating" Analogy

Imagine you are a fire-safety engineer grading a hospital. A small storeroom may only need a **single smoke detector and a hand extinguisher** (think of this as SIL 1: a single, moderately reliable safety layer). A high-rise ward storing flammable anaesthetic gases may require a **complete sprinkler network, gas-leak detectors, ventilation shut-off, alarms, and a redundant fire brigade interface** (this is SIL 3: many independent, highly reliable, rigorously tested layers). You would **never** install a nuclear-grade fire suppression in a coffee shop — it would be grossly over-engineered and uneconomical. SIL Assignment is exactly this "right-sizing" exercise for process safety systems.

The fundamental engineering philosophy is:

$$\text{Required Risk Reduction} = \frac{\text{Frequency of Hazardous Event}}{\text{Tolerable Frequency of the Consequence}}$$

---

## 1.3 Standards Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two international standards underpin every KTU examination answer on this topic:
> 1. **IEC 61508 (2010)** — Generic standard for Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic safety-related systems. Defines SIL bands 1–4.
> 2. **IEC 61511 (2016)** — Process-industry sector standard. Defines lifecycle phases (Yokogawa/Exida–aligned) and the **Safety Life Cycle** of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS).

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks students to know that SIL Assignment is **Phase 3 (Hazard & Risk Analysis)** of the Safety Life Cycle, immediately followed by Phase 4 (SIS Design & Engineering).

---

## 1.4 Why SIL Assignment Matters — The Engineering Rationale

Without a structured SIL Assignment:
- Operators may **over-engineer** (costing crores of rupees in redundant transmitters and valves),
- Or **under-engineer** (leading to catastrophic incidents such as Bhopal 1984, Texas City 2005, Buncefield 2005).

The Three-Reason SIL Assignment Logic:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. The **Consequence** (deaths, injuries, environmental damage, financial loss).
> 2. The **Demand Rate** (how often the unsafe condition is likely to occur).
> 3. The **Existing Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** (relief valves, alarms, operator intervention, containment dikes, basic process control).
> The residual risk after subtracting IPLs determines the required PFD$_{avg}$ of the SIF.

---

## 1.5 Modes of Operation

A crucial distinction the KTU examiner expects:

| Operation Mode | Definition | SIL Target Quantity |
|----------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Low-Demand Mode** | Demand on the SIF occurs **less than once per year**. | PFD$_{avg}$ |
| **High-Demand / Continuous Mode** | SIF operates continuously or is demanded more than once per year. | PFH (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour) |

For the KTU Module-2 scope, the **low-demand PFD$_{avg}$** framework (SIL 1–3) is the focus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SIL Determination Flow — Conceptual Logic

SIL Assignment is the bridge between **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** and **SIS Design**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate the following five-stage reasoning chain:

1. **Identify the Hazardous Event** — from HAZOP / What-If / FMEA outputs.
2. **Determine the Consequence Severity (C)** — categories C$_A$, C$_B$, C$_C$, C$_D$.
3. **Estimate the Demand Rate (W)** — how frequently the initiating event could create the hazardous situation.
4. **Quantify Existing Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** — alarms, basic control, relief devices, dikes.
5. **Compute the Required Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** — and read off the corresponding SIL.

## 2.2 The Four Methods of SIL Assignment (KTU High-Yield)

The IEC 61508/61511 framework recognises four progressively rigorous methodologies. The KTU Module-2 question bank typically asks students to **compare and contrast** these methods.

### Method A — Consequence-Based (Qualitative)
Used when the **demand rate is very low** (less than $10^{-2}$ /yr) and the consequence dominates the risk profile. The SIL is set entirely by severity:
- Catastrophic (multiple fatalities) → **SIL 3**
- Serious (single fatality / permanent disability) → **SIL 2**
- Major (serious injury) → **SIL 1**

### Method B — Safety Layer Matrix (Semi-Quantitative)
A 2-D matrix with **Consequence Severity** on one axis and **Initiating Event Frequency** on the other. The cell in which the scenario falls dictates the SIL.

### Method C — Risk Graph (Semi-Quantitative) ⭐ Most Tested in KTU
Originated by Prof. Wolfgang R. B. J. (now standardised in IEC 61508 Part 5, Annex F). Four parameters are scored:

| Parameter | Symbol | Options | Score (W$_X$) |
|-----------|--------|---------|---------------|
| Consequence | C | C$_A$ minor, C$_B$ serious, C$_C$ very serious, C$_D$ catastrophic | 0 to ∞ (5 typical) |
| Frequency of occupancy / exposure | F | F$_A$ rare→F$_B$ frequent | 1 to 5 |
| Possibility of avoidance | P | P$_A$ possible under certain conditions, P$_B$ almost impossible | 1 to 5 |
| Demand rate (initiating event) | W | W$_1$ < $10^{-2}$/yr, W$_2$ $10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$, W$_3$ $10^{-1}$–1, … | up to 7 |

**Calculation rule:**
$$ W = W_C + W_F + W_P + W_W $$

| Total W | SIL Required |
|---------|--------------|
| W < 60 | No SIL |
| 60 ≤ W < 70 | SIL 1 |
| 70 ≤ W < 80 | SIL 2 |
| 80 ≤ W < 90 | SIL 3 |
| W ≥ 90 | SIL 4 |

### Method D — Quantitative (Risk Reduction Factor)
Computes the actual RRF using the **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** principle.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **only** closed-form equations required for KTU 2024 Module-2 SIL Assignment problems. Memorise the symbols — they appear in every numerical.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor of a SIF | dimensionless |
| 2 | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{RRF}$ | Average Probability of Failure on Demand | dimensionless (per demand) |
| 3 | $RRF_{total} = \dfrac{F_{initiating}}{f_{tolerable}}$ | Quantitative SIL target | dimensionless |
| 4 | $RRF_{required} = RRF_{total} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} IPL_i$ | After subtracting existing IPLs | dimensionless |
| 5 | $PFD_{avg,SIS} = PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{logic} + PFD_{final\,element}$ | Sum of sub-system PFDs (IEC 61508) | per demand |
| 6 | $f_{tolerable} = \dfrac{Risk\,Tolerable}{Consequence\,Cost}$ | Tolerable frequency derivation | events/yr |
| 7 | $W = W_C + W_F + W_P + W_W$ | Risk-Graph score | numeric |
| 8 | $f_{n,p} = \lambda_D \times t_{CE}$ | Probability of failure on demand (single channel, proof-test interval $t_{CE}$) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $t_{CE} = \dfrac{T_i}{2}$ | Average channel equivalent downtime = half of proof-test interval | hours |
| 10 | $\sum f = f_{initiating} \times \prod PFD_{IPLs}$ | Layer-of-Protection Analysis (LOPA) frequency | events/yr |

> [!NOTE]
> The Greek symbols used above are:
> - $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate of a component (failures/hour)
> - $T_i$ = proof-test interval (hours)
> - $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime (hours)
> - $f_{n,p}$ = frequency of the hazardous event after n protection layers (LOPA)

## 2.4 The LOPA Bridge — Linking SIL to Layer of Protection Analysis

LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis) is the KTU-recommended quantitative bridge. The general form:

$$ f_{event,n} = f_{initiating\,event} \times PFD_{IPL\,1} \times PFD_{IPL\,2} \times \ldots \times PFD_{IPL\,n} $$

If after subtracting all IPLs the residual frequency $f_{event,n}$ is still above the tolerable target $f_{tolerable}$, the **SIF** is the additional IPL with required PFD$_{avg}$:

$$ PFD_{avg,SIF} = \dfrac{f_{tolerable}}{f_{event,n}} $$

The SIL is then read directly from the IEC 61508 table (Section 1.1 above).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Industry | Typical SIL Demand | Example |
|----------|-------------------|---------|
| Refineries | SIL 1–2 | Furnace over-pressure protection |
| Petrochemical | SIL 2–3 | Ethylene cracker reactor quench |
| LNG / Cryogenic | SIL 2–3 | LNG storage leak detection |
| Pharmaceutical | SIL 2 | Steriliser steam isolation |
| Power Plants | SIL 3 | Boiler drum level trip |

The IEC 61511 sector standard restricts process industries to **SIL 1–3**. Where the analysis demands SIL 4, the plant must be re-engineered (additional IPLs, inherently safer design) before the SIS is added.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Quantitative Method (LOPA → SIL)

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
A hydrocarbon pump delivers gasoline. Initiating event = seal failure (estimated $f_i = 0.1$ /yr). Existing IPLs: (a) flame detector with PFD = $10^{-1}$, (b) operator response with PFD = $10^{-1}$, (c) concrete dike with PFD = $10^{-2}$. Tolerable frequency of a major spill = $10^{-6}$ /yr. Determine the required SIL of the new Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute residual frequency after existing IPLs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{event,n} &= f_{initiating} \times PFD_{IPL1} \times PFD_{IPL2} \times PFD_{IPL3} \\
&= 0.1 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 0.1 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 10^{-5} \text{ events/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** We multiply the initiating-event frequency by the probability that *each* IPL *fails to act*. Three IPLs are credited, hence the factor $10^{-4}$.

**Step 2 — Compare residual to tolerable:**

Since $f_{event,n} = 10^{-5}$ /yr and tolerable $f_{tol} = 10^{-6}$ /yr, the residual is **10 times higher** than tolerable.

**Step 3 — Compute required RRF of the SIF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF_{SIF} &= \frac{f_{event,n}}{f_{tolerable}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-6}} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert RRF to PFD$_{avg}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg,SIF} &= \frac{1}{RRF_{SIF}} = \frac{1}{10} = 10^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Read SIL from the IEC 61508 table:**

$PFD_{avg} = 10^{-1}$ falls in the band **$10^{-2} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-1}$** ⇒ **SIL 1**.

**Conclusion:** A SIL 1 SIF is the minimum acceptable. If management applies an ALARP safety factor of 10, a **SIL 2** SIF would be selected.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Risk Graph Method

**Problem Statement:**
A chlorine railcar unloading station has the following risk-graph parameters: Consequence C = C$_C$ (very serious, several fatalities possible), Exposure F = F$_B$ (frequent presence of personnel), Avoidance P = P$_B$ (almost impossible to escape from chlorine cloud), Demand rate W = W$_3$ (initiating event occurs between 0.1 and 1 time per year). Determine the SIL.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the parameter scores from the Risk Graph calibration table:**

For a typical IEC 61508 Annex F calibration:

| Parameter | Chosen Option | Score ($W_X$) |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| Consequence C | C$_C$ | $W_C = 10$ |
| Exposure F | F$_B$ | $W_F = 5$ |
| Avoidance P | P$_B$ | $W_P = 5$ |
| Demand rate W | W$_3$ | $W_W = 7$ |

**Step 2 — Compute the total W:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= W_C + W_F + W_P + W_W \\
&= 10 + 5 + 5 + 7 \\
&= 27
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** The Risk-Graph method assigns a discrete numerical weight to each qualitative parameter. The sum W is then mapped to a SIL band.

**Step 3 — Map W to SIL:**

Using the standard calibration (Buncefield-aligned):

| Range of W | SIL |
|------------|-----|
| W ≤ 7 | No SIL required |
| 8 ≤ W ≤ 13 | SIL 1 |
| 14 ≤ W ≤ 24 | SIL 2 |
| 25 ≤ W ≤ 32 | SIL 3 |
| W ≥ 33 | SIL 4 |

Since $W = 27$, the required level is **SIL 3**.

**Conclusion:** The chlorine railcar unloading station requires a **SIL 3** Safety Instrumented Function (typically a hard-wired Emergency Shutdown System with redundant chlorine gas detectors, solenoid block valves, and vent scrubber activation).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Safety Layer Matrix (SLM) Method

**Problem Statement:**
A reactor has the following combination: Consequence = "Major" (single fatality) and Initiating Event Frequency = $10^{-1}$ per year. Use a standard SLM to find the SIL.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Position the scenario on the SLM matrix:**

| Consequence ↓ / Initiating Event Frequency → | $<10^{-2}$/yr | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$/yr | $>10^{-1}$/yr |
|---------------------------------------------|----------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Minor (no injury) | — | — | SIL 1 |
| Major (serious injury) | — | SIL 1 | **SIL 2** |
| Severe (single fatality) | SIL 1 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 |
| Catastrophic (multiple fatalities) | SIL 2 | SIL 3 | SIL 4 |

**Step 2 — Read the cell:**

Consequence = "Major", Initiating-event frequency = $10^{-1}$/yr → Cell intersects at **SIL 2**.

**Conclusion:** The reactor requires a **SIL 2** SIF.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Four Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners often award **5 marks** in Part B for a clean comparison table. Memorise the structure below.

| Aspect | Qualitative (Consequence) | Safety Layer Matrix | Risk Graph | Quantitative (LOPA) |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------------|------------|---------------------|
| Data demand | Very low | Low | Low–Medium | Very high |
| Demand-rate input | Not used | Categorical | Categorical ($W_i$) | Numerical |
| Existing IPLs | Not credited | Not credited | Not credited | Fully credited |
| Output resolution | SIL band | SIL band | SIL band | Exact PFD$_{avg}$ |
| Time to apply | Minutes | Hours | Hours | Days |
| Best application | Early screening | Conservative design | Standard process plants | Complex / high-hazard plants |
| Standards reference | IEC 61511 §5.2.6.1.1 | IEC 61511 §5.2.6.1.2 | IEC 61508 Part 5 Annex F | IEC 61511 §5.2.6.1.5 |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Insight — PFD$_{avg}$ vs Proof-Test Interval

A common KTU sub-question probes how the **proof-test interval ($T_i$)** influences whether a designed SIS achieves its target SIL. For a simple 1oo1 (one-out-of-one) sensor channel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \lambda_D \times \frac{T_i}{2} \\
\text{(1oo1 channel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical illustration:** Sensor dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr, $T_i = 8760$ hr (1 year).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= 5 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{8760}{2} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380 \\
&= 2.19 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This falls in the band $10^{-2} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-1}$ ⇒ **SIL 1 only**. To upgrade to SIL 2 ($PFD < 10^{-2}$) one must either (a) halve $T_i$ to 6 months, or (b) upgrade to a 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) voted channel.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level SIL Determination Workflow (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: HAZOP Output] --> B[Identify Hazardous Event]
    B --> C[Estimate Consequence Severity C]
    B --> D[Estimate Initiating Event Frequency f_i]
    C --> E[Choose SIL Method]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Method Selected?}
    F -- Qualitative --> G[Consequence-Based Look-up]
    F -- Semi-Q SLM --> H[Safety Layer Matrix Look-up]
    F -- Semi-Q Risk Graph --> I[Score W = Wc + Wf + Wp + Ww]
    F -- Quantitative LOPA --> J[Compute f_event,n via IPL chain]
    G --> K[Determine Target SIL]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{SIL ≤ 3?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Design SIS at Target SIL]
    L -- No --> N[Apply Inherently Safer Design or Add More IPLs]
    N --> A
    M --> O[Document SIL Assignment Record]
    O --> P[End: Proceed to SIS Engineering]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The process begins with HAZOP, branches into the four SIL methods, converges on a target SIL, and loops back if the demand exceeds SIL 3 (forcing a re-design). This mirrors the IEC 61511 Safety Life Cycle, Phase 3.

---

## 4.2 LOPA Bow-Tie Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initiating Event: Seal Failure] --> B[IPL 1: Flame Detector]
    B --> C[IPL 2: Operator Action]
    C --> D[IPL 3: Containment Dike]
    D --> E[IPL 4: SIS SIF]
    E --> F[Tolerable Frequency Achieved]
    A -.->|f_i = 0.1 per yr| F
```

> **Reading guide:** The arrow chain represents the sequential IPLs. The dashed feedback arrow shows the tolerable-frequency target that the cumulative product must beat. Each IPL has its own PFD; only when the chain reduces the event frequency to below $f_{tolerable}$ is the system deemed safe.

---

## 4.3 Risk-Graph Decision Tree (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Hazard Scenario] --> Q1{Consequence Severity?}
    Q1 -- C_A Minor --> Q1a[Score 1]
    Q1 -- C_B Serious --> Q1b[Score 3]
    Q1 -- C_C Very Serious --> Q1c[Score 7]
    Q1 -- C_D Catastrophic --> Q1d[Score 10]
    Q1a --> Sum
    Q1b --> Sum
    Q1c --> Sum
    Q1d --> Sum
    Sum[Add Scores of C F P W] --> Map{Map W to SIL}
    Map -- W ≤ 60 --> NoSIL[No SIL Required]
    Map -- 60 to 70 --> SIL1[SIL 1]
    Map -- 70 to 80 --> SIL2[SIL 2]
    Map -- 80 to 90 --> SIL3[SIL 3]
    Map -- W ≥ 90 --> SIL4[SIL 4]
```

> **Reading guide:** This decision tree condenses the Risk-Graph logic. A student can mechanically walk through the four parameters, sum the scores, and read off the SIL.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q:** Define Safety Integrity Level (SIL). List the four SILs with their target Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{avg}$) ranges as per IEC 61508.

**Model Answer:**
SIL is a discrete level (1–4) used to specify the integrity requirements of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) operating in low-demand mode. Each SIL corresponds to a target range of average probability of failure on demand.

| SIL | PFD$_{avg}$ Range |
|-----|-------------------|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the table)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q:** Differentiate between qualitative and quantitative methods of SIL Assignment. State one limitation of each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Qualitative methods** (Consequence-based, Safety Layer Matrix, Risk Graph) use categorical scores; they are fast but produce a SIL band rather than a precise PFD. *Limitation:* They do not credit existing Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) and may over-specify the SIS.
- **Quantitative methods** (LOPA) compute a numerical PFD$_{avg}$ from failure-rate data and IPL credits. *Limitation:* They require extensive, validated failure-rate data (e.g., OREDA, FARADIP) and consume significant engineering effort.

*(3 marks: 1+1 for contrast, 1 for limitation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — KTU 2024 Internal Choice Option

**Q (a)** [7 Marks]
Explain the four parameters of the IEC 61508 Risk-Graph method for SIL Assignment. Show how the total score W is mapped to a SIL level. *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Consequence (C):** Classifies the worst-case harm from the hazardous event — C$_A$ minor injury, C$_B$ serious injury, C$_C$ very serious (single fatality), C$_D$ catastrophic (multiple fatalities).

**(2) Frequency of Exposure / Occupancy (F):** Indicates how often personnel are present in the hazard zone — F$_A$ rarely to F$_B$ continuously.

**(3) Possibility of Avoidance (P):** Assesses whether the operator can escape or correct the situation once the hazard develops — P$_A$ avoidable under certain conditions, P$_B$ almost impossible to escape.

**(4) Demand Rate (W):** Estimated initiating-event frequency — W$_1$ less than $10^{-2}$/yr up to W$_7$ greater than 100/yr.

Each parameter is awarded a score ($W_C$, $W_F$, $W_P$, $W_W$); the sum $W = W_C + W_F + W_P + W_W$ is mapped to a SIL per the calibrated table:

| W Range | SIL |
|---------|-----|
| W ≤ 60 | No SIL |
| 60–70 | SIL 1 |
| 70–80 | SIL 2 |
| 80–90 | SIL 3 |
| ≥ 90 | SIL 4 |

*(7 marks: 1 each × 4 for parameters + 2 for mapping + 1 for example)*

---

**Q (b)** [7 Marks]
A distillate column has a high-pressure initiating event with frequency $f_i = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ /yr. Existing IPLs are (i) pressure-relief valve with PFD = $10^{-1}$, (ii) operator intervention with PFD = $10^{-1}$. Tolerable event frequency is $10^{-6}$ /yr. Determine the required SIL of the new high-pressure SIF using LOPA. *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute residual frequency after existing IPLs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{event} &= f_i \times PFD_{PRV} \times PFD_{Operator} \\
&= 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 10^{-3} \text{ events/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Required RRF of SIF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF_{SIF} &= \frac{f_{event}}{f_{tolerable}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} = 10^{3} = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Required PFD$_{avg}$ of SIF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg,SIF} &= \frac{1}{RRF_{SIF}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Read SIL:** $PFD_{avg} = 10^{-3}$ falls in the band $10^{-3} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$ ⇒ **SIL 2**.

*(7 marks: 1 for IPL multiplication, 1 for RRF, 1 for PFD, 2 for SIL mapping, 2 for narrative)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice Option

**Q (a)** [7 Marks]
Describe in detail the four methods of SIL Assignment as per IEC 61508 / IEC 61511. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Consequence-Based (Qualitative):** Assigns SIL purely on severity classification. Used only when demand rate is very low (less than $10^{-2}$/yr). Fast screening tool. Example: minor injury → SIL 0; serious injury → SIL 1; fatality → SIL 2; multiple fatalities → SIL 3.

**(2) Safety Layer Matrix (Semi-Quantitative):** 2-D matrix of Consequence Severity × Initiating Event Frequency. The intersecting cell determines the SIL. No credit to existing IPLs.

**(3) Risk Graph (Semi-Quantitative):** Four parameters C, F, P, W are scored and summed. Total W is mapped to SIL. Standardised in IEC 61508 Part 5, Annex F. Most widely used in process industries.

**(4) Quantitative (LOPA):** Calculates residual event frequency using a multiplicative IPL chain. The SIF is the additional IPL that brings the residual below the tolerable target. Produces an exact PFD$_{avg}$ and SIL.

The four methods exist on a **risk-data spectrum** — qualitative methods need little data but yield coarse SIL bands; LOPA needs extensive data but yields precise, defensible results.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks each for the four methods + 1 mark comparison)*

---

**Q (b)** [7 Marks]
A chlorine storage vessel has a runaway-reaction initiating event with frequency $5 \times 10^{-2}$ /yr. Existing IPLs: (i) rupture disc PFD = $10^{-1}$, (ii) alarm + operator with PFD = $10^{-1}$. Tolerable frequency of a toxic release = $10^{-5}$ /yr. Apply the Quantitative method to determine the required SIL. *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute residual frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{event} &= 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Required RRF of the SIF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF_{SIF} &= \frac{f_{event}}{f_{tolerable}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-5}} = 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Required PFD$_{avg}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 = 2 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — SIL Mapping:** $2 \times 10^{-2}$ lies between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-1}$ ⇒ **SIL 1**.

**Step 5 — ALARP Check:** If a safety factor of 10 is mandated by company policy, an additional decade of risk reduction is needed ⇒ upgrade to **SIL 2** for ALARP compliance.

*(7 marks: 1 for LIPA, 1 for RRF, 1 for PFD, 2 for SIL, 2 for ALARP commentary)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in SIL Assignment Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Initiating-event frequency $f_i$ must be in **events/year**, not dimensionless. A PFD is dimensionless. Conflating the two costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to credit existing IPLs:** Beginners often compute the SIF PFD as $f_i / f_{tolerable}$ directly, ignoring that **relief valves, alarms, and operator actions** already contribute risk reduction. This yields an unnecessarily high SIL and loses 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Mis-mapping PFD to SIL:** $PFD = 10^{-3}$ is **SIL 2**, NOT SIL 3. Always use the **band**: $10^{-3}$ ≤ PFD < $10^{-2}$ = SIL 2.
> 4. **Confusing SIL 4 inclusion:** In process industries, SIL 4 is **not normally applied**. If your calculation yields SIL 4, the examiner expects you to note that the hazard must be reduced by other means.
> 5. **Skipping the ALARP commentary:** Part (b) 14-mark questions almost always expect a concluding "ALARP justification" sentence — do not omit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points for SIL Assignment — KTU 2024**

- ✅ **SIL** = discrete level of risk reduction; defined in IEC 61508 (generic) and applied via IEC 61511 (process).
- ✅ **PFD$_{avg}$ bands:** SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4 = $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- ✅ **RRF = 1 / PFD$_{avg}$** — memorise this single equation; it is the foundation of every numerical.
- ✅ **Four SIL Assignment methods:** Consequence-based (qualitative), Safety Layer Matrix, Risk Graph (semi-quantitative), LOPA (quantitative).
- ✅ **Risk-Graph parameters:** C (Consequence), F (Frequency of exposure), P (Possibility of avoidance), W (Demand rate). Sum → SIL.
- ✅ **LOPA chain:** $f_{event,n} = f_{initiating} \times PFD_{IPL1} \times PFD_{IPL2} \times \ldots$
- ✅ **Process-industry ceiling:** SIL 1, 2, 3 only. SIL 4 → re-engineer.
- ✅ **ALARP principle:** If the calculated SIL produces an unacceptably high residual risk, apply the As Low As Reasonably Practicable test and add one SIL level.
- ✅ **High-demand mode:** Uses PFH (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour), NOT PFD$_{avg}$. KTU Module 2 focuses on low-demand mode.
- ✅ **PFD$_{avg}$ of 1oo1 channel:** $\lambda_D \times (T_i / 2)$. Shortening the proof-test interval $T_i$ is the most economical way to drop the SIL boundary.
- ✅ **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** is a device, system, or action that is capable of preventing a scenario from proceeding to its undesired consequence, **independent** of the initiating event and any other IPL.
- ✅ **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** is an Order-of-Magnitude (10⁻¹, 10⁻², …) semi-quantitative risk assessment method that uses the IPL chain.
- ✅ **HAZOP → SIL Assignment → SIS Design → Installation → Operation → Modification** is the IEC 61511 Safety Life Cycle; SIL Assignment is **Phase 3**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Decisions and Making Recommendations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Risk Decisions and Making Recommendations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Risk Decision-Making** is the systematic, evidence-based process of evaluating identified and analyzed risks against established acceptance criteria, organizational tolerance thresholds, and regulatory requirements, in order to select an appropriate course of action. **Making Recommendations** is the culminating step where the safety professional translates technical risk findings into prioritized, actionable, and resource-justified advice presented to decision-makers (management, regulators, or stakeholders). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Risk decisions sit at the apex of the risk management process (ISO 31000 framework). The output of risk analysis becomes meaningful only when converted into defensible decisions and recommendations. In KTU ESE papers, students must demonstrate the link between risk **evaluation** (Module 2) and **treatment/recommendation**. The decision-making architecture rests on three pillars: 1. **Risk Evaluation Criteria** – Quantitative or qualitative thresholds (e.g., individual risk of fatality ≤ 1×10⁻⁶/yr for the public; F-N curves). 2. **Decision Framework** – ALARP, GAMAB, MEM, Cost-Benefit, or Risk Matrix. 3. **Recommendation Logic** – Hierarchy of Controls (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Triage Model":** > Imagine a doctor in an emergency room. After diagnosing patients (risk analysis), the doctor must decide **who gets treated first, who gets a bedside prescription, and who can go home with advice**. The doctor uses a **triage scale** (red/yellow/green) — this is the **risk evaluation matrix**. Then the doctor prescribes a course of action — surgery, medicine, or rest — based on severity, resources, and practicality. This prescription handed to the patient and family is the **recommendation**. In safety engineering, the *workplace* is the patient, the *risk register* is the diagnosis, the *risk matrix* is the triage scale, and the *recommendation report* is the prescription. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Used Universally in KTU Board Exams) - **Individual Risk (IR):** Probability of fatality per person per year. Unit: **fatalities/person/year** (e.g., 1×10⁻⁵). - **Societal Risk (SR):** Frequency (F) of accidents causing N or more fatalities. Unit: **events/year**. - **Risk Tolerance Threshold:** IR ≤ **1×10⁻⁶ /year** (UK HSE public benchmark). - **Discount Rate (for cost-benefit):** Typically **3%–10%** real in KTU numericals. --- ## 1.4 Risk Acceptance Criteria — The Decision Compass | Criterion Type | Definition | Example Metric | |---|---|---| | **De Minimis** | Risk so small it can be ignored | IR < 1×10⁻⁷/yr | | **Tolerable (ALARP region)** | Risk accepted only if residual risk is ALARP | UK HSE 1×10⁻⁶ → 1×10⁻⁴/yr | | **Intolerable** | Risk must be reduced regardless of cost | IR > 1×10⁻⁴/yr | | **GAMAB** | "Globalement Au Moins Aussi Bon" — risk not greater than existing comparable installations | Used in France, transport | | **MEM** | "Minimum Endogenous Mortality" — risk not greater than natural mortality increment | Netherlands, Germany | | **Cost per Statistical Life Saved (CSLS)** | Economic measure of risk reduction | $3M–$10M per life | --- ## 1.5 Why This Topic Carries High KTU Weightage > [!NOTE] > Module 2 typically contributes **20–25 marks** of the ESE paper. Questions on ALARP justification, risk matrix construction, and recommendation hierarchies are **direct 7-mark sub-parts**. A well-structured risk recommendation answer can secure full marks with the right terminology, which is why KTU examiners reward the use of the **Hierarchy of Controls** and **ALARP test** language explicitly. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Risk Decision-Making Pipeline (ISO 31000 Aligned) The decision flow follows a **closed-loop** structure. Each step is a gate — the recommendation cannot be issued unless the previous gate is cleared. ### Step 1 — Risk Re-Confirmation - Revisit the risk register and the analyzed risk values. - Cross-check assumptions, confidence intervals, and uncertainty bands. ### Step 2 — Compare Against Acceptance Criteria - Plot each risk on the **Individual Risk (IR) scale** and the **F-N curve**. - A risk that crosses the **intolerable line** triggers mandatory treatment. - A risk below **de minimis** requires no action. - A risk in the **tolerable/ALARP region** triggers the **ALARP test** (cost-benefit + good practice). ### Step 3 — Apply Decision Frameworks Select the appropriate decision framework: - **Matrix-based** (qualitative/semi-quantitative) - **ALARP-based** (UK HSE) - **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)** (US OSHA, regulatory) - **Risk-Informed Decision Making (RIDM)** (nuclear, aerospace) ### Step 4 — Identify Risk Treatment Options Apply the **Hierarchy of Controls**: 1. **Elimination** (most effective, highest cost initially) 2. **Substitution** (replace hazard source) 3. **Engineering Controls** (guards, interlocks, ventilation) 4. **Administrative Controls** (procedures, training, signage) 5. **PPE** (last resort, lowest reliability) ### Step 5 — Justify the Recommendation - Link control to hazard. - Quantify **residual risk** after control. - Show **cost vs. risk reduction** trade-off. - State residual risk position on the matrix/F-N curve. ### Step 6 — Communication and Documentation - Write a **Risk Treatment Plan** with measurable KPIs. - Define **monitoring, review, and audit** cadence. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes the **must-know equations** for KTU 2024 ESE numericals on this topic. Master these — they appear in 7-mark and 14-mark Part B questions. | # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Individual Risk | $IR = f \times p$ | $f$ = frequency; $p$ = probability of fatality given accident. Unit: **/person/year** | | 2 | Societal Risk (F-N curve) | $F_N \cdot N^k = C$ | $F_N$ = frequency of N+ fatalities; $k$ = aversion slope (typically 1–2); $C$ = constant | | 3 | ALARP Cost-Benefit (Gross Implied Cost) | $GIC = \frac{Cost\ of\ Safety\ Measure}{Reduction\ in\ Statistical\ Risk\ (lives)}$ | Compared with **VSL** (Value of Statistical Life). If GIC $\le$ VSL → ALARP satisfied | | 4 | Cost-Benefit Ratio | $BCR = \frac{\Delta Risk\ Reduction\ in\ monetary\ value}{Cost\ of\ measure}$ | BCR $\ge 1$ → justifiable measure | | 5 | Present Value of Future Cost | $PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | $r$ = discount rate; $n$ = years | | 6 | Risk Score (Matrix) | $R = S \times L$ | $S$ = Severity (1–5); $L$ = Likelihood (1–5); $R$ in 1–25 | | 7 | Residual Risk | $R_{residual} = R_{initial} - \Delta R_{control}$ | After treatment | | 8 | Expected Loss | $E(L) = \sum_i f_i \cdot C_i$ | $f_i$ = frequency of scenario $i$; $C_i$ = consequence cost | | 9 | FN Curve Slope Check | $\frac{F_{N_1}}{F_{N_2}} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^k$ | Determines if risk is **above** or **below** tolerance line | | 10 | Implied Cost of Averting a Fatality (ICAF) | $ICAF = \frac{\Delta Cost}{\Delta Risk\ (in\ fatalities)}$ | Compared to societal benchmark | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When using the **|** symbol in a math table, always escape as `\vert` or `\mid`. KTU valuation scripts occasionally parse `|` and break the markdown rendering. Example: $|x|$ should be written as $\vert x\vert$ or $\mid x \mid$. --- ## 2.3 The ALARP Test — Three Legal Pillars (UK HSE Origin, Globally Adopted) The **As Low As Reasonably Practicable** test has three components. A risk is ALARP only if **all three** are demonstrated. 1. **Inherent Safety Question** — Has the hazard been eliminated or substituted? If not, why? 2. **Good Practice Comparison** — Does the industry/regulatory standard require this control? (e.g., IEC 61511, API 754, OSHA 1910). 3. **Cost-Benefit Test** — Is the **implied cost of saving a statistical life (ICAF)** disproportionately high? HSE benchmark ≈ **£1.3M–£1.7M per statistical life saved (2010 GBP, indexed)**. > [!NOTE] > **In KTU answers, explicitly state:** *"The control measure is selected because (a) it follows good industry practice per [standard], (b) the gross implied cost of averting a fatality is ₹X crore which is below the HSE benchmark, and (c) further reduction would require gross disproportion in cost. Hence the residual risk is ALARP."* This is full-marks language. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Process Industry (Oil & Gas, Petrochemicals):** ALARP + Safety Case preparation (UK COMAH regulations). - **Nuclear Power:** RIDM uses PRA (Probabilistic Risk Assessment) + defense-in-depth. - **Construction:** Risk Register + Permit-to-Work systems. - **Aviation:** SMS (Safety Management System) ICAO Doc 9859. - **Automotive:** ISO 26262 ASIL classification & FMEA-driven recommendations. - **Manufacturing:** OSHAS 18001 / ISO 45001 risk treatment plans. --- ## 2.5 Decision Frameworks — Comparative Matrix | Framework | Basis | Strength | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | **Risk Matrix** | Likelihood × Severity | Simple, visual | Resolution limited; ordinal scaling | | **ALARP** | Cost vs. Risk reduction | Legally robust (UK HSE) | Subjective "gross disproportion" | | **CBA** | Monetary valuation | Quantitative, transparent | Difficult to value human life | | **RIDM** | PRA + insights | Comprehensive (nuclear-grade) | Data-intensive, expensive | | **GAMAB** | Comparative | Transparent | Requires comparable baseline | | **Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA)** | Weighted scoring | Handles qualitative factors | Weighting subjectivity | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: ALARP Cost-Benefit Test (Gross Implied Cost of Averting a Fatality) The **Gross Implied Cost (GIC)** of a safety measure is derived by dividing the annualized cost of the measure by the statistical lives it saves per year. ### Step 1 — Define Annualized Cost of the Measure The annualized cost $C_a$ is computed from the capital cost $C_c$ amortized over $n$ years at discount rate $r$, plus the annual operating cost $C_o$. $$ C_a = \frac{C_c}{\left[\frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right]} + C_o $$ ### Step 2 — Define the Statistical Risk Reduction The statistical risk reduction is the **expected number of fatalities averted per year** $\Delta R$: $$ \Delta R = \sum_{i=1}^{m} f_i \cdot p_i - \sum_{i=1}^{m} f_i' \cdot p_i' $$ where $f_i$ is the original frequency of scenario $i$, $f_i'$ is the frequency after the control, and $p_i$ is the conditional probability of fatality. ### Step 3 — Compute GIC Divide annualized cost by statistical risk reduction: $$ GIC = \frac{C_a}{\Delta R} $$ ### Step 4 — Decision Rule Compare with the **Value of a Statistical Life (VSL)** benchmark. The measure is **ALARP-justified** if: $$ GIC \leq \text{VSL benchmark} $$ If $GIC \gg \text{VSL}$, the measure is **grossly disproportionate** in cost, and the existing residual risk is deemed ALARP. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem Statement:** > A chemical plant has a runaway reaction scenario with the following data: > - Original frequency: $f = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ /year > - Probability of fatality given accident: $p = 0.4$ > - Two operators exposed in the affected zone. > > A proposed engineering control (interlock + relief valve upgrade) costs: > - Capital cost: ₹80 lakh > - Annual operating cost: ₹6 lakh/year > - Useful life: 10 years > - Discount rate: 8% > - New frequency after control: $f' = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ /year > - Same $p = 0.4$ > > Using VSL benchmark = ₹10 crore per statistical life, determine whether the measure is ALARP-justified. ### Solution **Step A — Compute Original Risk** $$ IR_{original} = f \times p \times N_{exposed} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4 \times 2 $$ $$ IR_{original} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities/year} $$ **Step B — Compute Residual Risk After Control** $$ IR_{residual} = f' \times p \times N_{exposed} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.4 \times 2 $$ $$ IR_{residual} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ fatalities/year} $$ **Step C — Compute Statistical Risk Reduction $\Delta R$** $$ \Delta R = IR_{original} - IR_{residual} = 4 \times 10^{-3} - 4 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \Delta R = 3.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ statistical fatalities averted per year} $$ **Step D — Annualized Cost of Measure** First, compute the annuity factor $A(r, n)$: $$ A(r, n) = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08} $$ $$ (1.08)^{10} \approx 2.1589 $$ $$ (1.08)^{-10} \approx 0.4632 $$ $$ A(0.08, 10) = \frac{1 - 0.4632}{0.08} = \frac{0.5368}{0.08} = 6.7101 $$ Capital recovery per year: $$ \frac{C_c}{A} = \frac{80,00,000}{6.7101} \approx 11,922,420 \text{ ₹/year} \approx \text{₹1.192 lakh/year? No — unit check:} $$ Let me re-check: ₹80,00,000 / 6.7101 ≈ ₹11,92,200 per year. (Roughly ₹11.92 lakh/year.) Add operating cost $C_o$ = ₹6,00,000/year: $$ C_a = 11,92,200 + 6,00,000 = 17,92,200 \text{ ₹/year} \approx \text{₹17.92 lakh/year} $$ **Step E — Compute GIC (Gross Implied Cost per Statistical Life Saved)** $$ GIC = \frac{C_a}{\Delta R} = \frac{17,92,200}{3.96 \times 10^{-3}} $$ $$ GIC \approx 4.525 \times 10^{8} \text{ ₹ per statistical life} $$ $$ GIC \approx \text{₹45.25 crore per statistical life} $$ **Step F — Decision** $$ GIC = \text{₹45.25 crore} \gg \text{VSL benchmark} = \text{₹10 crore} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Since GIC vastly exceeds the benchmark, the measure is **grossly disproportionate** in cost. A **cheaper alternative control** (e.g., procedural administrative controls with operator training at ₹3 lakh/year reducing frequency to $1 \times 10^{-4}$/year) should be considered first. The current measure is **NOT ALARP-justified** at this cost — further cost reduction or alternative engineering is required. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: From Risk Score to Recommendation The following worked example demonstrates a **complete KTU 14-mark style recommendation** flow. **Scenario:** A maintenance workshop has identified four hazards. Risk scores calculated as $R = S \times L$ (Severity 1–5, Likelihood 1–5). | Hazard ID | Hazard | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) | R = S×L | Class | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | H-01 | Lack of LOTO procedure | 5 | 4 | **20** | High | | H-02 | Improper PPE usage | 3 | 4 | **12** | Medium | | H-03 | Slippery floor near wash area | 2 | 3 | **6** | Low-Medium | | H-04 | Improper chemical storage | 5 | 2 | **10** | Medium | ### Recommendation Engine (Analytical) For each hazard, follow this **decision logic**: 1. If $R \geq 15$ → **Immediate action**, escalate to top management, mandatory engineering control. 2. If $8 \leq R \leq 14$ → **Planned action**, apply hierarchy of controls, target 6-month closure. 3. If $R \leq 7$ → **Monitor**, document in risk register, periodic review. | Hazard | R | Decision | Recommended Control (Hierarchy) | Owner | Target Date | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | H-01 | 20 | Immediate | **Engineering:** Install LOTO devices & interlocks; **Admin:** Written energy-control procedure | Plant Manager | 30 days | | H-02 | 12 | Planned | **Substitution:** Replace with Type-5 disposable respirators; **Admin:** Refresher training | Safety Officer | 90 days | | H-03 | 6 | Monitor | **Admin:** Anti-skid mat, "wet floor" sign, weekly housekeeping audit | Supervisor | Routine | | H-04 | 10 | Planned | **Engineering:** Flammable cabinet, ventilation, grounding; **Substitution:** Replace with non-flammable solvent | Safety Officer | 120 days | --- ## 3.4 F-N Curve Interpretation (Societal Risk Decision) A refinery F-N plot shows the following pairs: | Scenario | N (fatalities) | F (events/year) | |---|---|---| | Major vapor cloud explosion | 25 | 1×10⁻⁵ | | Process unit fire | 8 | 1×10⁻⁴ | | LPG cylinder BLEVE | 50 | 1×10⁻⁶ | **Tolerance Line:** $F \cdot N^2 = 10^{-3}$ (UK HSE illustrative) Check if each scenario lies **above** (intolerable) or **below** (tolerable) the line. $$ F \cdot N^2 = 10^{-3} $$ | Scenario | $F \cdot N^2$ | Compare | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | 25 fatalities | $10^{-5} \cdot 25^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-3}$ | > 10⁻³ | **Above** (intolerable) | | 8 fatalities | $10^{-4} \cdot 8^2 = 6.4 \times 10^{-3}$ | > 10⁻³ | **Above** (intolerable) | | 50 fatalities | $10^{-6} \cdot 50^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ | > 10⁻³ | **Above** (intolerable) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision:** All three scenarios plot above the tolerance line → **mandatory risk reduction** required. Recommendations must target frequency reduction (one order of magnitude or more) to bring the F-N plot below the tolerance line. --- ## 3.5 Risk Treatment Selection — Decision Tree A formal decision tree is used when the choice of control is not obvious: 1. Is **elimination** physically possible? → Yes → adopt elimination. 2. If no, can **substitution** reduce the hazard? → Yes → adopt substitution. 3. If no, can **engineering control** reduce risk to ALARP? → Yes → adopt engineering. 4. If no, can **administrative control** achieve ALARP? → Yes → adopt administrative. 5. If no, use **PPE** as a supplementary measure. 6. Re-evaluate residual risk. If still above tolerable → escalate. --- # 4.
