## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`

**Q: As a newly appointed Safety Manager of a medium-scale manufacturing plant employing 380 workers, outline a comprehensive Implementation plan for the Occupational Health and Safety Management System (OHSMS) at your plant. Your answer must cover resource allocation, training, SOPs, emergency preparedness, and monitoring.**

#### (a) Resource Allocation and Organizational Structure (7 Marks)

The first step of implementation is **top management commitment** manifested through a **signed safety policy**, an **approved annual safety budget**, and the **establishment of a dedicated HSE department**.

**Organizational Structure to be created:**

- **HSE Manager** (1) — Overall responsibility for OHSMS.
- **Safety Officers** (minimum 2 per shift) — Shop-floor implementation.
- **Safety Committee** — Equal representation of management and workers (as per **Factories Act 1948, Section 41G**).
- **First Aiders** — At least **1 per 50 workers** during each shift.
- **Fire Wardens** — At least **1 per floor or per 200 workers**.
- **Emergency Response Team (ERT)** — Trained in fire, spill, and evacuation response.

**Resource Allocation Includes:**
- **Financial budget** — typically **2% to 5%** of operational expenditure in hazardous industries.
- **PPE inventory** — helmets, safety shoes, gloves, goggles, respirators, fall arresters.
- **Engineering controls** — machine guarding, ventilation systems, interlocks, fencing.
- **Inspection and monitoring equipment** — gas detectors, noise dosimeters, lux meters.

#### (b) Training, SOPs, Emergency Preparedness, and Monitoring (7 Marks)

**Training Plan:**
- **Induction training** — 2-day program for every new entrant before deployment.
- **Job-specific training** — Confined space, hot work, work-at-height, electrical safety, chemical handling.
- **Refresher training** — Every 6 months for high-risk tasks.
- **Mock drills** — Fire (twice a year), chemical spill (annually), evacuation (quarterly).

**Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs):**
- **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** for hot work, confined space, electrical isolation, work at height.
- **Lockout–Tagout–Tryout (LOTO)** for all energy sources during maintenance.
- **Hierarchy of Controls** strictly followed: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**.

**Emergency Preparedness:**
- **Emergency Response Plan (ERP)** with clearly defined roles.
- **Assembly points** marked and communicated to all workers.
- **First-aid kits and AEDs** placed at strategic locations.
- **Emergency contact tree** displayed at every entry point.

**Monitoring Framework:**
- **Daily safety inspections** by supervisors.
- **Weekly management walk-throughs** by senior managers.
- **Monthly internal audits** using ISO 45001 checklist.
- **Quarterly Management Review Meeting (MRM)** to evaluate KPIs.
- **Leading indicators** tracked: training coverage %, near-miss reports, audit scores.
- **Lagging indicators** tracked: LTIFR, TRIR, severity rate.

**Conclude:** A successful implementation requires **simultaneous execution** of all the above elements with active worker participation, supported by management review and continual improvement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: Explain the concept of Hierarchy of Controls in safety implementation. Describe how Permit-to-Work (PTW) and Lockout–Tagout (LOTO) systems are implemented in a hazardous industrial environment.**

#### (a) Hierarchy of Controls (7 Marks)

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is the **fundamental implementation framework** mandated by **ISO 45001:2018**, NIOSH, and OSHA. It prescribes a **ranked order of control measures** to be applied when dealing with workplace hazards. The principle is: **most effective at the top, least effective at the bottom.**

**The Five Levels (in descending order of effectiveness):**

1. **Elimination** — Physically removing the hazard. *Example:* Removing a toxic chemical from the process entirely.
2. **Substitution** — Replacing the hazard with a less dangerous alternative. *Example:* Replacing a carcinogenic solvent with a water-based cleaner.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Isolating people from the hazard through design. *Example:* Machine guarding, ventilation hoods, interlocks, sound enclosures.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Changing work procedures and behaviours. *Example:* SOPs, training, signage, rotation of workers.
5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — The **last line of defence**. *Example:* Helmets, gloves, respirators, earplugs.

**Key Principle:** PPE is placed at the bottom because its effectiveness **depends entirely on human compliance**. A worker who refuses to wear a helmet nullifies the control. Engineering controls, in contrast, **function by physical law** regardless of human behaviour.

#### (b) Permit-to-Work and Lockout–Tagout Systems (7 Marks)

**Permit-to-Work (PTW) System:**
A PTW is a **formal written authorization** issued before commencing **non-routine hazardous work**. It ensures that all hazards have been identified, controls are in place, and competent personnel are deployed.

**Types of PTW:**
- **Hot Work Permit** — Welding, cutting, grinding.
- **Confined Space Entry Permit** — Tanks, vessels, sewers.
- **Electrical Work Permit** — Isolation, maintenance, testing.
- **Work-at-Height Permit** — Above 1.8 meters or near edges.
- **Excavation Permit** — Digging operations.

**Implementation Steps:**
1. Identify the task and associated hazards.
2. The **Area In-charge** raises a PTW request.
3. **HSE Officer** reviews and approves with conditions.
4. The permit is **displayed at the work location**.
5. **Validity:** maximum one shift or 24 hours, whichever is shorter.
6. **Closure:** Permit is signed off after work completion and area inspection.

**Lockout–Tagout (LOTO) System:**
LOTO is a **mandatory energy isolation procedure** used during maintenance to ensure that machinery cannot be **unexpectedly energized, started, or released stored energy**.

**Six-Step LOTO Procedure:**
1. **Notify** all affected employees of impending shutdown.
2. **Identify** all energy sources (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, gravitational).
3. **Isolate** the energy source using appropriate devices (valves, breakers, blocks).
4. **Apply Lockout** devices with a unique personal key and **Tagout** label.
5. **Verify zero energy state** by attempting to start the machine (try-out step).
6. **Release** stored energy (drain capacitors, bleed pneumatics, lower suspended loads).

**Conclusion:** PTW and LOTO together form the **operational backbone of safe work implementation** in hazardous industries. Their strict adherence has been statistically proven to reduce maintenance-related fatalities by **over 70%**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Module 4 — Implementation questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Implementation with Planning.** Many students describe the entire PDCA cycle instead of focusing on the **"Do" phase**. Examiners allocate marks specifically for execution-level details like SOPs, PTW, LOTO, BBS, mock drills, and KPI tracking.
> 2. **Forgetting numerical units.** Always write **"per million man-hours"** next to LTIFR, **"per 200,000 man-hours"** next to Severity Rate. Marks are explicitly reserved for correct units.
> 3. **Listing Hierarchy of Controls in wrong order.** The order is **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**. Reversing the order is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Not stating the multiplier.** LTIFR uses **$10^6$**, while TRIR and Severity Rate use **$200{,}000$**. Mixing up these multipliers is a common error.
> 5. **Generic answers without industrial context.** Examiners reward answers that mention **Factories Act, ISO 45001, OSHA, NIOSH** or specific industries (oil & gas, construction, mining). A purely theoretical answer without context loses up to **3 marks**.
> 6. **No diagram.** A simple flowchart or hierarchy diagram in a 14-mark answer adds visual clarity and earns **1–2 extra marks** in examiner valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Implementation**

- ✅ Implementation is the **"Do" phase of the PDCA cycle** — the execution stage where plans become action.
- ✅ The **seven pillars** are: Resource Allocation, Communication, Training, Operational Controls, Emergency Preparedness, Documentation, and Monitoring.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls** must be applied in the exact order: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**.
- ✅ **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** is mandatory for non-routine hazardous tasks; maximum validity is **one shift or 24 hours**.
- ✅ **Lockout–Tagout–Tryout (LOTO)** is the six-step energy isolation procedure: **Notify → Identify → Isolate → Lockout & Tagout → Verify → Release**.
- ✅ **Behavioural-Based Safety (BBS)** targets the human factor — observing, coaching, and reinforcing safe behaviour.
- ✅ **LTIFR** = (LTI × $10^6$) ÷ Man-hours; benchmark is **< 1.0** per million man-hours.
- ✅ **Severity Rate** = (Lost days × $200{,}000$) ÷ Man-hours.
- ✅ **Emergency mock drills** must be conducted at least **twice a year** (fire) and quarterly (evacuation).
- ✅ **Leading indicators** (training coverage, audit scores) predict future performance; **Lagging indicators** (LTIFR, TRIR) record past performance — **monitor both**.
- ✅ **Safety Committee** must have equal worker and management representation as per **Factories Act 1948, Section 41G**.
- ✅ Successful implementation requires **top management commitment + worker participation + adequate resources + continuous monitoring**.
- ✅ **Without implementation, a safety policy is just a piece of paper — implementation is what saves lives.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Management Control and Review
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT831 - ADVANCED SAFETY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Management Control and Review

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ MANAGEMENT CONTROL AND REVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Management Control and Review (MCR)** in safety engineering is defined as the systematic, top-down governance process through which senior management plans, directs, monitors, evaluates, and continuously improves an organization's **Occupational Health and Safety (OHS)** performance. It operationalizes the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle to ensure that safety policies are effectively implemented, measurable, and aligned with statutory obligations under the **Factories Act 1948**, **OSHA standards**, and **ISO 45001:2018** frameworks.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MCR is treated as the **"control loop"** of the Safety Management System (SMS), transforming reactive accident recording into **proactive risk governance**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Car's Dashboard & Service Center**
>
> Imagine driving a car. Management Control is the **steering wheel and dashboard** — it tells the driver (management) how fast the car is going, how much fuel remains, and whether the engine is overheating. **Review** is the **periodic service visit** — a mechanic inspects what the dashboard reports and fixes problems *before* a breakdown.
>
> Without these, the car (organization) drives blind and eventually crashes (accident/incident). Safety MCR works identically: leaders continuously measure, steer, and service the safety system.

---

## 1.3 Core Pillars of Management Control & Review

The KTU syllabus recognizes **five interconnected pillars** that constitute effective Management Control and Review:

| Pillar | KTU 2024 Nomenclature | Core Function |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Safety Policy & Commitment** | Top-Down Governance Charter | Defines intent, authority, accountability |
| **2. Organizing for Safety** | Structural Architecture | Allocates roles, responsibilities, and resources |
| **3. Planning & Risk Control** | Forward-Looking Strategy | Hazard ID, risk assessment, objectives, KPIs |
| **4. Measurement & Monitoring** | Performance Telemetry | Active/reactive monitoring, leading/lagging indicators |
| **5. Management Review & Audit** | Strategic Verification | Periodic evaluation, corrective action, continual improvement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant Metric (KTU High-Yield):** The **Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR)**, expressed per **200,000 man-hours**, is the universally accepted **lagging indicator** benchmark in management review dashboards.

---

## 1.4 Why Management Control & Review? — The "Why"

- **Legal Compliance:** Statutory requirement under Section 7A of the **Factories Act 1948** and ISO 45001 Clause 9 (Performance Evaluation).
- **Accountability:** Establishes an unambiguous **chain of command** for safety decisions.
- **Resource Justification:** Provides empirical data to justify safety investments to boards and investors.
- **Cultural Embedment:** Sustains a **Proactive Safety Culture** rather than a reactive one.
- **Continual Improvement (CI):** The PDCA cycle institutionalizes learning from every near-miss and audit.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The PDCA Cycle Applied to Safety Management

The **Plan–Do–Check–Act (Deming Cycle)** is the operational backbone of Management Control and Review. Each quadrant carries specific safety functions:

### 🔹 PLAN — Establishing the Safety Roadmap
- Define the **Safety Policy** (signed by CEO/MD).
- Identify **Legal and Other Requirements** (Factories Act, BIS Standards, ISO 45001).
- Conduct **Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment (HIRA)**.
- Set **SMART Safety Objectives** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
- Establish **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**.

### 🔹 DO — Implementing the Plan
- Deploy **Safe Systems of Work (SSOW)**.
- Conduct **Toolbox Talks**, **Permit-to-Work (PTW)**, and **Job Safety Analyses (JSA)**.
- Deliver **Safety Training & Competency Programs**.
- Implement **Management of Change (MOC)** protocols.
- Maintain **Emergency Preparedness & Response** plans.

### 🔹 CHECK — Performance Monitoring & Review
- Conduct **Safety Inspections** (planned and unplanned).
- Perform **Internal Audits** (ISO 45001 Clause 9.2).
- Track **Leading Indicators** (training hours, near-miss reports) and **Lagging Indicators** (LTIFR, TRIR).
- Investigate incidents, near-misses, and dangerous occurrences.
- Review **Workplace Exposure Monitoring** data.

### 🔹 ACT — Management Review & Continual Improvement
- Conduct **Periodic Management Review Meetings** (quarterly/annually).
- Issue **Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)**.
- Update **Risk Registers** and **Emergency Plans**.
- Re-allocate resources where data shows deficiencies.
- Feed lessons learned back into the **PLAN** stage (closing the loop).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every formula, indicator, and benchmark used in Module 4 examination questions. **Memorize these for both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).**

| # | Metric / Formula | Mathematical Form | Standard Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TRIR** (Total Recordable Incident Rate) | $\text{TRIR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Recordable Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Per 200,000 man-hours | Most common lagging indicator |
| 2 | **LTIFR** (Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate) | $\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Lost-Time Injuries} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Per million man-hours | Industry benchmark |
| 3 | **LTISR** (Lost Time Injury Severity Rate) | $\text{LTISR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Days Lost} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Days per million man-hours | Severity measurement |
| 4 | **FAR** (Fatal Accident Rate) | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Fatalities} \times 10^{8}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Per 100 million man-hours | High-consequence indicator |
| 5 | **AIFR** (All Injury Frequency Rate) | $\text{AIFR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Injuries} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Per million man-hours | Comprehensive metric |
| 6 | **Safety Score (SS)** | $\text{SS} = \dfrac{\text{Hours without LTI}}{\text{Total Exposure Hours}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Behavioral tracking |
| 7 | **DART Rate** (Days Away/Restricted/Transfer) | $\text{DART} = \dfrac{\text{DART Cases} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}}$ | Per 200,000 man-hours | OSHA standard |
| 8 | **Audit Closure Ratio (ACR)** | $\text{ACR} = \dfrac{\text{Closed Non-Conformities}}{\text{Total Raised Non-Conformities}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Review effectiveness |
| 9 | **Hazard Identification Coverage (HIC)** | $\text{HIC} = \dfrac{\text{Identified Hazards}}{\text{Total Identified + Latent Hazards}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Leading indicator |
| 10 | **Severity Rate (SR)** | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Days Lost per Injury}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}} \times 10^{3}$ | Days lost per 1000 hours | Output per injury |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Always multiply the numerator by the **normalizing factor** (e.g., $10^{6}$ or $200{,}000$) and express the denominator in **man-hours**, NOT man-days. Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for unit inconsistency.

---

## 2.3 The Management Review Process — Step-by-Step

The **Management Review** is the apex audit function in safety governance. As per **ISO 45001 Clause 9.3**, it follows a strict sequence:

1. **Inputs Collection:** Audit results, KPIs, incident trends, legal updates, stakeholder feedback, resource adequacy.
2. **Pre-Review Documentation:** Safety Manager prepares a **Management Review Report (MRR)**.
3. **Review Meeting:** Convened by **Top Management** (minimum quarterly, ideally every 6 months for high-risk industries).
4. **Decision Outputs:** Policy changes, objective revisions, resource reallocation, CAPA.
5. **Communication:** Outcomes communicated to workers, contractors, and the Safety Committee.
6. **Action Tracking:** All decisions logged in a **Management Review Action Register**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application of MCR |
|---|---|
| **Construction** | Daily Permit-to-Work audits, weekly safety walks, monthly management reviews |
| **Petrochemical** | Process Safety Management (PSM) audits, MOC protocols, HIRA reviews |
| **Manufacturing** | Machine guarding inspections, lockout-tagout (LOTO) compliance audits |
| **Power Generation** | Permit audits, contractor safety reviews, electrical safety management |
| **Mining** | Statutory mine safety inspections, dangerous-occurrence reporting, MR meetings |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Problem-Solving Framework: Calculation of Safety Performance Indicators

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked examples are **exact KTU 2024 Part B (14-mark) style** numerical problems. The methodology shown is what KTU examiners expect for full marks.

---

### 📌 PROBLEM 1 — LTIFR & LTISR Calculation (Standard 7-Mark Sub-Question)

**Question:** A manufacturing plant employed **450 workers** during the year 2024. Each worker worked an average of **48 hours per week** for **50 weeks**. During the year, **6 lost-time injuries** occurred, and the total **days lost** due to these injuries was **84 days**. Calculate the **LTIFR** and **LTISR**.

#### ✅ Step 1: Calculate Total Man-Hours Worked

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Man-Hours} &= \text{No. of Workers} \times \text{Hours/Week} \times \text{No. of Weeks} \\[6pt]
&= 450 \times 48 \times 50 \\[6pt]
&= 21{,}600 \times 50 \\[6pt]
&= 1{,}080{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** KTU's standard convention uses **man-hours** as the denominator; never use man-days or man-months unless explicitly specified.

#### ✅ Step 2: Calculate LTIFR

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTIFR} &= \frac{\text{No. of Lost-Time Injuries} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{6 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}080{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{6{,}000{,}000}{1{,}080{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= 5.56 \text{ per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Step 3: Calculate LTISR

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTISR} &= \frac{\text{No. of Days Lost} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Total Man-Hours Worked}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{84 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}080{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{84{,}000{,}000}{1{,}080{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= 77.78 \text{ days per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\text{LTIFR} = 5.56$ and $\text{LTISR} = 77.78$. Industry good-practice benchmark for manufacturing is **LTIFR < 3.0**.

---

### 📌 PROBLEM 2 — TRIR Calculation with Cross-Verification (7 Marks)

**Question:** A construction site logged **12 recordable injuries** in a year. The site employed **800 workers**, each working **9 hours/day for 300 days**. Compute the **TRIR** and verify using **man-hours vs man-days** consistency.

#### ✅ Step 1: Compute Total Man-Hours

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Man-Hours} &= 800 \times 9 \times 300 \\[6pt]
&= 7{,}200 \times 300 \\[6pt]
&= 2{,}160{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Step 2: Apply TRIR Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TRIR} &= \frac{12 \times 200{,}000}{2{,}160{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2{,}400{,}000}{2{,}160{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= 1.11 \text{ per 200,000 man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A TRIR of 1.11 indicates a **better-than-average** safety record for the construction industry (US construction industry average ≈ 2.8–3.0).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Leading vs Lagging Indicators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important KTU Part A (3-mark) matrix** for this topic. Examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between leading and lagging safety indicators with examples."*

| Dimension | 🔵 **Leading Indicators** | 🔴 **Lagging Indicators** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Predictive metrics that measure proactive safety activities | Reactive metrics that measure outcomes of failures |
| **Nature** | Forward-looking, preventive | Backward-looking, after-the-fact |
| **Data Type** | Qualitative + Quantitative | Quantitative (count-based) |
| **Examples** | Safety training hours, near-miss reports, safety audits completed, toolbox talks held, hazard inspections conducted | Fatalities, lost-time injuries, TRIR, LTIFR, days lost, occupational diseases |
| **Frequency** | Daily / Weekly | Monthly / Quarterly / Annually |
| **Used By** | Operational management, supervisors | Top management, board, regulators |
| **Reaction Time** | Real-time | Delayed (post-event) |
| **Improvement Driver** | Drives **proactive** intervention | Confirms **reactive** lessons learned |
| **KTU Keyword** | "Predictive / Proactive / Activity-based" | "Outcome-based / Reactive / Performance-based" |

---

## 3.3 Management Review Decision Matrix

| Trigger Event | Review Type | Frequency | Attendees |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Major Incident (Fatality/MWI)** | Special Review | Immediate (within 48 hrs) | CEO, Plant Head, Safety Officer, Legal |
| **Statutory Audit** | Compliance Review | As scheduled | External Auditor + Top Management |
| **Routine Performance Review** | Periodic Review | Quarterly | Department Heads, Safety Committee |
| **Annual SMS Review** | Strategic Review | Annually | Board of Directors, Top Management |
| **Pre-Project Handover** | Project Review | Project-Specific | Project Manager, Safety Lead, Client |
| **Management of Change (MOC)** | Change Review | Triggered by change | Engineering, Operations, Safety |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The PDCA Safety Management Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PLAN: Safety Policy, HIRA, Objectives, KPIs] --> B[DO: Implementation, Training, SSOW, PTW, MOC]
    B --> C[CHECK: Inspections, Audits, Monitoring, Incident Investigation]
    C --> D[ACT: Management Review, CAPA, Continual Improvement]
    D --> A
    C -.Feeds Data.-> E[Management Review Meeting]
    E -.Issues CAPA.-> B
    E -.Updates Policy.-> A
```

> **Reading Guide:** The bold outer loop is the **Deming PDCA cycle**. The dotted lines represent the **Management Review feedback channel**, which is the only mechanism that converts operational data into strategic decisions.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Management Control and Review

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Management: Board, CEO, MD] --> B[Senior Management: VP Operations, Plant Head]
    B --> C[Mid Management: Department Heads, Safety Manager]
    C --> D[Supervisors: Foremen, Team Leads]
    D --> E[Workforce: Operators, Technicians, Contractors]
    E -.Reports Near-Misses, Hazards.-> D
    D -.Conducts Toolbox Talks, Inspections.-> C
    C -.Audits, KPIs, Incident Reports.-> B
    B -.Quarterly Review, Resource Allocation.-> A
    A -.Issues Policy, Strategic Direction.-> B
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is a **closed-loop hierarchical control system**. Communication flows both **downward (directives)** and **upward (performance data)**, mirroring the classic **management control cybernetic model**.

---

## 4.3 Safety Management System Architecture (ISO 45001:2018 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Context["CONTEXT OF ORGANIZATION"]
        C1[Internal and External Issues]
        C2[Needs of Workers and Other Interested Parties]
        C3[Scope of SMS]
    end
    subgraph Leadership["LEADERSHIP AND WORKER PARTICIPATION"]
        L1[Leadership Commitment]
        L2[Worker Participation]
    end
    subgraph Planning["PLANNING"]
        P1[Hazard ID and Risk Assessment]
        P2[Legal Requirements]
        P3[Safety Objectives]
    end
    subgraph Support["SUPPORT"]
        S1[Resources]
        S2[Competence]
        S3[Awareness]
        S4[Communication]
        S5[Documented Information]
    end
    subgraph Operation["OPERATION"]
        O1[Operational Planning]
        O2[Emergency Preparedness]
    end
    subgraph Eval["PERFORMANCE EVALUATION"]
        E1[Monitoring, Measurement, Analysis]
        E2[Evaluation of Compliance]
        E3[Internal Audit]
        E4[Management Review]
    end
    Context --> Leadership
    Leadership --> Planning
    Planning --> Support
    Support --> Operation
    Operation --> Eval
    Eval -.Feedback for Continual Improvement.-> Leadership
```

> **Reading Guide:** This block diagram maps **Clause 4 through Clause 10 of ISO 45001:2018**. The dashed feedback arrow from **Performance Evaluation → Leadership** is the **Management Review and Continual Improvement loop**, which is the answer to most 14-mark questions on MCR.

---

## 4.4 Management Review Input-Output Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs["INPUTS TO MANAGEMENT REVIEW"]
        I1[Audit Results]
        I2[KPI Performance Data]
        I3[Incident and Near-Miss Trends]
        I4[Legal and Regulatory Changes]
        I5[Stakeholder Feedback]
        I6[Resource Adequacy Review]
        I7[Opportunities for Improvement]
    end
    subgraph Process["MANAGEMENT REVIEW MEETING"]
        MR[Top Management Deliberation and Decision]
    end
    subgraph Outputs["OUTPUTS OF MANAGEMENT REVIEW"]
        O1[Revised Safety Objectives]
        O2[Resource Reallocation Decisions]
        O3[Corrective Actions Issued]
        O4[Policy Updates]
        O5[Action Items and Responsibilities]
    end
    Inputs --> MR
    MR --> Outputs
    Outputs -.Implemented in next PDCA cycle.-> I2
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define the term "Management Review" in the context of an Occupational Health and Safety Management System.*

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

> Management Review is a **formal, documented evaluation** conducted by **top management** at planned intervals (typically **annually**, or quarterly in high-risk industries) to assess the **continuing suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness** of the Safety Management System (SMS). It examines **audit results, KPI trends, incident data, legal compliance, and stakeholder feedback** to determine whether the safety policy and objectives remain aligned with organizational goals, and to issue **corrective actions and resource decisions** for continual improvement. As per **ISO 45001 Clause 9.3**, the outputs of management review must be documented and communicated to relevant workers.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Distinguish between "Leading" and "Lagging" safety performance indicators with two examples each.*

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

> **Leading Indicators** are **proactive, predictive metrics** that measure the **quality of safety activities and processes** before incidents occur. They indicate the **strength** of the safety system. *Examples:* (i) Number of safety inspections completed per month, (ii) Hours of safety training delivered per employee per quarter.
>
> **Lagging Indicators** are **reactive, outcome-based metrics** that measure the **consequences of safety failures** after they occur. *Examples:* (i) Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR), (ii) Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR).
>
> KTU examinations emphasize that **leading indicators drive improvement**, while **lagging indicators confirm it** — a robust SMS uses **both** in its management review dashboard.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ✍️ CHOICE A — 14 Marks

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Modified Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** as applied to a Safety Management System. Discuss the activities performed at each stage with reference to **ISO 45001:2018**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A chemical plant employs **600 workers** working **50 hours/week for 48 weeks/year**. In a year, there were **9 lost-time injuries** and **3 fatalities**, with total **days lost = 240**. Calculate the **LTIFR, FAR, and Severity Rate (SR)**. Comment on the safety performance. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks:

The **PDCA cycle** (Deming Cycle) provides the dynamic framework for managing and continually improving an organization's Safety Management System. Application to ISO 45001:2018 is as follows:

1. **PLAN — Clause 6 (Planning):** Establish safety policy, conduct **Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)**, identify legal obligations (Factories Act, OSHA, GHS), and define **SMART safety objectives** with measurable KPIs.

2. **DO — Clause 7 (Support) & Clause 8 (Operation):** Implement the plan through **resource allocation**, **competency-based training**, **worker consultation**, and **operational controls** such as Permit-to-Work (PTW), Safe Systems of Work (SSOW), and **Management of Change (MOC)** procedures.

3. **CHECK — Clause 9 (Performance Evaluation):** Monitor, measure, and analyze safety performance through **inspections, internal audits, incident investigations**, and **compliance evaluations**. Both **leading indicators** (training hours, near-miss reports) and **lagging indicators** (LTIFR, TRIR) are tracked.

4. **ACT — Clause 10 (Improvement) & Clause 9.3 (Management Review):** Top management conducts **Management Review Meetings** to decide on **corrective and preventive actions (CAPA)**, revise objectives, and reallocate resources. Outcomes are fed back into the PLAN stage, ensuring **continual improvement**.

> **Closing Statement (1 mark):** The PDCA cycle transforms the SMS from a static policy document into a **self-learning governance system** that adapts to changing hazards, regulations, and organizational realities.

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks:

**Given Data:**
- Workers = 600
- Hours/week = 50
- Weeks = 48
- Lost-time injuries = 9
- Fatalities = 3
- Total days lost = 240

**Step 1: Total Man-Hours**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Man-Hours} &= 600 \times 50 \times 48 \\[4pt]
&= 30{,}000 \times 48 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}440{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: LTIFR**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTIFR} &= \frac{9 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}440{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 6.25 \text{ per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: FAR (Fatal Accident Rate)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FAR} &= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1{,}440{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 208.33 \text{ per 100 million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Severity Rate (SR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SR} &= \frac{240}{9} = 26.67 \text{ days lost per injury (average)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comment on Safety Performance (2 marks):** An **LTIFR of 6.25** is **significantly above the industry acceptable benchmark (< 3.0)**, and an **FAR of 208.33** indicates an **unacceptably high fatality rate** (international chemical industry benchmark FAR < 5). The plant's safety performance is **POOR**, necessitating an **urgent Management Review**, root-cause investigation of fatalities, MOC protocol audit, and possible shutdown of high-risk operations pending CAPA closure.

---

### ✍️ CHOICE B — 14 Marks (Alternative Option)

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Adapted]**
> **(a)** What is **Management of Change (MOC)**? Describe its **six-stage process** and explain its role in Management Control. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **Management Review Process** as per **ISO 45001 Clause 9.3**, listing its **minimum 5 inputs and 5 outputs**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks:

**Definition (1 mark):** Management of Change (MOC) is a formal, documented process used to ensure that **any proposed organizational, technical, procedural, or personnel change** is evaluated for its **health, safety, and environmental implications BEFORE implementation**, and that appropriate controls are put in place to eliminate or mitigate newly introduced hazards.

**Six-Stage MOC Process (5 marks):**

1. **Stage 1 — Request Initiation:** Originator (engineer/supervisor) submits a formal MOC request describing the proposed change, its rationale, and affected areas.

2. **Stage 2 — Risk Screening & Technical Review:** A multi-disciplinary team (engineering, safety, operations) screens the change for HIRA implications, regulatory impact, and training requirements.

3. **Stage 3 — Risk Assessment & Control Definition:** Detailed **HIRA / HAZOP / JSA** is performed. New control measures, PPE, and procedures are specified.

4. **Stage 4 — Approval Authorization:** Designated **MOC Authority** (Plant Manager / HSE Head) reviews and formally approves the change with documented conditions.

5. **Stage 5 — Implementation with Controls:** The change is executed **only after** all pre-conditions are met — training completed, procedures updated, permits issued, communication done.

6. **Stage 6 — Post-Implementation Review & Close-Out:** After a defined period, the change is audited for unintended consequences. Lessons learned are documented and the MOC is formally **closed-out** in the register.

> **Role in Management Control (1 mark):** MOC is the **gatekeeping mechanism** that prevents uncontrolled modifications from introducing new hazards, ensuring that the **as-built/as-operated state never diverges unsafely from the assessed state**.

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks:

The **Management Review Process** per **ISO 45001 Clause 9.3** is the apex strategic review function of the SMS. The flowchart is reproduced below in textual block-form:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Schedule Review and Notify Top Management] --> B[Step 2: Collect Input Data: KPIs, Audits, Incidents, Legal Updates, Feedback]
    B --> C[Step 3: Prepare Management Review Report by Safety Manager]
    C --> D[Step 4: Convene Review Meeting Chaired by Top Management]
    D --> E[Step 5: Deliberate, Decide, and Issue Corrective Actions]
    E --> F[Step 6: Document Outputs and Communicate to Workers]
    F --> G[Step 7: Track Action Items in Action Register and Close-Loop]
    G -.Feeds Next Planning Cycle.-> B
```

**Minimum 5 Inputs (2.5 marks):**
1. Status of previous management review actions
2. OH&S performance and KPI trends (LTIFR, TRIR)
3. Audit results (internal and external)
4. Consultation and participation of workers
5. Hazards, risks, opportunities, and incidents
6. Adequacy of resources

**Minimum 5 Outputs (2.5 marks):**
1. Decisions on continual improvement opportunities
2. Changes to OH&S policy and objectives
3. Resource reallocation decisions
4. Actions to address non-conformities and risks
5. Action items with responsibilities and timelines

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (Loses 1–2 marks):** Students write man-days or man-months instead of man-hours in LTIFR/TRIR denominators. KTU strictly requires **man-hours**.
>
> 2. **Confusing PDCA with ISO Clauses (Loses 1 mark):** Many students describe PDCA but fail to **map each stage to the exact ISO 45001 Clause number** (6, 7, 8, 9, 10). Always do this for full marks.
>
> 3. **Skipping the "Comment / Interpretation" (Loses 2 marks):** After computing LTIFR/FAR, examiners **expect a qualitative verdict** (e.g., "performance is poor / below benchmark / needs urgent review"). Numerical answers alone are incomplete.
>
> 4. **Omitting the "Why" in MOC (Loses 1 mark):** Defining MOC without explaining **why it exists** (to prevent uncontrolled hazard introduction) fetches only partial credit.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Documentation Requirement (Loses 1 mark):** ISO 45001 mandates **documented information** as outputs of management review. Always mention "documented" or "recorded" outputs.
>
> 6. **Mixing Leading and Lagging Indicators (Loses 1 mark):** If asked for **two examples of leading indicators**, do not give TRIR or LTIFR — they are lagging. Use training hours, audits, near-miss reports.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Management Control and Review**

- ✅ **PDCA Cycle** = Plan (Clause 6) → Do (Clauses 7 & 8) → Check (Clause 9) → Act (Clauses 9.3 & 10). Map every answer to ISO 45001 clauses.

- ✅ **Five Pillars of MCR:** Policy, Organizing, Planning, Measurement, Review. Memorize in order.

- ✅ **Normalizing Constants:** TRIR uses **200,000**; LTIFR/LTISR/AIFR use **$10^{6}$**; FAR uses **$10^{8}$**. Denominator is always **man-hours**.

- ✅ **Leading Indicators** = Proactive (training, audits, near-misses). **Lagging Indicators** = Reactive (TRIR, LTIFR, fatalities).

- ✅ **Management Review** must be **top-management-led**, **documented**, **periodic** (quarterly/annually), and must produce **CAPA decisions and resource reallocation**.

- ✅ **MOC Six Stages:** Request → Screening → Risk Assessment → Approval → Implementation → Post-Implementation Review.

- ✅ **ISO 45001 Clause 9.3** specifies Management Review; **Clause 9.2** specifies Internal Audit — never confuse them.

- ✅ **SMART Objectives:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Used in PLAN stage.

- ✅ **HIRA** = Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment — the foundation of the PLAN stage.

- ✅ **Continual Improvement** is a **loop**, not a linear process — outcomes of ACT feed into the next PLAN.

- ✅ **Documentation is mandatory:** As per ISO 45001 Clause 7.5, all SMS processes must produce **documented information** as evidence.

- ✅ **Section 7A of Factories Act 1948** mandates **safety policy and management systems** for hazardous factories — a common KTU 3-mark question.

- ✅ **Safety Committee:** Mandatory under Section 41G of Factories Act, includes equal representation of workers and management, meets at least **once every quarter**.

- ✅ **Behavioral Safety Approach (BBS):** Focuses on **observable behaviors**, not just conditions — a leading-indicator approach gaining prominence in modern MCR.
